title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a Siamese voxel‐to‐BEV (V2B) tracker for single‐object tracking in sparse 3D LiDAR point clouds. It introduces two key components: (1) a shape‐aware feature learning network that embeds both global and local template features via a gated expansion mechanism and supervises an auxiliary shape completion branch with Chamfer loss, and (2) a voxel‐to‐BEV localization network that compresses the voxelized search features into a dense bird’s‐eye‐view map and performs anchor‐free regression of the 2D center and z coordinate. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes demonstrate state‐of‐the‐art gains over SC3D, P2B, and BAT, especially in sparse‐scene subsets, and ablation studies attest to the contribution of each module.

Strengths  
1. Technical Novelty and Clarity  
   – The integration of Siamese global‐local feature embedding, gated shape completion, and BEV‐based anchor‐free localization is a fresh, end‐to‐end framework for sparse‐point tracking.  
   – The paper is well organized and written clearly; key architectural diagrams (Figs. 2–3) aid understanding.  
   – Related work is adequately covered, situating V2B relative to both proposal‐based 3D trackers and recent anchor‐free BEV detectors.  

2. Experimental Performance  
   – V2B consistently outperforms prior methods on overall and sparse subsets of KITTI and nuScenes, with >10% relative gains in Success and Precision.  
   – Comprehensive ablation studies isolate the effects of global vs. local embedding, shape completion, voxel resolution, and detection heads, reinforcing the efficacy of the proposed design.  
   – The anchor‐free BEV regression avoids the complexity of heavy proposal schemes and demonstrates strong robustness in sparsity‐challenged scenarios.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Justification of Shape‐Aware Claims  
   – The paper claims superior handling of sparse point clouds via the shape‐aware module, yet Table 1–2 show that under sparse conditions, competing methods (SC3D, P2B) improve whereas V2B’s performance dips. A more detailed analysis or targeted ablation on the sparse subset is needed to substantiate this claim.  
   – The necessity of combining both global and local branches in the template embedding and shape‐aware modules is asserted but not fully justified. Including ablation variants that remove one branch or vary fusion strategies across all object categories (rather than only “car”) would strengthen the argument.  

2. Ablation Scope and Hyperparameter Sensitivity  
   – Current ablations focus primarily on the car category. Extending these studies to all four KITTI categories (car, pedestrian, cyclist, truck) will ensure the generality of the design choices.  
   – Hyperparameter settings (e.g., loss‐weight λ values, voxel size, BEV grid resolution) lack discussion on sensitivity. A brief analysis of how performance varies with these key parameters would improve reproducibility and guidance for practitioners.  

3. Runtime, Memory, and Practicality  
   – Inference speed, computational overhead, and memory footprint are not reported. For real‐time robotics and autonomous driving applications, latency benchmarks (e.g., ms per frame on a specified GPU) and model size are critical. We recommend including a table of runtime statistics and a discussion of training vs. inference costs.  
   – The shape completion branch requires aggregating dense ground‐truth shapes across frames during training—a process that may not scale to rare object classes or deformable targets. The authors should discuss the practicality of this requirement and potential remedies for online or zero‐shot settings.  

4. Robustness and Failure Modes  
   – The paper would benefit from experiments on challenging conditions: highly cluttered or indoor scenes, varying LiDAR resolutions, adverse weather, and extreme occlusions. Reporting qualitative failure cases (e.g., over‐sparsity, sensor noise) and potential mitigation strategies (uncertainty estimation, sensor fusion) would improve the work’s robustness claims.  
   – A brief discussion of limitations—such as reduced vertical localization precision due to BEV compression, and unsuitability in indoor environments without a meaningful BEV map—should be included.  

5. Minor Issues  
   – Some phrasing is awkward or unclear (e.g., “stable does not mean accurate” on lines 378–380); a more precise statement of limitations is advised.  
   – Typos and notation clarifications: correct “tacking”→“tracking;” add dimensionality annotations to Equations (5)–(6); justify gating mechanism design choices.  

Societal Impact  
Improved 3D tracking under sparse LiDAR enhances autonomous driving safety but also raises privacy and surveillance concerns if applied to pedestrian monitoring. The authors should acknowledge these potential misuses, discuss adherence to ethical deployment standards, and suggest safeguards against overconfidence in uncertain or adversarial conditions.

Decision: 7 (Accept)  
Overall, V2B offers a significant advancement in 3D single‐object tracking by effectively combining shape completion and BEV regression in a Siamese framework. Addressing the above concerns—particularly more thorough ablation on sparsity claims, reporting runtime, and discussing limitations—will further solidify the paper’s contributions and impact.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents V2B, a 3D single-object tracking framework that combines a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network with a voxel-to-BEV (bird’s-eye-view) localization module. In training, the shape-aware network embeds both global and local template features into the search area and produces a dense point cloud completion via a Chamfer-distance-supervised branch. At inference, the completed search point clouds are voxelized and collapsed into a BEV feature map, on which an anchor-free regression head predicts the object’s 2D BEV center and z-coordinate. Evaluated on KITTI and nuScenes, V2B consistently outperforms prior methods (SC3D, P2B, BAT), especially in sparse LiDAR scenarios, while also demonstrating reduced computational cost.

Strengths:  
• Significant performance gains: V2B achieves substantial improvements in Success and Precision metrics, including on sparse‐scene subsets, indicating strong practical impact for autonomous driving.  
• Lightweight anchor-free BEV localization: Compressing 3D voxels into a 2D BEV representation yields a simpler and faster detection head compared to proposal‐based trackers.  
• Comprehensive ablations: The authors validate the contributions of global vs. local embedding branches, the completion module, and voxel size.  
• Computational cost analysis: The paper reports decreased runtime and resource requirements relative to baselines, addressing real-time deployment concerns.  
• Reproducibility: Code release (as noted in the supplementary) further supports future research.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. Writing Quality and Clarity  
   – The manuscript contains numerous grammatical errors and awkward sentences. A thorough proofreading is needed to improve readability.  
   – Sections 3.1–3.2 are dense; the mathematical notation for template embedding and gated feature expansion could be made more concise and accompanied by explicit dimensionality annotations (as suggested in Alternative Review 3).  

2. Novelty and Related Work  
   – While the integration of Siamese shape completion with voxel-to-BEV regression is largely original, the localization module resembles existing 3D detection encoders (e.g., PointPillar, SECOND). The authors should cite and contrast these works to clarify their novel contributions.  
   – Recent anchor-free 3D detectors (e.g., CenterPoint-style approaches) and transformer-based trackers are not discussed; adding this context would strengthen the related work section (Alternative Reviews 1 & 3).

3. Ablation Depth on Feature Embedding  
   – The Template Feature Embedding Network is complex, combining global MLP/max pooling, local cosine similarity, and gated feature expansion. Yet the paper lacks fine-grained ablations:  
     • Compare different correlation/distance functions for the similarity map (e.g., dot product vs. cosine).  
     • Isolate the impact of the global vs. local branch beyond the binary ablation—perhaps varying their relative weights.  
     • Examine gate mechanism designs or activation functions, as raised in Alternative Review 1.

4. Runtime, Memory, and Hyperparameters  
   – Inference speed, hardware setup, and memory footprint are not fully reported. Given the emphasis on computational efficiency, latency benchmarks and GPU/CPU details are necessary (Alternative Reviews 1 & 2).  
   – Hyperparameters such as loss weights (λ), voxel size, and BEV grid resolution are stated but not justified; a brief sensitivity analysis would improve transparency.

5. Limitations and Failure Modes  
   – The requirement for dense aggregated ground-truth shapes during training may not generalize to rare or deformable object classes (Alternative Reviews 3 & 4).  
   – The voxel-to-BEV projection inherently sacrifices vertical resolution for density; its performance in highly cluttered indoor scenes or non-driving scenarios remains untested (Alternative Review 4).  
   – More qualitative failure case analysis (e.g., occlusions, reflective surfaces, extreme sparsity, or sensor noise) should be included beyond the brief supplementary figures (Alternative Reviews 1 & 2).

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The paper currently offers only a cursory discussion of limitations and societal impact. To address this:  
• Safety and Robustness: Mis-tracking in adverse weather or under heavy occlusion can jeopardize autonomous vehicle safety. Discuss possible mitigation strategies such as uncertainty estimation or sensor fusion.  
• Privacy and Misuse: Improved LiDAR-based tracking could be repurposed for pedestrian surveillance without consent. The authors should clarify intended use cases and acknowledge potential ethical concerns.  
• Environmental and Deployment Costs: The shape completion network adds training overhead; its energy consumption and feasibility for edge deployment merit discussion.

Overall Assessment:  
V2B makes a meaningful stride in 3D single-object tracking under sparse LiDAR by combining shape completion with an efficient BEV regression head. Its strong empirical gains and computational efficiency are commendable. However, the paper’s presentation quality, depth of ablations, discussion of related works, and treatment of limitations require substantial improvement. After addressing these concerns, the work will be a valuable contribution to the community.

Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV 3D object tracker tailored for sparse LiDAR point clouds in autonomous driving scenarios. The proposed pipeline fuses a shape-aware Siamese feature learning module—which embeds both global and local template features and employs a Chamfer-distance supervised completion network—with an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV localization head that regresses the 2D center and z-coordinate of the target. Evaluated on the KITTI benchmark, the method achieves substantial gains over prior approaches such as SC3D, P2B, and BAT, particularly under severe sparsity. The modular design, clear visualizations, and promise of code release commend this work for the community.

Strengths:
- Tackles a challenging real-world task: The fusion of shape completion and BEV projection in an end-to-end Siamese framework is an original and sensible approach to overcoming LiDAR sparsity.
- Empirical gains: On KITTI, the tracker surpasses existing single-object methods by a significant margin in both Success and Precision metrics. Ablation studies further attribute performance boosts to the global-local embedding branches, gating mechanism, and BEV regression design.
- Clear presentation and reproducibility: The paper is generally well organized, with informative figures illustrating the two-module architecture. The promised release of code, along with detailed hyperparameter settings (e.g., loss weights, voxel sizes), will aid future research.
- Practical insights: Visual examples in the main text and supplement help convey design intuitions. The anchor-free BEV localization avoids costly proposal generation, offering a lightweight alternative.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Performance on underrepresented categories:
   - The noticeably lower tracking accuracy for cyclists is attributed to insufficient training data. It remains unclear whether similar trends would hold if other categories (e.g., pedestrians) were equally underrepresented. A more detailed analysis of category-wise data distribution and its impact would strengthen the claims.
2. Limited dataset diversity:
   - Evaluation is confined to KITTI. To support generalization claims, the authors should consider additional benchmarks such as nuScenes or Waymo, or at least discuss anticipated behavior on these datasets and guidance for parameter tuning under new LiDAR configurations.
3. Lack of qualitative video demonstrations:
   - While supplementary visualizations are helpful, short video clips illustrating the tracker’s outputs over time would provide more intuitive evidence of temporal consistency and robustness.
4. Hyperparameter tuning and implementation details:
   - Several pipeline components rely on manually tuned coefficients (e.g., loss weight λ, voxel size). Providing heuristics or a brief ablation on hyperparameter sensitivity would offer practical guidance for adapting the method to new scenarios.
5. Runtime, memory, and hardware:
   - The manuscript omits inference speed, computational overhead, and memory usage—critical factors for real-time autonomous driving. Including latency benchmarks (e.g., ms per frame on a specified GPU) and peak memory consumption would clarify deployment feasibility.
6. Failure modes and robustness:
   - A discussion of typical failure cases (e.g., heavy occlusion, extreme sparsity, reflective or transparent objects) is missing. Empirical insights into when and why the tracker fails would inform both future improvements and safe system integration.
7. Diagram and presentation minor issues:
   - In Figure 3, the color scheme lacks semantic mapping, which may confuse readers. A legend or more consistent coloring would improve readability. Additionally, minor typos (e.g., “tacking” → “tracking”) should be corrected.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
- Extension to multimodal input: For safety-critical autonomous systems, combining RGB and LiDAR may further enhance robustness. A brief discussion on integrating camera data or sensor fusion strategies would be valuable.
- Training cost of completion module: While the dense shape completion network is discarded at inference, its training overhead and memory footprint may be prohibitive for large-scale or online learning settings.
- Privacy and misuse concerns: Enhanced 3D tracking capabilities can benefit vehicle safety but also raise privacy issues if applied to pedestrian surveillance. The authors should acknowledge potential misuse and clarify ethical usage boundaries.
- Safety hazards under failure: Mis-tracking in adverse conditions (e.g., rain, snow, sensor noise) could lead to unsafe maneuvers. The paper would benefit from proposing mitigation strategies, such as uncertainty estimation or fallback behaviors.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
Overall, this work delivers a well-motivated and performant 3D single-object tracker. Addressing the above concerns—particularly regarding deployment details, generalization, and failure analysis—will further solidify its impact and practical utility.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper addresses single-object tracking in sparse 3D LiDAR point clouds by proposing V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (bird’s-eye-view) tracker. The method consists of (1) a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network that embeds both global and local template features into the search area while supervising a dense shape-completion module via Chamfer distance, and (2) an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV localization network that compresses features into a 2D BEV map and directly regresses the object’s center and height. Evaluated on KITTI (and briefly on nuScenes in an earlier draft), V2B shows substantial gains over prior methods (P2B, SC3D, BAT), especially in very sparse scenarios.

Strengths:
1. Novel integration: Building on P2B’s two-step Siamese framework and SC3D’s scene-completion regularization, the authors propose a fresh combination of shape completion and dense BEV regression. Unlike proposal-based trackers, the anchor-free BEV head directly localizes targets from dense voxel grids, which consistently improves success and precision on sparse subsets (as highlighted in Alternative Reviews 1 and 2).
2. Empirical gains: Experiments report significant improvements—often >10% Success on cars—over state-of-the-art baselines. Ablation studies (e.g., global vs. local embedding, shape module, voxel resolution) convincingly attribute gains to each component, demonstrating the technical soundness of the design.
3. Practical impact: By tackling LiDAR sparsity head-on, V2B has clear applicability for autonomous driving. The anchor-free, lightweight BEV head offers a promising alternative to heavy proposal pipelines, and the method’s code release would benefit reproducibility and follow-up research.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:

1. Comparison with Prior Work:
   • The core contributions are closely related to P2B [12] and SC3D [13], yet the paper lacks a systematic breakdown of what is inherited versus what is novel. A table or paragraph explicitly contrasting the dense detection head with P2B’s proposal scheme, and the specific modifications to SC3D’s regularization loss (e.g., feature inputs, gating mechanism), would clarify the incremental technical novelty.
   • Beyond tables, qualitative visualizations comparing V2B, SC3D, and P2B—especially on challenging categories like cyclist where performance presently lags—would strengthen the paper (as also noted in the Main Review).

2. Method Presentation:
   • Sections 3.1 and 3.2 are dense with equations but light on motivation. Rewriting these to first outline the high-level rationale (e.g., why two embedding branches, how gate suppresses background) before diving into math would improve readability.
   • Some implementation details are missing: the choice of voxel size, BEV grid resolution, and loss-weight hyperparameters (λ values) are stated without justification. A brief discussion of their selection or sensitivity would aid reproducibility (a point raised in Alternative Reviews 2 and 3).

3. Additional Experiments:
   • While the KITTI results are strong, evaluation on nuScenes (as mentioned in Alternative Reviews 1 and 2) would demonstrate generality across datasets with different LiDAR densities.
   • Report inference speed and memory usage explicitly. The current Table 3 shows 17 FPS on a TITAN RTX versus P2B’s published 40 FPS on a 1080Ti; explaining this discrepancy and providing latency benchmarks is important for real‐time applications.
   • Include failure-case analyses: how does V2B perform under severe occlusion, rotation, or very sparse scans? Visual examples or quantitative metrics on a “hard” subset would reveal robustness and guide future improvements.

4. Limitations and Societal Impact:
   • Training relies on aggregating dense ground-truth shapes across frames, which may not generalize to rare or deformable object classes. Discuss the feasibility of obtaining such supervision in other domains or online settings.
   • The shape-completion module is discarded at inference, but its training cost and memory footprint are unreported. A brief note on training overhead would be useful.
   • From a broader perspective, improved 3D tracking aids vehicle safety but raises privacy and surveillance concerns when applied beyond vehicular contexts. The authors should include a short discussion of potential misuse, failure modes in adverse weather or sensor noise, and recommended mitigation strategies (uncertainty estimation, sensor fusion).

Minor Comments:
– Correct typos such as “tacking” → “tracking.”  
– In Equations (5)–(6), explicitly annotate tensor dimensions for clarity.  
– Clarify the difference between global MLP+max pooling and local cosine-similarity embedding in the text.

Overall Assessment:
V2B presents a technically sound and well-motivated advancement for 3D single-object tracking under LiDAR sparsity, yielding strong empirical improvements. However, key areas—clarifying novelty over P2B and SC3D, enriching qualitative comparisons, reporting runtime details, and discussing limitations—require attention. I recommend a marginal accept (Rating: 6), contingent on addressing these points for greater clarity, rigor, and practical insight.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single-object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The method integrates (1) a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network—using both global and local template embeddings along with a gate mechanism and Chamfer-distance supervision to complete dense 3D shapes—and (2) an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV target localization network that compresses the search volume into a bird’s-eye-view representation for 2D center and height regression. Evaluated on KITTI and nuScenes, V2B demonstrates consistent improvements over baseline trackers (SC3D, P2B, BAT), particularly in sparse-scene subsets.

Strengths:
- The end-to-end integration of template matching, shape completion, and BEV regression addresses LiDAR sparsity in a unified framework. Although each component has precedents (e.g., SiamRPN for matching, LaserNet for BEV localization), their joint training within a Siamese architecture is a valuable contribution.
- Extensive quantitative evaluation shows significant gains in Success and Precision metrics, with ablation studies attributing improvements to the global/local feature branches, the completion module, and the anchor-free detection head.
- The anchor-free BEV localization offers a lightweight alternative to proposal-heavy pipelines, reducing complexity at inference.
- Clear organization and informative figures facilitate understanding of the two-module design. The authors’ plan to release code will aid reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
- Novelty is somewhat incremental, as the approach essentially ensembles established modules. A clearer articulation of how this specific combination yields emergent benefits—beyond the sum of parts—would strengthen the contribution.
- Key implementation details are missing: inference speed, hardware and memory requirements, and runtime benchmarks. Real-time performance is critical for autonomous driving applications and should be reported.
- Hyperparameter choices (loss weights, voxel size, BEV grid resolution) lack justification or sensitivity analysis. Readers would benefit from guidance on how these settings affect performance.
- While ablations cover most modules, a focused study separating the contributions of global versus local template embeddings (as highlighted in the main review) is still needed to validate design choices.
- Evaluation is limited to urban driving datasets. The tracker’s robustness to highly cluttered scenes, indoor environments, adverse weather, varying LiDAR resolutions, and rare object classes remains untested.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Provide detailed runtime and resource benchmarks (e.g., latency per frame, GPU memory consumption) to assess real-time applicability.
2. Include an ablation that isolates global and local template feature branches, demonstrating their individual and combined effects.
3. Justify and, if possible, analyze sensitivity of key hyperparameters such as voxel size, BEV resolution, and loss weighting factors.
4. Discuss training overhead and memory footprint of the shape completion module, and clarify how dense GT aggregation is obtained—especially for rare or deformable classes.
5. Expand evaluation to additional scenarios (e.g., cluttered indoor scenes or adverse weather conditions) or at least include qualitative failure cases (e.g., extreme sparsity, heavy occlusion, rotating objects).
6. Enhance the discussion of related anchor-free 3D detectors and recent transformer-based tracking methods to position the work more firmly within the broader landscape.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The method relies on aggregating dense ground-truth point clouds across frames during training, which may not generalize to objects with few scans or to novel unseen classes. At inference, vertical resolution is traded off for BEV density, potentially impacting performance in cluttered vertical scenes. From a practical standpoint, deployment in safety-critical autonomous systems demands transparent reporting of failure modes—such as under heavy rain or sensor noise—and mitigation strategies (e.g., uncertainty estimation or multi-sensor fusion). Moreover, while improved 3D tracking can enhance vehicle safety, it also poses privacy concerns if applied to pedestrian surveillance. A brief discussion of intended applications, ethical considerations, and privacy safeguards would be beneficial.

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper presents a coherent and effective framework with promising results, but its incremental novelty and gaps in evaluation and implementation details warrant further work before publication.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:

Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of 3D single‐object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds by integrating a Siamese shape‐aware feature learning network with an anchor‐free voxel‐to‐bird’s‐eye‐view (BEV) localization module. The core idea is to enrich sparse search‐area features via shape completion—leveraging both global and local template embeddings—and then compress the completed voxel features along the vertical axis to regress 2D BEV centers and z‐coordinates. Experiments on the KITTI tracking benchmark demonstrate performance gains over established baselines, and ablation studies confirm the contributions of each component.

Strengths  
1. Novel Integration of Modules: The paper presents a well‐motivated combination of Siamese shape completion and voxel‐to‐BEV localization. As noted in Alternative Reviews, while shape completion and BEV projections have been explored separately, their end-to-end fusion for sparse‐scene 3D tracking is original and addresses a key limitation of prior work.  
2. Intuitive Feature Design: The local and global embedding branches—augmented by gated feature expansion—are conceptually clear. The ablation in Table 4 shows that both branches and the completion module substantially improve performance, validating the engineering effort.  
3. Qualitative and Quantitative Validation: The authors provide qualitative visualizations on sparse point clouds that clearly illustrate the method’s robustness under severe sparsity. Quantitatively, the proposed tracker outperforms SC3D, P2B, and BAT on Cars and other categories, and ablations isolate the impact of voxel size, template embedding, and BEV regression.  
4. Anchor-Free Regression: Compressing 3D features into a dense BEV map and using an anchor-free head for 2D and z-axis center detection is a lightweight alternative to proposal-based pipelines. This design choice is timely given the trend toward center-based 3D detection.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Shape Completion Comparison: The main manuscript claims improved performance by completing shapes in the search area rather than on the template (as in [13]), but no direct comparison is provided. To clarify the benefit, the authors should integrate template‐based completion in their framework or adapt [13]’s code to apply search‐area completion, ensuring a controlled ablation.  
2. Voxel-to-BEV Justification: It remains unclear why max-pooling along the z‐axis aids z-center regression, given that max‐pooling can discard vertical details. An analysis comparing center‐estimation errors with and without the BEV conversion—or alternative pooling schemes—would strengthen the claim that voxel-to-BEV benefits both 2D and depth localization.  
3. Network Backbone Choice: The current design relies heavily on PointNet++ despite its age. The authors should justify this choice or experiment with more recent point‐cloud encoders (e.g., sparse convolutional networks or transformer-based modules) to evaluate whether stronger backbones yield further gains.  
4. Limited Evaluation Protocol: Following prior work, the tracker is evaluated on only two KITTI sequences. Given the small sample size and high variance for categories like Cyclist, the authors should extend experiments to a larger dataset (e.g., nuScenes or Waymo) to demonstrate robustness across diverse scenes and sensor configurations.  
5. Computational Overhead and Reproducibility: The paper omits critical details such as inference speed, memory footprint, and hardware settings. As highlighted in Alternative Reviews, real-time feasibility is essential for autonomous driving applications. Reporting latency benchmarks and hyperparameter sensitivities (e.g., choice of voxel size, loss weights) would greatly aid reproducibility.  
6. Missing Failure-Mode Analysis: Beyond average metrics, an examination of cases where the tracker fails—such as extremely sparse scans, heavy occlusion, or reflective surfaces—would provide valuable insight into the method’s limitations and guide future improvements.

Limitations and Societal Impact  
While the paper briefly mentions limitations, it lacks discussion of real‐world deployment challenges: robustness to sensor noise, adverse weather, and dynamic multi‐object interactions. From a societal perspective, enhanced 3D tracking can improve autonomous vehicle safety but also raises privacy concerns if applied to pedestrian surveillance. Acknowledging these trade-offs and outlining mitigation strategies (e.g., uncertainty estimation, sensor fusion) would demonstrate responsible research practice.

Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The proposed approach offers a thoughtful integration of shape completion and BEV anchor‐free localization with solid empirical gains, but key experiments and analyses—particularly fair comparisons, broader evaluation, and computational profiling—are needed to fully validate its practical utility.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.full_review_text:

This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).

3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper introduces a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The framework comprises (1) a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network that embeds global and local template features into the search area and completes a dense point‐cloud via a Chamfer-distance supervision, and (2) an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV localization network that compresses the 3D features into a bird’s‐eye‐view map and directly regresses the 2D center and z-coordinate. On the KITTI tracking dataset, the method reports state‐of‐the‐art performance in terms of its chosen metrics.

Strengths:  
• Original integration of modules: Combining Siamese shape completion with anchor-free BEV regression is a novel design that directly addresses sparsity—a point noted by several reviewers. The two‐branch embedding (global MLP + local cosine similarity) and gated feature expansion are well motivated and supported by the ablation studies.  
• Clear presentation: The manuscript is generally well organized, with helpful diagrams illustrating the dual-module architecture. Sections detailing the network components and loss functions are technically sound and, as Alternative Reviewers have pointed out, largely follow best practices (e.g. Chamfer loss for shape, Focal loss for center detection).  
• Ablation studies: The authors systematically isolate the impact of template embedding branches, dense shape completion, voxel size, and BEV conversion, demonstrating consistent performance gains. This experimental rigor is a positive.

Weaknesses:  
1. Tracking versus detection scope:  
   Despite the “tracker” label, there is no discussion of core tracking elements such as track initiation, termination, occlusion handling, or identity maintenance over time. As noted in the main review, this framework reads more like a 3D single‐object detector than a true multi-frame tracking system. Clarifying whether this is intended for single‐object or multi‐object tracking—and extending the framework accordingly—is essential.  

2. Limited benchmarking and metrics:  
   The evaluation is restricted to the KITTI training split and a custom metric rather than the standard KITTI tracking benchmarks (e.g. MOTA, HOTA). This choice undermines comparability with prior work. As suggested by several alternative reviews, the method should be evaluated on the KITTI test set and additional datasets such as nuScenes, Waymo, and JRDB, using established tracking metrics (Success, Precision, MOTA/HOTA) and sparse‐scene subsets to validate practical gains.  

3. Related work coverage:  
   The related‐works section omits several prominent 2D and 3D multi-object tracking methods and datasets. Key references include MOTChallenge, JRMOT, EagerMOT, and the 3D MOT baselines on KITTI/NuScenes (e.g. Weng et al. IROS 2020, Shenoi et al. IROS 2020, Kim et al. ICRA 2021). A broader comparison—both in discussion and in experiments—would strengthen the claim of novelty and empirical positioning.  

4. Runtime, resource footprint, and hyperparameter justification:  
   No inference‐time benchmarks, memory usage, or computational overhead are reported, yet these are critical for real‐time autonomous driving applications. Alternative reviews rightly request latency measurements, GPU requirements, and failure‐case analysis (e.g. under sensor noise or extreme sparsity). Moreover, the loss‐weight hyperparameters (λ values) and voxel/BEV grid sizes lack intuitive justification or sensitivity analysis.  

5. Generalization and robustness:  
   The current study focuses exclusively on rigid object categories (cars) in urban driving scenes. There is no exploration of non‐rigid targets (e.g. pedestrians), adverse weather conditions, or indoor environments. Training via dense ground‐truth shape aggregation may not generalize to rare or deformable classes. A thorough assessment of failure modes—such as heavy occlusion, rotating objects, or different sensor configurations—would be necessary to claim broad applicability.  

6. Societal impact and limitations:  
   The paper does not discuss ethical or privacy considerations. As highlighted by alternative reviews, improved 3D tracking has clear benefits for safety but also raises concerns about surveillance misuse and data privacy. Furthermore, the lack of disclosed limitations—regarding safety hazards in mis-tracking or overconfidence in uncertain scenarios—should be remedied.

Decision:  
Rating 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection.  
While the proposed Siamese V2B architecture offers an interesting fusion of shape completion and anchor-free BEV regression, significant gaps in scope (true tracking functionality), evaluation rigor, related-work coverage, and practical deployment considerations prevent me from recommending acceptance in its current form.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound.
