Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

reviews.text:
Hybrid Review

Summary  
This paper delivers a theoretical investigation of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two distinct supervised, strongly convex settings. First, for “recurring” test tasks drawn from the training set, the authors introduce a novel (γ,K)-uniform stability notion and derive an excess population loss bound of order O(1/(m n)), where m is the number of tasks and n the samples per task. Second, for “unseen” test tasks, they bound the excess loss in terms of the total variation distance between the new task distribution and the training-task mixture. Both results rest on clear convexity, smoothness, and boundedness assumptions and leverage one-step SGD adaptation.

Originality & Significance  
Building on prior O(1/m) meta-learning bounds, the paper’s stability definition is a meaningful refinement: it explicitly captures per-task sample dependence and yields the first O(1/(m n)) guarantee for convex MAML. The treatment of novel tasks via total variation coupling is also original, linking distributional divergence to transfer performance. These insights sharpen our theoretical understanding in the convex regime and could inform future nonconvex or algorithm‐design extensions.

Quality & Technical Soundness  
The proofs are rigorous and align with established SGD convergence and stability arguments. Key lemmas and propositions are well supported in detailed appendices. In particular, the coupling argument that avoids explicit K-dependence in the unseen‐task bound is insightful. However, several aspects merit improvement:

• Assumptions vs. Practice. The strong convexity and single‐step adaptation assumptions limit the immediate applicability to deep‐network settings where MAML is most popular. While Remark 2 acknowledges the convexity caveat, a more thorough discussion of the challenges in extending to nonconvex objectives—and of how realistic hyperparameters (e.g., larger SGD steps K) might affect stability—is needed.

• Empirical Illustration. The paper would be strengthened by synthetic or small‐scale experiments demonstrating bound tightness and clarifying how the TV-distance term behaves in practice. At present, the theoretical rates stand unvalidated, leaving readers uncertain about their real‐world impact.

• Presentation & Clarity. The manuscript is generally well structured, yet the dense notation and lengthy definitions can impede readability. A roadmap figure or a summary table of main bounds, assumptions, and their dependencies would guide the reader. Likewise, an illustrative example—perhaps visualizing the coupling construction in Section 3.3—would demystify the unseen‐task analysis. Minor encoding glitches (misplaced characters, inconsistent Greek symbols) should also be addressed.

Broader Impact & Ethical Considerations  
Although this is a theoretical work, the unseen‐task analysis presumes knowledge of the total variation distance to new distributions—information often unavailable in practice. A brief discussion on how one might estimate or bound this divergence in realistic scenarios would enhance transparency. Furthermore, since meta-learning can amplify biases across tasks, a short statement on potential fairness implications or on responsibly deploying MAML in sensitive domains (e.g., healthcare) would round out the broader impact narrative.

Minor Corrections  
• Assumption 1(ii): replace “unbounded” with “bounded.”  
• Line 196: “from [1]” → “from [24].”  
• Line 218: “deferentially private” → “differentially private.”  
• Lines 286–287: use “K” instead of “k.”  
• Section 3.3 title: lowercase “Task.”

Decision & Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This work advances convex‐regime meta-learning theory with tighter stability-based bounds and a novel distributional analysis for unseen tasks. Despite its limitations—chiefly the convexity assumption, lack of empirical validation, and heavy notation—the paper makes a solid theoretical contribution. With the suggested clarifications and contextual discussions, it should interest the meta-learning community.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper provides a theoretical analysis of the generalization behavior of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) under strongly convex, smooth loss functions. Focusing on two scenarios—recurring test tasks drawn from the training set and novel unseen tasks—the authors establish an O(1/(m n)) excess risk bound for the former by introducing a novel (γ, K)-uniform stability notion, and a total-variation-based bound for the latter that scales with the distributional distance between training and test tasks. The technical development leverages algorithmic stability arguments, standard SGD convergence results, and a coupling argument to handle task heterogeneity.

Strengths  
• Novel Stability Definition and Tight Bound: Building on and sharpening prior O(1/m) rates, the paper’s (γ, K)-uniform stability captures both the number of tasks (m) and per-task sample size (n), leading to the first O(1/(m n)) generalization bound for convex MAML (alternatives 1&2).  
• Unseen-Task Analysis via Total Variation: The coupling-based argument framing generalization in terms of total variation distance is an elegant and original approach that directly links distributional divergence to excess risk (alternatives 1&2).  
• Rigorous and Detailed Proofs: Under clear assumptions (strong convexity, smoothness, bounded gradients and Hessians), the paper presents logically structured proofs with comprehensive appendices. The separation of training and generalization errors is well motivated and carefully executed (alternative 3).  
• End-to-End Excess Risk Guarantees: By combining stability-based generalization bounds with standard SGD convergence for both last and averaged iterates, the authors furnish complete excess risk guarantees, which can inform both theory and algorithm design in convex meta-learning (alternative 2).

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Convexity and Extension to Non-Convex Settings  
   While strong convexity and smoothness streamline the stability analysis, they limit applicability to deep non-convex models commonly used in meta-learning. The authors acknowledge this in their response, but a more detailed discussion—perhaps comparing to algorithmic stability analyses in non-convex settings (Hardt et al., 2016; London, 2017; Zhang et al., 2020)—would guide readers on the challenges and potential pathways to relax convexity assumptions (main review point 1, alternative 3). Including a concrete example illustrating why the current stability notion struggles in non-convex landscapes would be especially informative.

2. Connection to Bilevel Meta-Learning  
   Many practical MAML implementations adopt a bilevel optimization structure. It would strengthen the paper to discuss how the current bounds might extend to the bilevel case, referencing recent stability analyses in that setting (e.g., Ji et al., 2020; Arxiv:2106.04188) and highlighting any new technical obstacles (main review point 2).

3. Clarity and Readability  
   • Notation Density: The abundance of symbols and dense definitions can challenge readers. A concise road map at the start of Section 3 and a summary table listing main assumptions and resulting bounds would greatly improve navigability (alternatives 1, 3, 4).  
   • Illustrative Examples: Incorporating a simple example or schematic to illustrate the coupling argument in the unseen-task analysis would demystify Section 3.3 for a broader audience (alternatives 1, 2).  
   • Large-K Regime: The large-K analysis is deferred to the appendix; a brief synopsis of these results in the main text—along with discussion of how the bounds scale with K—would provide a more complete picture (alternatives 1, 2).

4. Lack of Empirical Validation  
   Although the focus is theoretical, simple synthetic experiments could illustrate the tightness of the O(1/(m n)) rate and the impact of task heterogeneity on unseen-task performance. Even small-scale simulations would help readers appreciate the practical relevance of the bounds (alternative 3).

5. Societal Impact and Responsible Reporting  
   As meta-learning methods are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, a brief discussion of potential biases—especially when tasks differ in distribution—would underscore responsible use. Addressing how distributional shifts might amplify spurious correlations or unfair outcomes would strengthen the broader impact section (alternatives 2, 3, 4).

Decision  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by refining stability-based generalization bounds for convex MAML and framing unseen-task performance in precise distributional terms. With the suggested clarifications—particularly on extending to non-convex and bilevel settings, improving readability, and contextualizing societal impacts—the manuscript will be of clear interest to the meta-learning theory community.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:

In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. 3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error.

1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper offers a theoretical investigation of the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithm under strongly convex and smooth loss functions. Building on existing uniform‐stability analyses, the authors first introduce a (γ,K)-uniform stability notion tailored to the two–stage adaptation in MAML and prove an O(1/(m n)) excess generalization bound when test tasks recur among those seen in training. They then extend their analysis to novel, unseen tasks by bounding the excess risk in terms of the total variation (TV) distance between the new task’s distribution and the training distributions—using a coupling argument to eliminate explicit dependence on the adaptation batch size K. A toy experiment in the supplement illustrates how the sample counts m (tasks) and n (per-task samples) affect generalization.

Strengths:
• The paper is generally well written, with clear definitions, assumptions, and proof sketches.  Notational conventions and the roadmap for main results are systematically presented.  
• By capturing the dependence on both the number of tasks m and per-task sample size n, the O(1/(m n)) bound refines earlier O(1/m) results and highlights the role of intra-task sample counts—a significant step for few-shot theory.  
• The novel (γ,K)-uniform stability notion bridges algorithmic stability and meta-learning structure, and the unseen-task TV‐distance bound provides an interpretable measure of distributional divergence affecting transfer.  
• Technical arguments leverage well‐known convex optimization and SGD‐convergence tools, and the appendices contain detailed derivations that underpin the main theorems.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited practical novelty.  Although the convex‐MAML setting is a useful theoretical sandbox, MAML’s widespread use in nonconvex deep networks is not addressed.  The assumptions of strong convexity and smoothness significantly restrict immediate applicability.  
2. Tightness and insight of bounds.  Beyond the 1/(m n) rate, it remains unclear whether constants or the dependence on K (in large‐K regimes) are optimal.  The results largely mirror standard supervised‐learning rates and do not yet reveal deeper implications of MAML’s composite training-adaptation structure.  
3. Insufficient empirical validation.  
   – The toy experiment only explores m and n; it omits the role of adaptation batch size K and does not validate the TV‐distance bound in practice.  
   – Results show large variance but rely on a single seed; averaging over multiple runs would bolster confidence.  
   – No experiments are conducted on supervised-classification benchmarks or on synthetic tasks designed to test distributional divergence metrics.  
4. Clarity and presentation.  The notation, while consistent, is dense; key definitions (e.g., stability measures) could be summarized in a table.  Minor formatting issues (misplaced footnotes, encoding glitches) occasionally impede readability.  A concise summary of large‐K findings in the main text and illustrative examples for the coupling argument would help guide readers.  
5. Limited discussion of broader impact.  As meta-learning methods are deployed in sensitive domains, potential biases arising from task distribution mismatches warrant brief treatment, yet the manuscript lacks such commentary.

Suggestions for Improvement:
• Empirically validate key theoretical insights: run additional seeds, include experiments that vary K, and test classification tasks (e.g., few-shot image benchmarks).  In particular, examine whether TV distance correlates with empirical generalization gaps and whether the derived bound is tight in controlled settings.  
• Clarify the novelty relative to prior stability analyses (e.g., Bousquet & Elisseeff, Fallah et al.) by pinpointing technical challenges unique to MAML’s nested update.  
• Introduce a summary table of main assumptions and resulting bounds; streamline the introduction by relocating extended related-work discussion to a dedicated section.  Provide a short illustrative example for the coupling argument in Section 3.3 and summarize large-K refinements from the appendix in the main text.  
• Discuss limitations more explicitly: outline prospects for extending to nonconvex or overparameterized regimes, and comment on practical estimation of TV distance for unseen tasks.  Include a brief note on ethical considerations or bias amplification when transferring across heterogeneous tasks.

Decision:
While the paper makes a valid theoretical contribution by tightening stability-based generalization bounds for convex MAML and framing transfer in terms of distributional divergence, its practical impact is limited by strong convexity assumptions and a lack of empirical verification.  I therefore maintain a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold, with a weak-reject recommendation.  

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold (Weak Reject)

In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.
reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Summary:
The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in the supervised, strongly convex regime. It tackles two test‐time scenarios: (i) recurring tasks drawn from those seen during training, for which the authors introduce a novel (γ,K)-uniform stability definition and prove an O(1/(m n)) excess risk bound (m = number of tasks, n = samples per task), and (ii) unseen tasks sampled from the same distribution family, with a bound on excess loss that scales with the total variation distance between training and test task distributions. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML under disjoint train/test tasks, refining prior O(1/m) rates by capturing per‐task sample dependence and formalizing the intuition that transfer degrades with distributional divergence.

Strengths:
1. Novel Stability Definition and Sharp Bounds: The (γ,K)-uniform stability notion is tailored to MAML’s two‐stage adaptation and leads to the first O(1/(m n)) generalization bound in the convex meta‐learning literature. This refinement over earlier O(1/m) analyses underscores the impact of per‐task sample size, which is central to few‐shot learning theory.
2. Unseen‐Task Analysis via Total Variation: The coupling argument linking excess risk to total variation distance offers a clean, interpretable measure of task heterogeneity and its effect on transfer. Avoiding explicit dependence on the adaptation batch size K in this bound is especially appealing.
3. Rigorous and Detailed Proofs: Under standard assumptions (strong convexity, smoothness, bounded gradients/Hessian Lipschitz), the paper provides precise statements and full derivations in the appendices. The adaptation of SGD convergence guarantees to the meta‐learning setting is carefully executed.
4. Clear Exposition of Theoretical Contributions: The main theorems are accompanied by discussions of their practical implications. The writing is generally lucid, with background on MAML and algorithmic stability presented thoroughly.

Areas for Improvement:
1. Convexity and Learning‐Rate Assumptions: As noted in similar meta‐learning analyses, the reliance on strong convexity limits applicability to deep neural networks. In practice, the fast‐adaptation learning rate (α) often violates the small‐step requirements needed for stability. Relaxing or at least discussing weaker assumptions on α would enhance practical relevance.
2. Notation Density and Roadmap: While the paper is well organized, the abundance of symbols and dense definitions (γ, K, TV distance, various Lipschitz constants) can challenge the reader. A summary table of key notation and main bounds, along with a high‐level road map or illustrative example of the (γ,K) stability argument, would improve readability.
3. Treatment of the Large-K Regime: The primary focus on small adaptation batch size yields tight O(1/(m n)) rates, but the large‐K analysis is deferred to the appendix and lacks exposition. Summarizing those results in the main text—perhaps with concrete conditions under which large K remains acceptable—would round out the theoretical picture.
4. Empirical Validation and Bound Tightness: Although the paper is theoretical, a small set of synthetic experiments demonstrating the bound behavior (e.g., scaling with 1/(m n) or with TV distance) would help readers gauge tightness and connect theory to practice, as suggested by Alternative Reviewers.
5. Related Work and Societal Impact: The related‐work section could be expanded to contrast this analysis with recent results on generalization in deep meta‐learning [e.g., Wang et al. (2020) on convergence/generalization of gradient‐based methods] and with studies on sample distribution across tasks [e.g., Cioba et al. (2021)]. Furthermore, while theoretical, the paper should briefly acknowledge that meta‐learning systems may amplify biases when task distributions differ, and suggest cautious application in sensitive domains.

Recommendation:
This paper makes a solid, novel contribution to the theoretical foundations of MAML by deriving tight, stability-based generalization bounds for both recurring and unseen tasks. The main limitation—its convexity assumption—is shared by much of the current literature, and the work nonetheless lays groundwork for future nonconvex extensions. With minor revisions to improve clarity, broaden the related work discussion, and address practical considerations, I strongly support acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization properties of MAML in the strongly convex setting. It's assumed that there are $m$ training tasks, each with $n$ samples, and the inner loop adaptation is one step of SGD using $K$ samples. It's proved that if the new task is a uniformly random task from one of the $m$ training tasks, MAML incurs a generalization error of $O(\frac{K}{mn})$; if the new task is an unseen task, the error depends on the TV distance between the new task distribution and all the training task distributions as well as their average. The analysis is based on a modified version of algorithmic stability tailored to the MAML setup.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The contribution of this paper is new. The paper obtains an $O(1/mn)$ bound in the strongly convex setting, while the previous result in [25] is $O(1/m)$ (though [25] covers non-convex losses). The proof technique is based on algorithmic stability but also needs nontrivial modification.

Quality: The paper is technically sound. The results are clearly stated and discussed.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well organized.

Significance:
- I think the most interesting result is the $O(1/mn)$ error bound for "in-task generalization", i.e., generalizing to a random training task. The previous bound for MAML is only $O(1/n)$ in [25]. To obtain the $O(1/mn)$ bound, a new type of stability (Definition 3) is required, which is non-trivial.
- However, I believe the result of generalization to an unseen task (Theorem 3) is very loose and does not capture the actual interesting aspects of meta-learning. Essentially, for this bound to be small it requires the new task distribution to be very close in TV distance to all the training tasks as well as their average. However, this is almost never the case. For example, suppose that all the tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution over tasks (which is usually true in a lot of few-shot learning benchmarks). Then $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{m+1}$ could all be very different from each other, but we can still expect MAML to generalize well to $p_{m+1}$, since it's a task drawn from the same meta-distribution. The bound provided in the current paper dramatically fails to capture this.
- The strong convexity is certainly very restrictive, and it's noted that the technique used in the paper cannot generalize beyond the convex case. That said, this is understandable and acceptable as a first step for theoretical purpose. 

Other questions:
- The paper only considers 1-step SGD adaptation in the inner loop. Is there any hope to generalize the result to multiple steps?
- It seems that a somewhat non-standard data splitting procedure is used, i.e. first splitting the data into $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$ each with size $n$, and then the samples used for inner and outer loops are sampled from these two subsets. How important is this in the theoretical analysis and is it unavoidable?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As noted in the main review, I believe the generalization result to an unseen task is very loose and can be misleading. A discussion of this is needed.

Summary:
This paper studies the generalization properties of MAML in the strongly convex setting. It's assumed that there are $m$ training tasks, each with $n$ samples, and the inner loop adaptation is one step of SGD using $K$ samples. It's proved that if the new task is a uniformly random task from one of the $m$ training tasks, MAML incurs a generalization error of $O(\frac{K}{mn})$; if the new task is an unseen task, the error depends on the TV distance between the new task distribution and all the training task distributions as well as their average. The analysis is based on a modified version of algorithmic stability tailored to the MAML setup.

Main Review:
Originality: The contribution of this paper is new. The paper obtains an $O(1/mn)$ bound in the strongly convex setting, while the previous result in [25] is $O(1/m)$ (though [25] covers non-convex losses). The proof technique is based on algorithmic stability but also needs nontrivial modification.

Quality: The paper is technically sound. The results are clearly stated and discussed.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well organized.

Significance:
- I think the most interesting result is the $O(1/mn)$ error bound for "in-task generalization", i.e., generalizing to a random training task. The previous bound for MAML is only $O(1/n)$ in [25]. To obtain the $O(1/mn)$ bound, a new type of stability (Definition 3) is required, which is non-trivial.
- However, I believe the result of generalization to an unseen task (Theorem 3) is very loose and does not capture the actual interesting aspects of meta-learning. Essentially, for this bound to be small it requires the new task distribution to be very close in TV distance to all the training tasks as well as their average. However, this is almost never the case. For example, suppose that all the tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution over tasks (which is usually true in a lot of few-shot learning benchmarks). Then $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{m+1}$ could all be very different from each other, but we can still expect MAML to generalize well to $p_{m+1}$, since it's a task drawn from the same meta-distribution. The bound provided in the current paper dramatically fails to capture this.

Other questions:
- The paper only considers 1-step SGD adaptation in the inner loop. Is there any hope to generalize the result to multiple steps?
- It seems that a somewhat non-standard data splitting procedure is used, i.e. first splitting the data into $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$ each with size $n$, and then the samples used for inner and outer loops are sampled from these two subsets. How important is this in the theoretical analysis and is it unavoidable?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As noted in the main review, I believe the generalization result to an unseen task is very loose and can be misleading. A discussion of this is needed.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper offers a theoretical investigation of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in the supervised, strongly convex setting. Under the assumption of \(m\) training tasks each with \(n\) samples and a one-step SGD inner loop using \(K\) adaptation samples, the authors establish two main results. First, for a recurring test task drawn uniformly from the training set, they introduce a novel \((\gamma,K)\)-uniform stability definition and prove an \(O(1/(m n))\) generalization bound—sharpening the prior \(O(1/m)\) rate. Second, for an unseen test task, they bound excess risk in terms of the total variation (TV) distance between the new task distribution and those of the training tasks. The analysis leverages a modified algorithmic stability framework tailored to the meta-learning setup and standard convex optimization guarantees for SGD.

Originality and Significance:  
– The introduction of a \((\gamma,K)\)-uniform stability notion represents a meaningful advance, capturing both the number of tasks and per-task samples and yielding the first \(O(1/(m n))\) bound in this convex MAML context.  
– The coupling argument for unseen tasks, linking generalization to TV distance, is a novel theoretical contribution that frames task heterogeneity in distributional terms.  
– While these results lay important groundwork for understanding meta-learning generalization, the restriction to strongly convex losses limits immediate applicability to nonconvex deep-network scenarios where MAML is most widely used.

Technical Quality:  
– The proofs appear sound and are supported by detailed appendices. Standard SGD convergence arguments are carefully adapted to the two-stage meta-learning objective.  
– That said, the notation is dense and heavy, and several constants—especially in the large-\(K\) regime—are deferred to appendices. A concise summary table of main bounds and assumptions, along with a clearer road map of the proof structure, would greatly aid readability. Alternative reviews similarly noted that an illustrative example (e.g., for the coupling in the unseen-task analysis) would demystify the technical ideas.

Clarity and Presentation:  
– The paper is generally well organized, and definitions, lemmas, and propositions are clearly labeled.  
– However, inconsistencies in formatting and the abundance of Greek symbols can impede comprehension. A distilled table of stability definitions and key results—perhaps accompanied by a brief, high-level overview—would streamline the exposition.  
– More discussion of the large-\(K\) adaptation regime in the main text (rather than relegating it to the appendix) would also improve the narrative flow.

Weaknesses and Limitations:  
1. Unseen-Task Bound Is Overly Loose:  
   The TV-distance bound for novel tasks requires the new task distribution to lie very close (in TV) to each training distribution, which seldom holds in practice. In common few-shot benchmarks where tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution, tasks can differ significantly in TV yet still yield good empirical performance. As the main review notes, this bound fails to capture the more nuanced generalization behavior observed in meta-learning.  
2. Strong Convexity Assumption:  
   While understandable for a first theoretical step, requiring strong convexity (and Hessian Lipschitzness) rules out most deep-network applications. Extending the analysis to weakly convex or nonconvex losses remains a key open challenge.  
3. Single Inner-Loop Step:  
   The focus on one-step SGD adaptation limits the scope. It is unclear whether and how the stability arguments extend to multiple adaptation steps—a question raised in the main review and echoed by alternative critiques.  
4. Lack of Empirical Validation:  
   No experiments or synthetic simulations are provided to demonstrate the tightness or practical relevance of the theoretical rates. Empirical illustrations would bolster confidence in the bounds.  
5. Practicality of TV Distance:  
   Estimating or bounding total variation distance between unknown task distributions is nontrivial. A discussion of practical proxies (e.g., Wasserstein distance or kernel-based measures) or methods to estimate TV in meta-learning contexts would strengthen the unseen-task analysis.

Questions and Suggestions:  
– Can the authors generalize their stability framework to multi-step SGD adaptation, and if so, how does the rate degrade?  
– How critical is the nonstandard data splitting into \(S_i^{\text{in}}\) and \(S_i^{\text{out}}\)? Could a more realistic sampling scheme be analyzed?  
– Consider summarizing main results and assumptions in a single table and providing a high-level roadmap in the introduction.  
– An illustrative toy example of the coupling argument for unseen tasks would help readers grasp the core idea.  
– Discuss potential alternative divergence measures beyond TV distance that might yield tighter unseen-task bounds.  

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
As noted, the convex assumptions greatly narrow the paper’s applicability. The loose unseen-task bound may mislead practitioners into overestimating MAML’s robustness under distribution shift. While this work is primarily theoretical, meta-learning methods have been shown to propagate biases present in task distributions; a brief discussion of fairness and robustness under distributional heterogeneity would enhance responsible reporting.

Rating: 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection.  
While the paper makes novel contributions in refining stability-based bounds for MAML and formalizing unseen-task generalization via TV distance, the overly restrictive assumptions, loose distributional bound, and absence of empirical validation limit its practical impact. The manuscript would benefit from tighter unseen-task analysis, extensions beyond strong convexity, clearer exposition, and illustrative experiments before considering publication.

Summary:
This paper studies the generalization properties of MAML in the strongly convex setting. It's assumed that there are $m$ training tasks, each with $n$ samples, and the inner loop adaptation is one step of SGD using $K$ samples. It's proved that if the new task is a uniformly random task from one of the $m$ training tasks, MAML incurs a generalization error of $O(\frac{K}{mn})$; if the new task is an unseen task, the error depends on the TV distance between the new task distribution and all the training task distributions as well as their average. The analysis is based on a modified version of algorithmic stability tailored to the MAML setup.

Main Review:
Originality: The contribution of this paper is new. The paper obtains an $O(1/mn)$ bound in the strongly convex setting, while the previous result in [25] is $O(1/m)$ (though [25] covers non-convex losses). The proof technique is based on algorithmic stability but also needs nontrivial modification.

Quality: The paper is technically sound. The results are clearly stated and discussed.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well organized.

Significance:
- I think the most interesting result is the $O(1/mn)$ error bound for "in-task generalization", i.e., generalizing to a random training task. The previous bound for MAML is only $O(1/n)$ in [25]. To obtain the $O(1/mn)$ bound, a new type of stability (Definition 3) is required, which is non-trivial.
- However, I believe the result of generalization to an unseen task (Theorem 3) is very loose and does not capture the actual interesting aspects of meta-learning. Essentially, for this bound to be small it requires the new task distribution to be very close in TV distance to all the training tasks as well as their average. However, this is almost never the case. For example, suppose that all the tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution over tasks (which is usually true in a lot of few-shot learning benchmarks). Then $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{m+1}$ could all be very different from each other, but we can still expect MAML to generalize well to $p_{m+1}$, since it's a task drawn from the same meta-distribution. The bound provided in the current paper dramatically fails to capture this.
- The strong convexity is certainly very restrictive, and it's noted that the technique used in the paper cannot generalize beyond the convex case. That said, this is understandable and acceptable as a first step for theoretical purpose. 

Other questions:
- The paper only considers 1-step SGD adaptation in the inner loop. Is there any hope to generalize the result to multiple steps?
- It seems that a somewhat non-standard data splitting procedure is used, i.e. first splitting the data into $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$ each with size $n$, and then the samples used for inner and outer loops are sampled from these two subsets. How important is this in the theoretical analysis and is it unavoidable?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As noted in the main review, I believe the generalization result to an unseen task is very loose and can be misleading. A discussion of this is needed.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Stabilizing Deep Q-Learning with ConvNets and Vision Transformers under Data Augmentation

reviews.summary:
This paper studies the problem of high-variance Q-targets under data augmentation, and proposes a method to reduce the variance of the targets called SVEA. The key idea contains three parts: (1) Apply augmentation to the Q-value estimation of the current state and not the next state Q-targets for bootstrapping, and (2) modify the objective to include both the targets using augmented and original observations and (3) optimize the actor using only the unaugmented data for actor-critic algorithms, with parameter sharing. 

The paper performed experiments in DeepMind control suite and its variants (with raw pixel inputs). Compared to other data augmentation methods (e.g. DrQ) their proposed SVEA method is robust to the type of data augmentation and is more sample efficient with a better final performance. Some ablations were also presented among the above components to indicate the usefulness of all components. Generalization performance was demonstrated in the DistractingCS and robotic manipulation environments. The method also appears to help when using a Vision Transformers architecture.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality**: The proposed framework can be seen as a generalization of existing data augmentation approaches, by considering combinations of both augmented and unaugmented data in the loss function. The related work section is adequately touching upon the several areas which this framework can be viewed from. 

**Quality**: There is no theoretical analysis for the proposed framework, but the paper presents several empirical results to back up the claims, including ablations on the various components. However, I would suggest backing up the claim on Pitfall 1: Non-deterministic Q-target with some empirical data as well. How bad is the variance with non-deterministic Q-targets in practice? With SVEA, even though we have deterministic Q-targets, we still have noisy predictions due to the data augmentation. So there can still be comparison of variance in the case of combination of augmented and unaugmented current state and next state. 

For the alpha and beta hyperparameter: it was mentioned that 0.5/0.5 works well for the experiments. Have the authors tried varying this parameter throughout the training (i.e. some sort of annealing schedule), and whether that hinders or helps the performance even more?

**Clarity**: The paper is overall fairly clear to read. 

**Significance**: Given the recent popularity of applying data augmentation in vision-based reinforcement learning agents, I believe that the proposed technique, which is simple to implement, can be easily incorporated as a toolbox of RL practitioners/researchers. Demonstrating that this framework still scales to ViT architectures is also promising. It would be nice to have some more theoretical justification for the framework by the original authors, but perhaps that could be a follow up work (potentially by others).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Hybrid Review

Summary:  
This paper addresses the instability that arises when applying strong data augmentations in off-policy deep Q-learning and proposes SVEA (Stabilized Q-Value Estimation under Augmentation) to mitigate two key pitfalls: (1) high‐variance, non-deterministic Q-targets when augmenting successor states for bootstrapping; and (2) over-regularization from training solely on augmented observations. SVEA’s recipe is threefold: apply augmentations only to current-state Q-value estimates (not to next-state targets), employ a joint loss that mixes augmented and unaugmented inputs for Q-network updates, and restrict actor updates to raw observations. Crucially, these modifications require neither extra network parameters nor additional forward passes beyond standard off-policy algorithms. The authors validate SVEA across the DeepMind Control Suite (including DistractingCS), robotic manipulation tasks, multiple augmentation types, and both ConvNet and Vision Transformer backbones. Compared to strong baselines (e.g. DrQ, RAD, CURL, SODA), SVEA yields substantially improved sample efficiency, stability, and out-of-distribution performance at comparable or lower computational cost.

Originality:  
While previous works have applied data augmentation or auxiliary regularizers in vision-based RL, this paper makes a novel contribution by pinpointing how naïve augmentation destabilizes Q-learning and by unifying three lightweight, principled remedies into one cohesive framework. The insight to decouple augmentation from bootstrapping, to mix data modalities in the Q-value loss, and to isolate policy updates is a new combination that extends and generalizes existing augmentation schemes. The related work discussion is comprehensive, situating SVEA relative to on-policy and self-supervised methods, though connections to classical TD variance-reduction techniques and bisimulation metrics could be elaborated.

Quality:  
The empirical evaluation is extensive and well controlled. The authors test six distinct augmentation strategies, multiple task suites (DMControl, DistractingCS, robotics), and both CNN and ViT architectures. They report means and standard deviations over several seeds, and include ablations that isolate each SVEA component. Wall-clock comparisons demonstrate that no extra forward passes or parameters are required, underscoring the low computational overhead.

That said, the paper would be strengthened by:
•  Empirical quantification of the variance in Q-targets under naïve augmentation versus SVEA, to substantiate Pitfall 1 more concretely.  
•  A sensitivity analysis or scheduling scheme for the mixing coefficients (α, β); the fixed 0.5/0.5 choice may not be optimal across domains or during different training phases.  
•  Pseudocode for the overall algorithm and a concise summary table of key hyperparameters in the main text to improve reproducibility.  
•  Reporting per-iteration runtime or FLOPs alongside wall-clock measurements to help practitioners assess compute trade-offs precisely.

Clarity:  
Overall, the manuscript is well written and logically structured. The problem motivation and architectural overview are clearly conveyed, and figures effectively illustrate the pitfalls and the SVEA pipeline. A few refinements would enhance readability:
•  Move lengthy derivations and dense notation in the preliminaries to an appendix, replacing them with a high-level summary in the main text.  
•  Streamline equations (3)–(7) by consolidating redundant symbols and ensure consistent formatting (e.g., boldface parameters, subscript placement).  
•  Provide a brief algorithm box or pseudocode to anchor the key steps of SVEA.

Significance:  
Stabilizing strong augmentations is a pressing challenge in visual RL, especially as researchers move toward large-scale architectures like ViTs. SVEA’s simplicity, empirical gains, and minimal overhead make it a valuable addition to the RL toolbox. Its demonstrated compatibility with ViTs suggests broad applicability to modern vision models. Adoption in other off-policy algorithms (e.g., TD3, DQN variants) or on-policy settings could further extend its impact. While the current results are promising, real-world validation under sensor noise, dynamics mismatch, or in safety-critical robotic deployments remains an important avenue for future work.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The authors acknowledge ecological costs but stop short of quantification. To solidify claims of efficiency, reporting energy consumption, GPU hours, or carbon footprint alongside performance would be beneficial. The fixed mixing coefficients and exclusive focus on SAC leave open questions about generalization to other training regimes. Real-world applications may face additional challenges from adversarial visuals or domain shifts; guidance on safe exploration protocols and monitoring failure modes would strengthen the broader impact statement. Finally, while open-sourcing code supports reproducibility, it also raises potential for misuse; a brief note on responsible deployment practices would be prudent.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
Given the novelty of the identified pitfalls, the methodological clarity, and the strong empirical results—even as there is room for deeper theoretical analysis and more extensive compute/energy reporting—I recommend this paper for acceptance.

reviews.summary:
(Auxiliary Review only -- the authors need not respond to this).

This work proposes issues with data augmentation in RL and proposes a couple of techniques to address those issues. It applies augmentation only on the current state and not future state to address erroneous bootstrapping. It also proposes a Q objective that uses both the augmented and un-augmented data. Also, for actor critic experiments, the actor is optimised only with un-augmented data and learn a  policy that is general via sharing parameters between the networks. These are simple techniques that help the paper demonstrate good results in a range of tasks.

This is a clear paper with good set of experiments. The problem has been described clearly and the work proposes simple refinements to the data augmentation strategies used in RL. While I am convinced of the quality of the method itself and the experiments, this work could have benefitted from more rigour with the baselines to make a stronger case for the method. Hence, I am offering a score of 6 noting that the paper is marginally above acceptance threshold.
reviews.main_review:
Note : * This is an AUXILIARY REVIEW only. The authors are not expected to respond to this review or any of the concerns presented here. * *

The paper is well-written, clear and provides a good description of the experiments. It clearly sets up the problem that standard data augmentation methods suffer from. It also provides empirical solutions to these problems and backs up these claims with experimental results. Even though the paper justifies the claims only empirically based on experimentation, the ideas proposed here have made simple and easily extendable modifications, which can be very useful in complex state action spaces when RL+Data augmentation is employed. The ideas proposed here are pretty simple and straightforward, but I am not convinced these methods alone provide us a full-fledged framework for data augmentation as claimed. Also, any theoretical justification provided with this work is not rigorous enough to demonstrate that these methods are universal for all data augmentation problems.  On the novelty front, the paper's key contribution seems to be the idea of using un-augmented inputs to the targets. There are two ways to look at this -- just as a simple innovation being not novel enough on its own or trying to understand how the work arrives at this simple innovation and whether the innovation helps improve the algorithm performance over a wide variety of tasks. I am more inclined towards the latter. I believe this is still a useful enough contribution to practitioners and researchers alike, where a few simple modifications help greatly stabilize performance.

My major concern with the paper was the hyper-parameters and architectures used for the baselines. It looks like the adapted baselines were handicapped a bit by architectural changes and these baselines were not tuned to optimality, or at least it was not very evident that this was done thoroughly. This takes a bit of the shine off the impressive results presented by the paper and I am not sure if tuning the parameters would change any relative comparison between those baselines and this method.

The paper's proposed methods are good and the results are useful on their own to the community. Having said that, because of the other minor issues mentioned earlier, I am not able to give a higher score than 6 for this paper.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the instability caused by naïve data augmentation in off-policy deep Q-learning and proposes a simple yet effective framework, SVEA (Stabilized Q-Value Estimation under Augmentation). The authors identify two core pitfalls: (1) high-variance, non-deterministic Q-targets when successor states are augmented, and (2) over-regularization when training exclusively on augmented observations. To remedy these, SVEA (i) applies augmentations only to the current state for Q-value estimation, (ii) employs a mixed-data Q-objective that jointly optimizes on both augmented and unaugmented inputs, and (iii) restricts policy (actor) updates to raw observations. The approach entails no extra parameters or additional network passes beyond the standard off-policy RL pipeline. Extensive experiments on the DeepMind Control Suite (including generalization benchmarks), Distracting Control, robotic manipulation tasks, and with both ConvNet and Vision Transformer encoders demonstrate improved stability, sample efficiency, and out-of-distribution performance compared to strong baselines at comparable or lower computational cost.

Strengths:
1. Clear Problem Setup and Motivation  
   – The paper lucidly diagnoses why standard augmentation methods (e.g., DrQ, RAD) can destabilize off-policy Q-learning, supported by quantitative measurements of Q-target variance and empirical Q-value discrepancies.  
   – Illustrative figures and a concise pitfall analysis help readers grasp the necessity for targeted modifications rather than blind augmentation.

2. Simplicity and Practicality  
   – SVEA’s three modifications are straightforward to implement and require no extra forward passes or architectural changes. This low overhead makes it attractive for practitioners seeking to enhance generalization in vision-based RL.  
   – The joint optimization on mixed data and the decision to train the actor only on unaugmented observations represent a novel combination of ideas that advances the state of the art in stabilized augmented RL.

3. Empirical Thoroughness  
   – Experiments cover six types of augmentations, multiple benchmarks (DMControl-GB, Distracting Control Suite), and both ConvNet and Vision Transformer encoders, demonstrating consistency across domains.  
   – Ablation studies isolate the contribution of each SVEA component (no-augment targets, mixed loss, unaugmented actor), showing that each element yields non-trivial gains.  
   – Reporting of multiple random seeds, standard deviations, and implementation details fosters reproducibility. Wall-clock comparisons further suggest that SVEA matches or improves sample efficiency at similar compute budgets.

4. Significance for Vision-Based RL  
   – Stabilizing augmentation enables the use of stronger perturbations and modern architectures (e.g., ViTs), broadening the applicability of off-policy RL to more diverse and challenging visual tasks.  
   – By addressing a key barrier to robust, generalizable RL policies, SVEA is likely to be adopted in both academic and industrial settings.

Weaknesses:
1. Baseline Tuning and Fairness of Comparison  
   – It remains unclear whether the competing methods (DrQ, CURL, RAD, SODA) were tuned to optimal hyperparameters and network architectures comparable to those used for SVEA. The architectural adaptations for baselines may have inadvertently handicapped their performance, casting some doubt on the reported gains.

2. Limited Theoretical Justification  
   – While empirical variance measurements motivate the method, no formal variance bound or convergence analysis is provided. A more rigorous theoretical treatment—e.g., bias–variance trade-off discussion or convergence guarantees—would strengthen confidence in SVEA’s general applicability.

3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity  
   – The mixing coefficients (α, β) are fixed (e.g., 0.5) throughout. Alternative reviews suggest that sensitivity analyses over these weights or dynamic scheduling strategies could clarify the robustness of SVEA across tasks.  
   – Additional ablations on how performance varies with augmentation strength and data-mix ratios would bolster the empirical claims.

4. Scope of Evaluation  
   – The experiments are confined to SAC as the base algorithm. It is unknown how SVEA performs with other off-policy variants (e.g., TD3, DQN family) or on-policy methods (e.g., PPO).  
   – Real-world deployment considerations—such as sensor noise, dynamics mismatch in sim-to-real transfer, and safety-critical failure modes—are not addressed. Including a brief discussion of potential deployment risks and validation protocols would enhance the paper’s societal impact section.

5. Compute and Energy Reporting  
   – Although wall-clock efficiency is qualitatively mentioned, precise measurements of GPU hours, FLOPs, and carbon footprint are absent. Quantifying compute costs relative to baselines would provide a clearer picture of SVEA’s environmental and economic benefits.

Minor Comments:
– Section 3’s notation could be streamlined to improve readability; moving some derivations to the appendix may reduce cognitive load.  
– Providing pseudocode for the mixed-data update and key hyperparameters in the main text would help practitioners reproduce the method more readily.  
– A brief discussion connecting SVEA’s variance-reduction effect to classical TD variance-reduction techniques could situate the work within broader RL theory.

Decision:  
Rating 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
Despite some concerns about baseline tuning, theoretical grounding, and hyperparameter sensitivity, the paper offers a clear, practical, and empirically validated framework that meaningfully advances stability under data augmentation for off-policy RL. Its simplicity and broad applicability justify acceptance, conditional on the authors addressing the outlined issues in the final version.

reviews.summary: This work proposes a simple technique to improve the robustness and sample-efficiency of reinforcement learning. The technique can be applied to any algorithm via a simple modification of the input pipeline to said algorithm's Q-learning subroutine. The claim is that this modification reduces the variance of Q-targets and therefore stabilizes learning.
reviews.main_review:
Disclaimer: Emergency review with all the short-comings that comes with.

# Originality

While the idea of data-augmentation has been extensively used in both supervised and reinforcement learning, this paper proposes an approach that in some ways generalizes the DrQ approach, and in others simplifies it. In my view the modifications, though subtle, are novel.

# Quality

The work produces an extensive array of empirical results showing reobust generalization and impressive sample-efficiency. The comparisons to DrQ lack specification of the hyperparamters K and M, which can easily be added. If, as I suspect, the comparison is with DrQ[K=1, M=1], it would be nice to see a comparison to the default DrQ[K=2, M=2] and/or at the very least DrQ[K=2, M=1], which matches the number of forward passes through the Q-network.

Similarly, in the empirical demonstration of Pitfall 2 (Figure 1) it would be nice to see the equivalent plot for various values of K.

Finally, I would slightly quibble with the claim of "theoretically grounded" on line 30, as this paper does not feature any rigorous theoretical statements.

# Clarity

Overall the paper is very clearly and well written. The only major point of contention is that DrQ is parameterized by a K and M which control how many samples the target Q-values and TD loss, respectively, are averaged over. It would be good to specify which is being used here. It sounds like [K=1, M=1] (?) but I would think the fairest comparison would be [K=2, M=1].


## Minor points

- The targets on line 215 and line 9 of Algorithm 1 presumably look different in continuous control cases, which is the main focus of the experimental section.
- Very minor point: would the expressions not be equivalent but simpler if the trade-off was simply alpha and (1 - alpha), instead of beta?
- The experimental setup is slightly unclear in the text, copying the caption of Figure 3 into the main body would help. In particular, the sentence "all methods are trained for 500k frames and evaluated on the full set of tasks".
- Figure 6 (left) needs error-bars.

# Significance

Data-augmentation seems to be a very simple technique for improving the sample-efficiency and generalizability of RL agents. This is particularly important for real-world applications where interactions with the environment can be expensive and the sim-to-real gap can be prohibitive.
