reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work proposes a simple technique to improve the robustness and sample-efficiency of reinforcement learning. The technique can be applied to any algorithm via a simple modification of the input pipeline to said algorithm's Q-learning subroutine. The claim is that this modification reduces the variance of Q-targets and therefore stabilizes learning.

Main Review:
Disclaimer: Emergency review with all the short-comings that comes with.

# Originality

While the idea of data-augmentation has been extensively used in both supervised and reinforcement learning, this paper proposes an approach that in some ways generalizes the DrQ approach, and in others simplifies it. In my view the modifications, though subtle, are novel.

# Quality

The work produces an extensive array of empirical results showing reobust generalization and impressive sample-efficiency. The comparisons to DrQ lack specification of the hyperparamters K and M, which can easily be added. If, as I suspect, the comparison is with DrQ[K=1, M=1], it would be nice to see a comparison to the default DrQ[K=2, M=2] and/or at the very least DrQ[K=2, M=1], which matches the number of forward passes through the Q-network.

Similarly, in the empirical demonstration of Pitfall 2 (Figure 1) it would be nice to see the equivalent plot for various values of K.

Finally, I would slightly quibble with the claim of "theoretically grounded" on line 30, as this paper does not feature any rigorous theoretical statements.

# Clarity

Overall the paper is very clearly and well written. The only major point of contention is that DrQ is parameterized by a K and M which control how many samples the target Q-values and TD loss, respectively, are averaged over. It would be good to specify which is being used here. It sounds like [K=1, M=1] (?) but I would think the fairest comparison would be [K=2, M=1].


## Minor points

- The targets on line 215 and line 9 of Algorithm 1 presumably look different in continuous control cases, which is the main focus of the experimental section.
- Very minor point: would the expressions not be equivalent but simpler if the trade-off was simply alpha and (1 - alpha), instead of beta?
- The experimental setup is slightly unclear in the text, copying the caption of Figure 3 into the main body would help. In particular, the sentence "all methods are trained for 500k frames and evaluated on the full set of tasks".
- Figure 6 (left) needs error-bars.

# Significance

Data-augmentation seems to be a very simple technique for improving the sample-efficiency and generalizability of RL agents. This is particularly important for real-world applications where interactions with the environment can be expensive and the sim-to-real gap can be prohibitive.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I did not see any mention of limitations. The authors mentioned the growing footprint of large models and how this work could help negotiate that.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes SVEA, a simple yet effective modification to augment-based off-policy Q-learning aimed at improving robustness and sample efficiency. By identifying two key pitfalls of naïve augmentation—high-variance, non-deterministic Q-targets when bootstrapping on augmented successor states, and over-regularization when training exclusively on augmented inputs—SVEA remedies instability through three lightweight changes: (1) computing Q-targets on unaugmented successor states, (2) jointly optimizing Q-values over both augmented and raw observations via a mixed-data objective, and (3) restricting policy updates to unaugmented data. Crucially, these adjustments incur no extra network parameters or additional forward passes. The authors present extensive empirical evidence on the DeepMind Control Suite (including generalization and distracting-control benchmarks), robotic manipulation tasks, and architectures ranging from ConvNets to Vision Transformers. Results show that SVEA consistently reduces variance, stabilizes learning, and outperforms strong baselines (e.g., DrQ, CURL, RAD, SODA) at comparable or lower computational cost.

Originality:
While data augmentation and auxiliary objectives have appeared in prior work, this manuscript stands out by dissecting the specific mechanisms that destabilize off-policy Q-learning under augmentation and by unifying three minimal modifications into a coherent framework. Compared to DrQ, SVEA generalizes and simplifies the augmentation strategy without relying on extra hyperparameters for target sampling. The conceptual connection to classical variance-reduction techniques and bisimulation metrics, though not fully elaborated, underscores the novelty of isolating augmentation effects in both the target and policy update steps.

Quality:
The empirical evaluation is thorough and rigorously controlled. The authors examine multiple augmentation types, report standard deviations across random seeds, and provide wall-clock time comparisons to emphasize efficiency gains. Ablation studies isolate the contribution of each SVEA component, and varying the mixing coefficients (α, β) would further clarify robustness—an extension that could be included. The comparison to DrQ would benefit from clearer specification of its hyperparameters (K and M); in particular, including DrQ[K=2, M=1] would ensure a fair match in forward passes through the Q-network. Additionally, while the empirical variance measurements substantiate the pitfall hypotheses, a brief theoretical analysis or formal variance bound could strengthen the technical foundation and temper the claim of being “theoretically grounded.”

Clarity:
Overall, the paper is well written and logically structured. Figures illustrating the two augmentation pitfalls and the SVEA architecture are effective. However, certain sections feature dense notation—streamlining equations (3)–(7) and relocating detailed derivations to an appendix would improve readability. The main text should specify the experimental protocol more clearly (e.g., by incorporating key details from the caption of Figure 3), clarify continuous-control target expressions in Algorithm 1, and consistently annotate hyperparameters. Finally, Figure 6 (left) needs error bars to convey statistical significance.

Minor Points:
- The trade-off parameter could be simplified to (α, 1−α) instead of introducing β, which would streamline notation.
- Targets in continuous control scenarios might differ; explicitly stating this in the text or pseudocode would aid replication.
- Copying the evaluation setup from figure captions into the experimental section would help readers avoid ambiguity.
- Consistency in formatting subscripts and boldface notation would polish the presentation.

Significance:
Given the high cost of environment interactions and the challenges of sim-to-real transfer, SVEA’s ability to stabilize learning under strong image augmentations has clear practical importance. Its compatibility with existing off-policy methods and scalability to Vision Transformers positions it as a readily adoptable tool for both academic research and real-world robotics applications. By reducing variance and improving generalization without added computational burden, SVEA meaningfully advances the state of the art in visual reinforcement learning.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The study focuses exclusively on off-policy deep Q-learning (specifically SAC). Exploring SVEA’s benefits for other algorithms (e.g., TD3, DQN variants, or on-policy methods like PPO) would clarify its generality. Hyperparameter sensitivity—particularly the fixed mixing coefficients—remains underexplored; automated or theoretically guided tuning could enhance robustness. All experiments are conducted in simulation; real-world sensor noise, dynamics shifts, and safety constraints may introduce new challenges. While the authors note compute savings, quantifying energy usage, carbon footprint, and GPU-hour requirements would substantiate ecological claims. Finally, as robust vision-based RL systems move toward deployment in safety-critical or surveillance domains, a brief discussion of potential misuse, bias from augmentations, and guidelines for safe validation would strengthen the broader impact statement.

Recommendation:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary:
The paper addresses the question of zero-shot or fast generalization of value-based deep reinforcement learning agents using visual inputs to image transformation which do not change the (conceptual) state of the environment. The long term goal is therefore training agents which learn efficient, high return control policies both which are robust to irrelevant variations in high dimensional input observations. The ultimate goal seems to be generalization of learned skills to novel environments, but as far as I can tell this particular evaluation philosophy is implemented in experiments.

The paper reviews previous attempts to learn robust state representations to visual variations and formulates clear hypotheses for explaining the causes of difficulties which prior methods faced and were hindered by. The said hypotheses suggest a simplified approach (SVEA) which is empirically developed and evaluated in the remainder of the paper. Control tasks from widely used benchmarks are used to evaluate the method, somewhat less usual is the focus on vision-based high-dimensional versions of such tasks, instead of standard domains such as Atari games and benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari [1], CoinRun/ProcGen [2].
reviews.main_review:
I am generally happy with the paper, I have a few suggestions:

* The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function. Strictly speaking, value estimation is just a crutch. Hence, why bother with robust value estimation? Why not learn value functions without augmentations, use them to discover an optimal policy, and then use supervised learning [3, 4] to encode a robust policy? Please clarify in the text! The proposed method is attempting to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which may not be relevant if all that is needed is good control under diverse observation augmentations. How am I getting this wrong?
* Indeed, for all these environments it is not clear that value prediction from vision is needed at all, since a policy could be derived much faster from full state representations in terms of sample efficiency and computational speed, and then have that policy supervise a vision to action mapping, circumventing the robust value estimation problem altogether.
* There are many possible definitions of generalization in reinforcement learning; one is accurate value prediction in unseen states, as realistic problems have enormous state spaces; another is robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where states may have been seem before but there is extraneous variance in the observations which needs to be dealt with. Finally, there are progressively stronger definitions of generalization, namely across domain gaps which may or may not introduce variability relevant to the control policy itself. Which definition have you tested for in your experiments?
* Could you please clarify in the main text which experiments use some form of state information, e.g. "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, as well as target object information, and which do not? If such information is used, how does it reflect on the ability to extrapolate the presented results to problems where such information is not available.
* Could you please specify for each set of results whether the set of initial states was randomized and by how much? One surefire way to get robustness to high variability in observations is open-loop control, which can also be implemented by non-recurrent architectures, e.g. by relying heavily on proprioceptive inputs or general statistics of observations. 


References:
1. (Natural Atari) Amy Zhang et al. 2018. Natural Environment Benchmarks for Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.06032
2. (CoinRun/ProcGen) Karl Cobbe et al. 2019. Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01588
3. Andrei Rusu et al. 2015. Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06295
4. Wojciech Czarnecki et al. 2019. Distilling Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.02186
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:

The long term goal is therefore training agents which learn efficient, high return control policies both which are robust to irrelevant variations in high dimensional input observations. The ultimate goal seems to be generalization of learned skills to novel environments, but as far as I can tell this particular evaluation philosophy is implemented in experiments.

The paper reviews previous attempts to learn robust state representations to visual variations and formulates clear hypotheses for explaining the causes of difficulties which prior methods faced and were hindered by. The said hypotheses suggest a simplified approach (SVEA) which is empirically developed and evaluated in the remainder of the paper. Control tasks from widely used benchmarks are used to evaluate the method, somewhat less usual is the focus on vision-based high-dimensional versions of such tasks, instead of standard domains such as Atari games and benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari [1], CoinRun/ProcGen [2].

Main Review:

* The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function. Strictly speaking, value estimation is just a crutch. Hence, why bother with robust value estimation? Why not learn value functions without augmentations, use them to discover an optimal policy, and then use supervised learning [3, 4] to encode a robust policy? Please clarify in the text! The proposed method is attempting to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which may not be relevant if all that is needed is good control under diverse observation augmentations. How am I getting this wrong?
* Indeed, for all these environments it is not clear that value prediction from vision is needed at all, since a policy could be derived much faster from full state representations in terms of sample efficiency and computational speed, and then have that policy supervise a vision to action mapping, circumventing the robust value estimation problem altogether.
* There are many possible definitions of generalization in reinforcement learning; one is accurate value prediction in unseen states, as realistic problems have enormous state spaces; another is robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where states may have been seem before but there is extraneous variance in the observations which needs to be dealt with. Finally, there are progressively stronger definitions of generalization, namely across domain gaps which may or may not introduce variability relevant to the control policy itself. Which definition have you tested for in your experiments?
* Could you please clarify in the main text which experiments use some form of state information, e.g. "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, as well as target object information, and which do not? If such information is used, how does it reflect on the ability to extrapolate the presented results to problems where such information is not available.
* Could you please specify for each set of results whether the set of initial states was randomized and by how much? One surefire way to get robustness to high variability in observations is open-loop control, which can also be implemented by non-recurrent architectures, e.g. by relying heavily on proprioceptive inputs or general statistics of observations. 


References:
1. (Natural Atari) Amy Zhang et al. 2018. Natural Environment Benchmarks for Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.06032
2. (CoinRun/ProcGen) Karl Cobbe et al. 2019. Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01588
3. Andrei Rusu et al. 2015. Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06295
4. Wojciech Czarnecki et al. 2019. Distilling Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.02186

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper investigates the instability that arises when applying data augmentation in off-policy, value-based deep reinforcement learning and proposes SVEA (Stabilized Q-Value Estimation under Augmentation) as a simple yet effective remedy. The authors first formulate clear hypotheses about two primary pitfalls: (1) high-variance, non-deterministic Q-targets when augmenting bootstrap successor states, and (2) over-regularization when training exclusively on augmented observations. SVEA addresses these by computing Q-targets on unaugmented next states, optimizing a mixed-data Q-objective over both raw and augmented inputs, and restricting policy updates to unaugmented observations. The method introduces no extra network parameters or additional forward passes beyond a standard off-policy algorithm.

Strengths:  
• Novelty and Clarity of Insight: As in prior work, data augmentation has boosted representation learning in RL (e.g., DrQ, RAD), but the targeted analysis here—isolating why naïve augmentation destabilizes Q-learning—and the minimal modifications of SVEA together represent a fresh, principled contribution. The paper’s illustration of variance spikes under augmentation and the over-regularization effect are particularly well motivated.  
• Empirical Rigor: A broad suite of experiments on the DeepMind Control Suite (including Distracting Control Suite), robotic manipulation tasks, and both ConvNet and Vision Transformer (ViT) encoders demonstrates consistent gains in stability, sample efficiency, and out-of-distribution generalization compared to strong baselines (DrQ, CURL, RAD, SODA). Ablation studies tease apart each SVEA component’s contribution, and wall-clock comparisons underscore practical efficiency.  
• Practical Impact: By enabling stable training under strong augmentations and ViT backbones with minimal overhead, SVEA provides a recipe that is immediately useful to practitioners aiming for robust vision-based RL. The simplicity of the approach facilitates adoption in existing off-policy frameworks such as SAC.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Motivation around Value vs. Policy Learning: The introduction frames robust value estimation under observation augmentation as the core problem, yet the ultimate goal in RL is discovering high-return policies. Could the authors clarify why they focus on augmenting value functions rather than, for instance, learning value-free policies and then distilling these into robust vision-based policies via supervised learning [3,4]? A short discussion contrasting SVEA with policy-distillation approaches would strengthen the motivation.  
2. Definitions of Generalization: The paper references “generalization” broadly, from prediction in unseen states to domain gaps with irrelevant visual variability. It would help to explicitly state which notion(s) of generalization each experiment targets. For example, do the robotic tasks test domain shifts in dynamics or only visual distractors?  
3. Experimental Details:  
   • Proprioceptive Inputs and State Information: Please clarify which environments supply low-dimensional “state” (e.g., joint angles) or object-centric features alongside pixel observations, and whether these were used in SVEA’s policy and Q-networks.  
   • Initial State Randomization: For each benchmark, specify how initial states were randomized. As pointed out by prior works, open-loop control or reliance on proprioception can masquerade as robustness.  
   • Hyperparameter Sensitivity: While SVEA uses fixed mixing coefficients (α=β=0.5), the alternatives suggest exploring their sensitivity. An ablation sweep over these weights—or at least a qualitative discussion—would increase confidence in the method’s robustness.  
   • Pseudocode and Notation: Including concise pseudocode in the main text (or a boxed algorithm) and streamlining dense notation (particularly in Equations 3–7) would enhance clarity for practitioners replicating the work.  
4. Theoretical Considerations: All alternative reviewers noted the lack of formal analysis. While full convergence proofs may be beyond scope, a brief variance-reduction bound or discussion of bias–variance trade-offs in SVEA’s mixed-data objective would bolster the paper’s theoretical footing.  
5. Broader Impact and Limitations:  
   • Compute and Environmental Cost: The manuscript claims improved sample efficiency and lower computational cost, yet does not quantify energy usage, GPU hours, or FLOPs alongside baselines. Reporting these metrics would substantiate claims of ecological benefit.  
   • Real-World Deployment: Current results are in simulation. The paper would benefit from a discussion of sim-to-real transfer challenges—such as sensor noise, dynamics mismatch, and safety considerations—for robotic deployment. Suggestions for monitoring or certification strategies under unseen visual perturbations would be valuable.  
   • Societal Risks: Finally, as robust vision-based RL methods mature, they may be applied in safety-critical or surveillance contexts. A brief discussion of ethical deployment, adversarial vulnerabilities, and bias mitigation (e.g., under adversarial visual inputs) would strengthen the broader impact statement.

Minor Comments:  
– Some derivations could be moved to an appendix to streamline the main text.  
– Inconsistent formatting (e.g., subscript placement, boldface for network parameters) should be corrected.  
– Citations to related variance-reduction techniques from classical temporal–difference learning could be added when discussing prior methods.

Recommendation:  
I recommend marginal acceptance (Rating: 6: marginally above threshold). Despite some gaps in theoretical analysis and experimental detail, the paper presents a clear and practically significant contribution to stabilizing off-policy RL under strong visual augmentations. The method’s simplicity, broad empirical validation, and relevance to modern architectures (including ViTs) make it a strong candidate for publication, provided the authors address the above clarifications and augment the discussion of limitations and computational costs.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a way of improving training stability of DrQ (an image-based RL algorithm that uses data augmentation) by applying image augmentation more carefully. The authors propose to input a mix of augmented and un-augmented observations into the critic, and stop augmenting the actor's inputs. On the considered benchmarks the proposed method demonstrates better generalization results.
reviews.main_review:
The paper identifies an issue with the increased variance of the target estimation in DrQ (image-based RL with data augmentation). The authors conjecture two root causes for the increased variance: non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization.
To remedy these issues they propose to apply data augmentation more carefully. Specifically, the authors suggest to mix augmented and un-augmented inputs to the critic and stop augmenting of the actor's inputs. This method is then empirically justified in a series of experiment on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite.

Pros:
* The author investigate an interesting question of an effect of data augmentation on generalization in image-based RL. 
* The method is thoroughly verified in different settings. 
* The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
* The exploration of alternative architecture, such as ViT.

Cons:
* The paper proposes a small trick that lacks in novelty. The impact of data-augmentation on the estimation variance was also studied in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et. al), where a very similar technique was proposed -- the target estimation was done over 2 different augmentations. This work introduces a tiny modification of the DrQ's variance reduction approach, that just combines one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of combining two augmented observations. Furthermore, the authors fail to mention this prior result in their paper and compare to it. 
* From the paper it is not clear what hyper-parameters are used for the baselines, specifically for DrQ. If the authors use the original DrQ's parameters, then I see several major issues with the empirical results: 1) SVEA uses encoder arch that consists of 11 conv layers, while DrQ only uses 4. 2) The size of replay buffer of SVEA is 500K, while DrQ's 100K. 3) SVEA virtually increases the batch size by factor of 2, which usually leads to better performance on the DeepMind Control Suite tasks, it is not clear if the authors increase batch size for DrQ or not. These discrepancies alone could result in a drastic difference in performance and can render the empirical study to be invalid. 
* There is no experiments that compare individual components of SVEA to verify is that the improved performance comes from the proposed method or better/different hyper-parameters. 
* The authors claim in line 30 that they "theoretically ground" their findings, but this is not the case as there is no theoretical results presented. In general, the authors don't do a good job justifying that the two pitfalls are real. Their argument is hand-wavy and is based on a series of indirect comparisons to the baseline (DrQ). Unfortunately, there are too many moving parts in those comparisons that make it virtually impossible to validate the claim (see also the point above about the hyper-parameter discrepancies).

Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to mention the prior work that is almost identical, and the inconclusive empirical study I recommend to reject this paper from a high-profile venue of NeurIPS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a way of improving training stability of DrQ (an image-based RL algorithm that uses data augmentation) by applying image augmentation more carefully. The authors propose to input a mix of augmented and un-augmented observations into the critic, and stop augmenting the actor's inputs. On the considered benchmarks the proposed method demonstrates better generalization results.

Main Review:
The paper identifies an issue with the increased variance of the target estimation in DrQ (image-based RL with data augmentation). The authors conjecture two root causes for the increased variance: non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization.
To remedy these issues they propose to apply data augmentation more carefully. Specifically, the authors suggest to mix augmented and un-augmented inputs to the critic and stop augmenting of the actor's inputs. This method is then empirically justified in a series of experiment on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite.

Pros:
* The author investigate an interesting question of an effect of data augmentation on generalization in image-based RL. 
* The method is thoroughly verified in different settings. 
* The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
* The exploration of alternative architecture, such as ViT.

Cons:
* The paper proposes a small trick that lacks in novelty. The impact of data-augmentation on the estimation variance was also studied in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et. al), where a very similar technique was proposed -- the target estimation was done over 2 different augmentations. This work introduces a tiny modification of the DrQ's variance reduction approach, that just combines one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of combining two augmented observations. Furthermore, the authors fail to mention this prior result in their paper and compare to it. 
* From the paper it is not clear what hyper-parameters are used for the baselines, specifically for DrQ. If the authors use the original DrQ's parameters, then I see several major issues with the empirical results: 1) SVEA uses encoder arch that consists of 11 conv layers, while DrQ only uses 4. 2) The size of replay buffer of SVEA is 500K, while DrQ's 100K. 3) SVEA virtually increases the batch size by factor of 2, which usually leads to better performance on the DeepMind Control Suite tasks, it is not clear if the authors increase batch size for DrQ or not. These discrepancies alone could result in a drastic difference in performance and can render the empirical study to be invalid. 
* There is no experiments that compare individual components of SVEA to verify is that the improved performance comes from the proposed method or better/different hyper-parameters. 
* The authors claim in line 30 that they "theoretically ground" their findings, but this is not the case as there is no theoretical results presented. In general, the authors don't do a good job justifying that the two pitfalls are real. Their argument is hand-wavy and is based on a series of indirect comparisons to the baseline (DrQ). Unfortunately, there are too many moving parts in those comparisons that make it virtually impossible to validate the claim (see also the point above about the hyper-parameter discrepancies).

Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to mention the prior work that is almost identical, and the inconclusive empirical study I recommend to reject this paper from a high-profile venue of NeurIPS.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper tackles the instability that arises when naively applying image augmentations in off-policy deep Q-learning, specifically in the DrQ framework. The authors pinpoint two key pitfalls: (1) high-variance, non-deterministic Q-targets when augmenting bootstrap successor states, and (2) over-regularization when training exclusively on augmented observations. To mitigate these issues, they propose a simple recipe—mixing augmented and unaugmented inputs in the critic’s Q-loss and halting augmentation of the actor’s inputs—and validate this approach on a suite of DeepMind Control Suite tasks. The method requires no extra network parameters or additional forward passes and shows improved generalization and stability compared to DrQ baselines.

Strengths:
• Relevance and Clarity: The paper addresses an important, practical problem in vision-based RL—how to harness strong data augmentations without destabilizing training. The writing is clear, the problem statement is well motivated (echoing the lucid motivation seen in recent work on SVEA), and the method is easy to follow.  
• Empirical Breadth: The authors conduct extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks (standard and generalization variants of DMControl), various augmentation schemes, and encoder backbones (ConvNets and Vision Transformers), demonstrating consistent gains in held-out performance and sample efficiency.  
• Low Overhead: As noted in several alternative reviews, the proposed adjustments introduce negligible computational cost over standard DrQ, requiring no additional network branches or expensive auxiliary losses.  

Weaknesses:
• Incremental Novelty and Prior Work: The core trick—combining one augmented with one unaugmented observation for target estimation—closely mirrors a variance-reduction approach hinted at in the original DrQ paper, where targets were averaged over two augmentations. The authors do not cite or compare against that prior result, which undermines claims of novelty.  
• Baseline Discrepancies and Hyperparameters: It remains unclear whether baseline DrQ runs use identical architectures and hyperparameters. For instance, SVEA and competing methods often employ deeper encoders (11 conv layers vs. 4 in DrQ), larger replay buffers (500K vs. 100K), and effectively doubled batch sizes—factors known to influence performance on DMControl. Without controlling for these variables, the empirical improvements may be conflated with architectural or tuning advantages rather than the proposed data-mixing strategy.  
• Lack of Component-wise and Hyperparameter Ablations: While alternative reviews praise the broad empirical sweep, no ablation disentangles the contributions of (i) stopping augmentation for the actor, (ii) mixing losses, and (iii) avoiding successor augmentation. Similarly, sensitivity to the mixing coefficients (α, β) is not explored, leaving practitioners without guidance on hyperparameter selection.  
• Missing Theoretical Grounding: The manuscript claims to “theoretically ground” its findings, yet offers no formal analysis of variance reduction, bias–variance trade-offs, or convergence guarantees. A concise bound or more rigorous insight into why mixing unaugmented data reduces target variance would strengthen the technical contribution.  
• Limited Scope and Impact Discussion: The evaluation is confined to SAC-based off-policy Q-learning in simulated environments. There is no assessment on other algorithms (e.g., TD3, DQN variants) or on-policy methods. Moreover, while the authors briefly mention compute and ecological costs, they do not provide wall-clock time, FLOPs, or GPU-hour comparisons to substantiate claims of efficiency gains. Finally, a more nuanced discussion of real-world deployment risks (e.g., sim-to-real transfer, safety in robotics) and potential biases introduced by synthetic augmentations would deepen the societal impact section.

Recommendations for Revision:
1. Prior Work and Novelty: Explicitly discuss and compare to the original DrQ variance-reduction technique and other related augmentation strategies.  
2. Controlled Baselines: Re-run DrQ baselines with identical encoder depths, replay buffer sizes, and batch sizes to isolate the effect of the proposed method.  
3. Ablations and Hyperparameter Sweeps: Include component-wise ablations and sensitivity analyses for mixing weights α and β to clarify which elements drive performance gains.  
4. Theoretical Insight: Provide at least a sketch of variance reduction analysis or convergence properties to substantiate the “theoretical grounding” claim.  
5. Computational Accounting: Report wall-clock times, compute budgets, and GPU usage to support efficiency claims.  
6. Broader Evaluation and Impact: Evaluate on additional off-policy algorithms or on-policy methods, and expand the societal impact discussion to cover real-world risks and eco-cost quantification.

Decision:
While the method is practically motivated and the empirical results are promising, the paper’s novelty is limited by close overlap with prior work, and the experimental evaluation suffers from uncontrolled baselines and missing ablations. Given these concerns, I recommend rejection at this stage. Rating: 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection.

title: On the Value of Infinite Gradients in Variational Autoencoder Models

reviews.summary: The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: not applicable

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper provides a rigorous theoretical and empirical investigation of unbounded (“infinite”) gradients that arise in variational autoencoders (VAEs) when seeking perfect data reconstruction with parsimonious latent codes. Building on a clear problem statement, the authors prove (Theorem 4) that any autoencoder‐based energy guaranteeing both zero reconstruction error and maximally sparse latent representations must exhibit unbounded gradients near its optima. They further show that learning the decoder variance parameter γ smooths the training objective—mitigating large‐gradient instabilities early in optimization—while still permitting infinite gradients at convergence. Illustrative toy examples and experiments on CelebA support the theoretical findings.

Strengths:  
- The paper offers a novel theoretical perspective by formalizing why infinite parameter gradients are not merely pathological but necessary for achieving optimal sparse encodings on data concentrated near a low‐dimensional manifold. This formalization extends prior VAE analyses and fills a clear gap in our understanding of gradient behavior.  
- Theorem 4 and its associated counterexample are stated and proved with commendable rigor under the current manifold and capacity assumptions. Supplementary toy examples and 1D loss‐slice visualizations make these abstract results more tangible.  
- The empirical study—particularly the comparison of fixed vs. learned γ on CelebA—demonstrates that models with a learnable decoder variance achieve comparable reconstruction errors while yielding better latent regularization (e.g., lower MMD to the prior). These results align well with the theoretical smoothing argument.  
- Overall organization and clarity are solid: the paper progresses logically from motivation to formal definitions, main impossibility result, and the practical γ‐dependent smoothing discussion. Figures are generally helpful in conveying core ideas.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Narrow Assumptions and Scope  
   - The analysis hinges on idealized conditions: exact low‐dimensional manifold structure, perfect noise‐free reconstructions, and arbitrarily flexible decoder networks. As noted in Alternative Reviews, these strong assumptions may limit applicability to real‐world data, which often exhibit noise, manifold heterogeneity, or finite network capacity. A more explicit discussion of how approximate reconstruction or noisy data might affect the necessity of infinite gradients would strengthen the paper.  
   - It remains unclear whether the infinite‐gradient phenomenon generalizes beyond VAEs with Gaussian decoders. For instance, do kernel PCA, GMMs, Bayesian regression, or PCA with interpolation constraints exhibit similar behavior? Adding a brief comparison or commentary on these other models would place the results in a broader context.  

2. Relation to Bayesian Perspectives and Prior Work  
   - The optimization of γ is framed informally in Bayesian terms, yet the manuscript lacks a full Bayesian discussion. Integrating perspectives on how a proper prior on parameters might regularize or avert infinite gradients (e.g., heavy‐tailed priors, SCAD/MCP‐style penalties) could offer further intuition and potential mitigation strategies.  
   - The work cites Dai & Wipf (ICLR 2018) but does not sufficiently discuss how the new theoretical results relate to or extend their diagnostics and enhancements of VAE models. A dedicated paragraph contrasting the current impossibility proof with their findings would clarify contributions.  

3. Empirical Validation and Practical Guidance  
   - While the CelebA experiments are suggestive, they are relatively narrow in scope. Additional evaluations on diverse datasets (e.g., Fashion MNIST) or ablations over network architectures and manifold dimensions would bolster confidence in the results. Comparisons to other gradient‐smoothing or clipping strategies could also illuminate how learning γ compares to existing techniques.  
   - The paper recommends tuning γ to achieve the interpolation property but provides only general advice. Introducing specific algorithms or protocols for selecting γ—perhaps via cross‐validation or Bayesian estimation—would increase practical impact. Similarly, exploring whether adaptive modelling of σ_z (rather than holding it constant) can compensate for instability merits discussion or preliminary experiments.  

4. Clarity and Presentation  
   - Some formulas and notation remain confusing. For example, in Equation (6), it is unclear why the encoder’s density term uses a function h(·)—a more detailed explanation or alternative criterion for autoencoder learning might help readers.  
   - Theorems and dense proofs in Section 3 could be prefaced with concise, intuitive summaries or simple illustrative examples to aid non‐specialist readers. Figures (e.g., Figure 3) would benefit from clearer axis labels and legends.  
   - Minor typographical issues should be addressed: Line 103 (“maximal”→“minimal”), Line 325 (“spare”→“sparse”), and removal of formatting artifacts (“(cid:13)”, undeclared footnotes).  

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
Although the work is primarily theoretical, real‐world generative modeling frequently departs from the ideal conditions assumed here. A dedicated limitations section could discuss:  
- The impact of finite sample effects and finite network capacity on sparse representation guarantees.  
- Extensions to non‐Gaussian likelihoods or discrete data, where gradient behavior may differ.  
- Potential ethical implications: while robustly reconstructive VAEs can aid anomaly detection in healthcare, they may also facilitate misuse in deepfake generation or surveillance applications. A brief discussion of mitigation strategies and ethical safeguards would be valuable.

Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.
reviews.main_review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper investigates a well‐known but underexplored phenomenon in variational autoencoders (VAEs): when the decoder variance γ is treated as a learnable parameter, it tends to collapse toward zero, yielding unbounded (“infinite”) gradients. Contrary to viewing this as a pathology, the authors prove (Theorem 4) that such unbounded gradients are in fact necessary to achieve optimal sparse latent representations—that is, zero reconstruction error with the fewest active dimensions—on data concentrated near a low‐dimensional manifold. They further show that learning γ provides an implicit smoothing of the loss early in training, mitigating optimization instabilities while still permitting infinite gradients near the optimum. Empirical results on toy examples and CelebA demonstrate that VAEs with learnable γ achieve comparable reconstruction error but improved latent regularization (e.g., lower MMD) compared to models with fixed small γ.

Strengths:  
1. Originality and Theory: The paper delivers a novel theoretical perspective, formally connecting infinite parameter gradients to provably parsimonious representations. This reframing—viewing gradient divergence as a necessary feature rather than a defect—fills a clear gap in VAE theory. Theorem 4’s generality is particularly compelling, as are the intuitive toy counterexamples that illustrate why no bounded‐gradient energy can guarantee both perfect reconstruction and maximal sparsity.  
2. Practical Insights: By showing how learning γ dynamically smooths the objective and concentrates large gradients only near minima, the authors provide actionable guidance for VAE practitioners. This helps explain why heuristic fixes (e.g., arbitrarily small fixed variance) may under‐regularize the model or hamper sample quality.  
3. Empirical Support: Although conducted on a limited set of tasks (toy manifolds and CelebA), the experiments effectively showcase the γ‐dependent smoothing effect and its impact on latent sparsity and sample metrics (e.g., MMD to the prior). The inclusion of 1D loss‐slice visualizations and quantitative CelebA results strengthens the theoretical claims.  
4. Significance: These findings have immediate implications for designing stable and expressive generative models, especially when data lie on or near low‐dimensional manifolds. The work advances both theoretical understanding and practical recommendations for VAE training and anomaly/OOD detection.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. Idealized Assumptions: The analysis hinges on strong assumptions—exact manifold structure, perfect reconstructibility, unlimited decoder capacity, and Gaussian decoders. It remains unclear how robust the infinite‐gradient necessity is under noisy data, approximate manifolds, finite‐capacity networks, or non‐Gaussian likelihoods. A dedicated discussion (or preliminary experiments) on extensions to non‐Gaussian/ discrete data and finite‐sample effects would fortify the paper’s practical relevance.  
2. Limited Empirical Scope: Beyond CelebA, additional benchmarks (e.g., Fashion MNIST as in one alternative review) or comparisons across architectures would help assess generality. Moreover, contrasting with other bounded‐gradient regularizers (such as SCAD or MCP penalties) could clarify the unique benefits of learnable γ.  
3. Initialization and Dynamics: The reviewer shares the main review’s query on γ initialization: does starting γ at different values (e.g., its convergence point) alter the training dynamics or the emergence of infinite gradients? Empirical or theoretical insights on this point (perhaps extending Figure 4b) would deepen understanding.  
4. Sampling Quality Discussion: As noted in the main review, since learnable γ converges to very small values, VAE samples may remain under‐regularized. A brief discussion comparing direct VAE sampling versus two‐stage generative pipelines (e.g., fitting a separate model on the aggregated posterior) would clarify practical sample‐quality trade‐offs.  
5. OOD Detection Baseline: The paper shows that learned γ and fixed tiny γ yield similar reconstruction error; do they also deliver similar OOD detection performance? Including this comparison would address the main review’s third question and further validate the utility of learnable γ in anomaly detection.  
6. Clarity and Presentation: Some mathematical sections (especially Theorem 4’s proof) are densely presented. A concise in‐text summary of key technical lemmas and clearer cross‐references would help non‐specialist readers. Additionally, the manuscript contains minor formatting artifacts (e.g., undeclared footnote markers, stray symbols) and figures with occasionally unclear axis labels. Cleaning up these issues will improve overall readability.  
7. Training Details and Stability: Details on hyperparameters, optimizers, gradient clipping (if used), and convergence criteria are sparse. Explicitly listing these settings is crucial for reproducibility. Moreover, since large gradients can stress optimization, practical guidelines (e.g., adaptive step‐size heuristics) could be briefly outlined.  
8. Societal Impact and Ethical Considerations: While theoretical, the paper’s insights into more powerful generative modeling and anomaly detection warrant a short reflection on potential misuse (e.g., deepfakes or surveillance applications) and suggestions for ethical safeguards.

Decision:  
Marginally above the acceptance threshold. Rating: 6 (Weak Accept). The paper offers important theoretical contributions and practical insights for VAE training, but would benefit from broader empirical validation, deeper discussion of assumptions, and clearer presentation.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.

Main Review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper revisits the well-known issue of exploding gradients in Gaussian‐likelihood (V)AEs with learnable variance and reframes these “unbounded” gradients not as a pathology but as a necessary feature for learning lossless, optimally sparse representations on data concentrated near a low-dimensional manifold. Building on prior observations of variances collapsing to zero, the authors (1) formally define “optimal sparse representations” as those achieving zero reconstruction error with minimal latent dimensions, (2) prove via an impossibility result (Theorem 4) that any autoencoder objective guaranteeing such representations must admit unbounded gradients around the optimum, and (3) propose learning the decoder variance γ to smooth the loss landscape early in training, thereby mitigating optimization issues while still permitting infinite gradients near minimum.

Strengths:  
– Novel theoretical insight: The impossibility result rigorously shows that bounded‐gradient objectives cannot, in the worst case, guarantee both lossless reconstruction and maximal parsimony, thus reframing exploding gradients as an intrinsic regularization mechanism.  
– Rigorous analysis: Under clear manifold‐and‐capacity assumptions, the proofs (e.g., Theorem 4) and toy counterexamples are sound and extend previous VAE pathology discussions.  
– Practical guidance: Demonstrating how a trainable decoder variance γ dynamically smooths the objective offers an actionable prescription for practitioners struggling with gradient instabilities.  
– Well‐structured narrative: The paper logically moves from motivation through formal definitions to main theorems and empirical illustrations, aided by intuitive figures that illustrate the γ-dependent smoothing effect.

Weaknesses and Concerns:  
1. Scope of applicability: Most claims are specific to Gaussian‐likelihood decoders with fixed priors. As noted in the literature on Bernoulli likelihoods or models using aggregate posteriors, likelihoods without unbounded support do not exhibit the same pathology. The paper would benefit from explicitly stating these modelling assumptions upfront and discussing how—and if—the theory extends to non-Gaussian or discrete likelihoods, or to architectures with aggregate posterior priors.  
2. Definition of “optimal sparse representations”: The current lossless‐compression framing overlooks settings where lossy representations are desired (e.g., noisy or task-irrelevant variations). A discussion of rate‐distortion theory and the Information Bottleneck (e.g. Tishby et al. 2000; Alemi et al. 2017/2018; Achille et al. 2018) would help qualify the definition and clarify when zero reconstruction error is—and is not—appropriate.  
3. Empirical evaluation: Experiments are limited to a single CelebA study (fixed vs. learned γ) and a toy manifold. Broader empirical support is needed: multiple datasets (e.g. Fashion-MNIST), ablations on network architectures, repetitions to assess statistical significance, and comparisons to alternative strategies for handling large gradients (e.g. bounded‐gradient regularizers like SCAD, MCP, or gradient clipping).  
4. Assumptions vs. real data: Theorems assume perfect reconstructibility on exact manifolds and infinite decoder capacity. Real data often deviate from these ideals. A paragraph discussing finite-sample effects, approximate manifold structures, and the impact of realistic architectural constraints would strengthen the practical relevance.  
5. Clarity and presentation:  
   • Some proofs and definitions are dense; including a brief, intuitive example alongside Theorem 4 in the main text would aid comprehension.  
   • Notational clutter and formatting artifacts (e.g. undeclared footnotes, encoding placeholders) should be cleaned. Figure labels and legends (e.g. axes in Fig. 3) need clearer descriptions.  
6. Limitations and societal impact: While the theoretical focus limits direct ethical concerns, powerful generative models can be misused (e.g. deepfakes, surveillance). A concise discussion of potential misuse and safeguards, as well as a dedicated limitations paragraph reiterating the paper’s modelling constraints, is recommended.

Detailed Suggestions:  
– Explicitly qualify in the Introduction that all main results assume Gaussian decoder likelihoods with trainable variance and fixed priors; discuss extensions or failures for other likelihoods or aggregate posterior schemes.  
– In Section 2, temper Definition 1 by noting that optimal sparse representations for noisy or task-driven settings often permit non‐zero reconstruction error; cite rate-distortion and Info Bottleneck works.  
– Add a short subsection listing alternative modeling choices (e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods, normalizing flows, adaptive or aggregated priors) and when they may be preferred.  
– Expand Experiments: include additional benchmarks, report multiple runs with error bars, measure latent sparsity quantitatively (e.g. number of inactive dimensions), and compare against gradient‐clipping or alternative smoothing techniques.  
– Streamline notation: provide a table of symbols; integrate key proof sketches or intuitive remarks into the main text.  
– Add a dedicated “Limitations and Societal Impact” section addressing modelling assumptions, computational stability in large-scale settings, and ethical considerations.

Verdict: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
Overall, this work offers a valuable theoretical reframing of a classic VAE pathology and practical advice for smoother optimization. While certain claims rest on specific assumptions and empirical validation is currently narrow, the core insights are sound and of interest to both theoreticians and practitioners. Addressing the above concerns in a revision will substantially enhance clarity, scope, and impact.
