2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.

Main Review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.main_review:
===== After Rebuttal =====

Please refer to my "After Rebuttal" post.

=====================

~~This is clearly not a NeurIPS level submission. I know it is quite frustrating to receive a rejection, so I have patiently read this manuscript until page 5, trying my best to prepare as much feedback as possible to help the author(s) improve this work, then I gave up. Its contents are simply not technically sound. I scrolled down to the Experiment section and found out it is not adequate as well, so here I am writing this review. The language issues in writing (sentences too long, grammar issues, bad expressions, etc.) I can tolerate them, but there are factual errors in this paper, so my evaluation is a firm no. The author(s) have some fundamental misunderstanding of variational inference models, as reflected in the definitions, analysis, and examples presented in this work. This work will be of limited value to the community. See more detailed comments in the limitation section below.~~
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
- Infinite gradients or overfitting? It turned out the infinite gradient issue the author(s) heavily focused on is caused by letting the precision parameter of the Gaussian noise model be trainable, which always explodes to infinity. Well, it is well known this is simply the overfitting in the finite sample scenario, and of course, it will drive the gradient to infinity. 
- The PCA example is inconsistent with the optimal sparse representation definition, as its latent representation has no uncertainty at all. 
- Reconstruction error is just an intuitive explanation of likelihood loss under the Gaussian observation model. For a proper theory of variational inference, the concepts should be instead defined by conditional likelihoods. 
- Please use vector formats instead of bitmaps in the Figures.
- The experiments need to be substantially enriched. 
- I do not feel the author(s) have shed real valuable insights to guide practice. What does the statement "this result suggests that heuristic modifications to or constraints on the VAE energy function may be ill-advised, and large gradients should be accommodated to the extent possible." even mean? Such confusing.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This manuscript investigates the phenomenon of unbounded (“infinite”) gradients in variational autoencoders (VAEs), aiming to explain why such gradients arise and how they relate to model regularization and sparse latent representations. Building on a formal definition of “optimal sparse representations” for data on or near a low‐dimensional manifold, the authors prove (Theorem 4) that any autoencoder‐based energy function guaranteeing both zero reconstruction error and maximally parsimonious latent codes must admit unbounded gradients in the neighborhood of optimal solutions. They further argue that learning the decoder variance parameter γ during training induces a form of objective smoothing—mitigating disruptive large gradients in early optimization while still permitting infinite gradients near minima. Empirical illustrations on toy manifolds and a CelebA experiment are provided to support these claims.

Strengths  
- The paper tackles an underexplored theoretical question: rather than treating gradient divergence as a pathology, it reframes unbounded gradients as an intrinsic feature required for provably sparse, lossless encodings (Theorem 4).  
- The notion of γ‐dependent smoothing offers an appealing explanation for why learned decoder variance can stabilize VAE training in practice.  
- Related literature on VAE regularization, posterior collapse, and sparse representations is generally well cited, and the core theorems are accompanied by toy counterexamples that clarify the main ideas.

Weaknesses  
1. Technical Soundness and Conceptual Clarity  
   - The focus on exploding precision (γ→0) as the root of infinite gradients overlooks that, in finite‐sample settings, γ simply overfits—driving variance to zero and trivially producing unbounded gradients. This is a well‐known effect rather than a novel phenomenon.  
   - Key definitions (e.g., reconstruction error, sparse representation) are not grounded in conditional likelihoods, which is the customary framework for variational inference. As a result, some theoretical claims rest on shaky foundations.  
   - The PCA example fails to satisfy the paper’s own definition of optimal sparse representations, since PCA’s latent codes carry no uncertainty. This inconsistency undermines confidence in the formalism.  

2. Overly Idealized Assumptions  
   - Like several recent works, the analysis assumes data lie exactly on a low‐dimensional manifold and grants the decoder unlimited capacity. The paper lacks discussion of how these strong assumptions break down under noise, finite network capacity, or approximate manifold structure.  
   - Real‐world data often deviate from Gaussian observation models; the authors do not explore extensions to non‐Gaussian or discrete likelihoods, despite this being raised in prior literature.  

3. Empirical Validation  
   - Experiments are limited to a single real‐world dataset (CelebA) and one toy manifold. There is no ablation on network architectures (e.g., convolutional vs. fully connected), no comparisons to alternative smoothing or bounded‐gradient regularizers (e.g., SCAD, MCP), and no additional datasets such as Fashion-MNIST.  
   - Details on hyperparameters, training protocols, statistical significance of improvements, and reproducibility (e.g., code or supplementary proofs) are sparse or missing.  
   - Figures use bitmap graphics with unclear axes or legends; vector formats and more informative captions would greatly aid interpretation.  

4. Writing and Presentation  
   - The manuscript contains formatting artifacts (e.g., undeclared footnotes, encoding glitches) and lengthy, grammatically awkward sentences that hinder readability.  
   - Notation is sometimes cluttered, and key technical lemmas are relegated to the supplement without concise summaries in the main text.  

5. Practical Implications and Guidance  
   - The paper’s recommendation—to “accommodate large gradients to the extent possible”—remains too vague to guide practitioners. Concrete strategies (e.g., adaptive step‐size rules, gradient clipping protocols with theoretical guarantees) are not proposed.  
   - Potential societal impacts, particularly around misuse of generative models in sensitive domains, are not addressed. A brief ethical discussion would be appropriate given the growing concerns around deepfakes and surveillance.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
- Recast core definitions in terms of conditional likelihoods to align with standard variational inference frameworks.  
- Revise the PCA example or replace it with one that truly reflects uncertainty in the latent code under the proposed definitions.  
- Expand empirical studies to additional datasets (e.g., Fashion-MNIST), architectures, and comparisons with bounded‐gradient regularization schemes. Include hyperparameter details, statistical analyses, and open‐source code.  
- Convert figures to vector formats, clarify axes and legends, and summarize key proofs in the main text to improve accessibility.  
- Discuss how the theory extends to non‐Gaussian likelihoods, discrete data, and finite‐capacity networks, and propose concrete optimization techniques for managing large gradients.  
- Add a short section on ethical considerations, noting both the positive and potentially negative applications of more powerful generative models.  

Decision  
Rating: 5 – Marginally below the acceptance threshold  
While the paper brings an interesting theoretical perspective on infinite gradients in VAEs, foundational flaws in the formulations, reliance on idealized assumptions, limited empirical validation, and presentation issues currently preclude it from meeting the standard for acceptance.

title: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

reviews.summary: This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

-129 A comment regarding the subscript in the expectation may add some more clarity, as some times this means expected value given this quantity, or expected value given everything else other than this quantity
- 132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480  Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the problem of designing the best possible interpolator in overparameterized linear regression under a Bayesian prior. Building on the notion of “best possible interpolator” from Muthukumar et al. (2019), the authors restrict attention to the class of estimators that are linear functions of the response vector (“response‐linear achievable” interpolators). In this setting they derive a closed‐form expression for the optimal member of this class, show that it arises as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with a carefully chosen initialization, and extend these results to the random features regime. To bridge theory and practice, they propose a fully empirical estimator—replacing population quantities (covariance Σ and signal‐to‐noise ratio δ) with graphical lasso and cross‐validated SNR estimates—and demonstrate in synthetic experiments that this estimator can substantially outperform common interpolators (e.g. minimum‐norm) in regimes where those suffer arbitrarily large risk gaps.

Strengths:
1. Originality and Theoretical Contribution  
   - Introducing the class of response‐linear achievable interpolators and characterizing its optimal element in closed form is novel.  
   - The connection between this optimal interpolator and the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent (cf. Gunasekar et al., 2018) offers fresh insight into how initialization affects generalization in overparameterized models.  
   - Extending the framework to random features further broadens the paper’s impact, suggesting potential applicability beyond simple linear models.

2. Quality and Rigor  
   - The theoretical derivations are sound and carried out under transparent technical assumptions. Finite‐sample formulas are complemented by asymptotic analyses using random matrix theory, convincingly showcasing regimes where the minimum‐norm and optimal‐variance interpolators perform arbitrarily worse.  
   - Empirical results on isotropic and spiked‐covariance settings align well with theory, illustrating both the risk gaps and the effectiveness of the proposed data‐driven estimator.

3. Significance and Practical Relevance  
   - By quantifying potentially unbounded performance differences among interpolators, the paper advances our understanding of implicit bias in interpolation.  
   - The empirical approximation via graphical lasso and cross‐validated δ makes the optimal interpolator tangible for practitioners, marking a step toward algorithmic designs that exploit population structure.

4. Clarity and Presentation  
   - Overall the manuscript is well organized, with clear definitions, numbered propositions, and a logical flow from setup to main results and experiments.  
   - Figures effectively illustrate key phenomena, and the supplement contains thorough proofs and experimental details.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Restriction to Isotropic Regime  
   - The fully empirical estimator is validated primarily in isotropic or spike‐model regimes. Extending experiments (or theory) to more general, structured covariances would strengthen the practical claim.  

2. Lack of Theoretical Guarantees for Empirical Approximation  
   - As acknowledged by the authors, there are no nonasymptotic bounds on the error of the graphical lasso estimator for Σ nor on the impact of SNR estimation error. Providing (even rough) theoretical guarantees or sensitivity analyses for these approximations would add rigor.  
   - In particular, a precise bound on how covariance estimation error translates to generalization error of the empirical interpolator would directly address point (c) in the main review.

3. Generalization Error Bounds  
   - Beyond empirical validation, a theoretical result bounding the generalization error of the data‐driven estimator relative to the optimal response‐linear estimator would elevate the contribution.  

4. Clarity in Proofs and Notation  
   - Some early steps in the proof of Proposition 1 (e.g. around lines 64–129) could be clarified—adding a brief explanatory sketch in the main text may help.  
   - The dense notation involving Σ^{−1/2}, P, and various transforms could be summarized in a notation table or accompanied by a running example. Including pseudocode for the empirical algorithm would also improve reproducibility.

5. Experimental Details and Reproducibility  
   - Reporting error bars or confidence intervals in figures would better convey the variability of the empirical estimator.  
   - Discussing the computational complexity of repeated graphical lasso fits and cross‐validation in high dimensions would inform readers about scalability considerations.  
   - A comment on the choice of regularization parameter for graphical lasso and its impact on performance would be valuable.

6. Minor and Presentation Points  
   - Clarify the meaning of subscripts in expectations (line 129) to avoid confusion between conditional and marginal expectations.  
   - In equation (17), consider starting with a general W before specifying W₀ to improve exposition.  
   - Correct small typographical issues (e.g., “Mourtada” interpolation reference) for accuracy.

Decision and Rating:
I recommend acceptance with a marginally above threshold rating of 6. The paper offers a novel theoretical framework and practical algorithm for optimal interpolation in linear models, backed by rigorous analysis and compelling examples. Addressing the above points—particularly theoretical guarantees for the empirical approximation, enhanced clarity in proofs, and broader experiments—would substantially strengthen the work and its impact.

They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
In line:
-64  I don't think that Mourtada deals with interpolators in this paper.
-129 A comment regarding the subscript in the expectation may add some more clarity, as some times this means expected value given this quantity, or expected value given everything else other than this quantity
- 132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480  Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolator in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.
reviews.main_review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates optimal interpolation strategies in overparameterized linear regression under a Bayesian prior on the true parameter. Focusing on the class of “response-linear achievable” estimators—those linear in the observed responses and depending on the data covariance, signal-to-noise ratio, and prior covariance but not on the unknown signal or noise realizations—the authors derive a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator with respect to expected excess risk. They show this optimum arises as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with a specific initialization and identify regimes where both the minimum-norm and variance-minimizing interpolators perform arbitrarily worse. An empirical algorithm based on Graphical Lasso and cross-validated SNR approximates the optimal interpolator from data, and the framework is further extended to random features regression.

Strengths:
1. Novelty and Significance: The response-linear achievable framework and its closed-form optimal solution represent a fresh contribution to the study of benign overfitting. By linking statistical optimality to the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent, the paper extends our understanding of how initialization and preconditioning critically shape generalization in overparameterized models. The random features extension suggests broader applicability beyond linear regression.
2. Theoretical Rigor: The paper provides precise finite-sample expressions and asymptotic analyses under clear technical assumptions. Propositions are supported by detailed proofs in the supplement, and the asymptotic examples (e.g., isotropic and spike models) convincingly demonstrate unbounded suboptimality of standard interpolators.
3. Practical Relevance: Introducing an empirical procedure—estimating the population covariance via Graphical Lasso and the SNR by cross-validation—and demonstrating its near-optimal performance makes the theory actionable for practitioners.
4. Clarity of Exposition: The manuscript is generally well organized, with a logical progression from problem setup to main results and experiments.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:

1. Comparison to Prior Work:
 a. Relation to Amari et al. (2021): The paper cites Amari et al. in studying asymptotic optimality of bias or variance, but it would benefit readers to clarify how the present techniques and setting differ. Can bias–variance balancing methods from that work be adapted to this framework, or is the response-linear restriction crucial?
 b. Context with Bartlett et al. (2020): The claim that minimum-norm interpolant may not be close to optimal has been partially addressed by Bartlett et al. (e.g., isotropic covariances where OLS is inconsistent). A more nuanced discussion contrasting these scenarios would strengthen the narrative.

2. Assumptions on Distributions:
 a. Gaussianity of Noise and Prior: Both the noise ξ and w* are assumed Gaussian with zero mean. Can these assumptions be relaxed—say to heavier-tailed or nonzero-mean priors? In particular, does Proposition 1 extend to w* with nonzero mean, and if so, how do the optimal formulas change?
 b. Impact of Model Misspecification: Empirical robustness to deviations from the Bayesian prior (e.g., covariance estimation errors) merits discussion, especially in light of the practical approximation.

3. Empirical Approximation and Experiments:
 a. Structure and Validation of Σ_e: In the experiments of Section 4.1, what is the actual structure of the population covariance? Is it isotropic, heavy-tailed, or spike-like? Reporting whether Σ_e approximates Σ closely—via plots or quantitative metrics—would bolster confidence.
 b. Error Bars and Regimes: The synthetic experiments could include error bars or confidence intervals to reflect variability over trials. Exploring additional regimes beyond isotropic settings—such as heavy-tailed spectra or structured covariances—would demonstrate robustness.
 c. Computational Complexity: Graphical Lasso in high dimensions can be costly. A brief discussion of the algorithm’s scalability and potential alternatives (e.g., thresholded covariance estimators) would help practitioners assess feasibility.

4. Presentation and Reproducibility:
 a. Proof Sketches: Including brief sketches of the proofs for Propositions 1–3 in the main text would aid reader intuition, complementing the full derivations in the supplement.
 b. Notation Summary: The repeated use of operators such as Σ^{-1/2}, P, and preconditioning transformations is dense. A notation table or schematic overview of key matrices and transformations would improve accessibility.
 c. Pseudocode: Providing pseudocode for the empirical interpolation algorithm (Graphical Lasso + cross-validation) would enhance reproducibility, alongside the detailed supplement.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors acknowledge limitations in the theoretical guarantees for Graphical Lasso under general covariance structures. It would be beneficial to discuss how covariance estimation error propagates to the risk of the empirical interpolator and whether nonasymptotic bounds can be derived. As with any interpolation technique, naive application in sensitive domains could exacerbate biases present in the data; thus, future extensions might consider integrating fairness or robustness constraints. Overall, direct negative societal impacts are limited given the theoretical focus, but guidance on responsible use in practice would be valuable.

Decision: I continue to recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper, accept). This work addresses an important question in overparameterized learning, offers novel theoretical insights, and proposes a practical method for achieving optimal interpolation, making it a strong contribution to the literature.

reviews.summary: The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.

reviews.text:
Summary:

This paper addresses the problem of optimal interpolation in overparameterized linear regression by introducing the class of response‐linear achievable interpolators—estimators that are linear in the observed responses and may depend on the population covariance Σ, signal‐to‐noise ratio δ, and prior covariance but not on the unknown signal or noise realizations. The authors derive a closed‐form expression for the optimal such interpolator, prove that it emerges as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with a specific initialization (Proposition 2), and identify regimes—via random matrix theory—where both the minimum‐norm and variance‐minimizing interpolators can generalize arbitrarily worse. To demonstrate practical relevance, they propose a data‐driven approximation using Graphical Lasso to estimate Σ and cross‐validated SNR estimates, and they extend the framework to random features regression.

Overall Comments:

The manuscript is very well written: definitions and propositions are clearly numbered, the narrative flows smoothly, and notation is consistent. The theoretical contributions are original and substantial. Deriving an explicit optimal response‐linear interpolator and connecting it to the bias of gradient descent preconditioned by Σ⁻¹ deepens our understanding of implicit regularization in interpolation. Extensions to random features regression and illustrative finite‐sample and asymptotic examples showcase the breadth of the approach.

However, while the proofs in the supplement are rigorous, the paper would greatly benefit from more intuition and commentary. Beyond the technical derivations, readers would appreciate insights into why the optimal interpolator takes its particular form and how its dependence on Σ, δ, and the prior covariance shapes its performance. A brief sketch of the proofs of Propositions 1–3 in the main text could illuminate the key ideas without overloading the reader. Similarly, summarizing complex operators (e.g., Σ⁻¹/², projection matrices P) in a notation table or a running example would improve accessibility.

Proposition 2 elegantly shows that preconditioned gradient descent with a tailored initialization recovers the optimal solution. Yet, empirical plots comparing this to unpreconditioned GD (as suggested in the supplement) or varying the initialization would concretely illustrate how initialization choices affect convergence and generalization. Moreover, the paper currently focuses its experiments on isotropic and simple spike‐model settings; extending empirical validation to more challenging covariance structures or reporting error bars/confidence intervals would strengthen confidence in the claims.

A central empirical component is the Graphical Lasso and cross‐validated SNR approximation. While this practical algorithm is promising, the manuscript lacks discussion of its statistical and computational properties. For example:

 • Under what sampling regimes can we expect the Graphical Lasso estimate of Σ to be sufficiently accurate?  
 • How does estimation error in Σ and δ propagate to the final interpolator’s risk?  
 • What is the computational cost of repeated covariance estimation in high dimensions, and are there scalable alternatives?  

Addressing these points—either by providing nonasymptotic bounds or by empirically studying sensitivity—would solidify the practical impact of the work.

Minor Comments:

1. The section on random matrix preliminaries may arrive too early. Consider postponing it to Section 4.2 or moving some material to the Appendix, introducing only the essentials when first needed.

2. The random features extension is interesting but currently lacks commentary. If space is tight, relocating some of these results to the Appendix and focusing the main text on linear regression insights might streamline the narrative.

3. In Section 4.2, you pose the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator. If a closed‐form expression is infeasible, please clarify the obstacles or discuss approximate characterizations.

4. Please expand on the motivation for allowing dependence on Σ, δ, and the prior covariance. Why is this dependence acceptable, and how might one relax these requirements in practical scenarios? In particular, more discussion on the Graphical Lasso approximation—why it is appropriate and what its limitations are—would be valuable.

5. Include pseudocode for the empirical algorithm (Graphical Lasso + SNR estimation) to aid reproducibility. Likewise, ensure all figures have detailed axis labels and, where possible, error bars or shaded confidence regions.

6. A brief note on potential societal impacts—e.g., how misestimation of Σ in sensitive applications could exacerbate biases—would demonstrate broader awareness, even if direct negative impacts are limited.

Conclusion:

This paper makes a meaningful contribution to our understanding of interpolation in overparameterized linear models by providing a principled, closed‐form optimal interpolator and connecting it to optimization bias. While the theoretical results are sound and the writing is clear, the manuscript would benefit from deeper intuition, more extensive empirical validation (including sensitivity to covariance estimation), and stronger guidance on practical implementation. With these improvements, the work would have broader appeal and immediate applicability. 

Recommendation: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.
reviews.main_review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.

Main Review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please see the main review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper studies the design of optimal interpolators in overparameterized linear regression within the class of “response-linear achievable” estimators—i.e., those linear in the observed responses and permitted to depend on the population covariance Σ, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and a prior covariance but not on the unknown signal or noise realizations. The authors derive a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class, show it arises as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with a specific initialization, and extend the framework to random features regression. They further propose a practical algorithm that approximates the optimal interpolator using only training data via Graphical Lasso and cross-validated SNR estimates.

Strengths:  
- Originality: As highlighted in several accept reviews, the notion of response-linear achievable interpolators and its exact closed-form optimal solution represent a novel contribution to the theory of interpolation in linear models.  
- Connection to optimization bias: The paper deepens understanding of implicit bias by showing how preconditioned gradient descent with tailored initialization exactly recovers the optimal interpolator.  
- Extension to random features: Generalizing the framework beyond classical linear regression to random features models broadens the potential impact of the work.  
- Empirical approximation: Proposing a data-driven estimator via Graphical Lasso and SNR cross-validation is a practical step toward implementation without prior population knowledge.

Major Comments:  
1. Unrealistic knowledge assumptions:  
   The paper assumes that Σ, the SNR, and the prior covariance are known—an assumption that is arguably stronger and less realistic than knowing the true regression parameter or noise realizations, particularly in high dimensions. Estimating Σ alone entails O(d²) parameters, which can far exceed the number of coefficients. In a Bayesian perspective, assuming full knowledge of the parameter distribution effectively sidesteps the core estimation challenge.  

2. Practical approximation and theoretical guarantees:  
   While the Graphical Lasso plus cross-validation scheme provides an empirical route to approximating Σ and SNR, the paper currently lacks non-asymptotic guarantees on the estimation error of these quantities under general covariance structures. As several reviewers noted, without bounds on how inaccuracies in Σ̂ or SNR̂ propagate to the final interpolator, it is difficult to assess the reliability and robustness of the empirical algorithm.  

3. Computational complexity and scalability:  
   Relatedly, the reliance on Graphical Lasso in high dimensions may become computationally prohibitive. The authors should discuss the scalability of their empirical scheme, perhaps propose alternatives or approximate solvers, and provide runtime or complexity analysis.  

4. Clarity of risk definition:  
   The definition of population risk around lines 125–126 is unclear: expressing it as a conditional expectation given X, ξ, w* makes the risk appear random. A risk measure should be deterministic or clearly defined as an expectation over all relevant randomness.  

5. Notation and typos:  
   The manuscript suffers from inconsistent and sometimes confusing notation. For example, the use of y versus y_i in several lines (117, 120, 124, 131–132) should be standardized. Definition 4’s function f is said to “exist,” but it is unclear whether f is known or depends on unknowns. These issues, combined with missing parentheses in equation (5) and stray typographical errors (e.g., Hastie et al. (2019) likely did not assume Φ = I_d), hamper readability. A notation table or a running example illustrating key operators (such as Σ^−1/2 and the projection P) would greatly aid the reader.  

6. Technical conditions and assumptions:  
   - Line 142: The condition γ ≥ 1 appears necessary for certain propositions but is not stated.  
   - Lines 156 versus 160: The paper initially claims “rank(X)=n with probability 1” but only later treats it as a condition. These consistency issues should be resolved.  
   - Definition 4 and the term “achievable” should specify whether the mapping from (X, y) to w is known a priori and does not depend on w* or ξ.  

7. Experimental details and reproducibility:  
   Although synthetic experiments in isotropic and spike-model settings validate the theory, axis labels on figures lack units or scales, and no error bars or confidence intervals are shown. Providing pseudocode for the empirical algorithm, reporting variability across trials, and including a sketch of main proofs in the text (as suggested by some reviewers) would enhance reproducibility and reader intuition.  

Minor Issues:  
- Line 53: change “are best” to “are the best.”  
- Include parentheses in equation (5) for clarity.  
- Confirm whether Φ denotes a correlation matrix, since referring to r² as “signal” suggests otherwise.  

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The authors candidly acknowledge that estimating Σ and SNR without guarantees is challenging. To strengthen the work, they should either derive theoretical bounds on the estimation error of the empirical interpolator under realistic sampling regimes or discuss the sensitivity of their method to regularization and cross-validation choices. While direct societal risks are limited in this theoretical setting, applying these methods in sensitive domains (e.g., biased prediction tasks) could exacerbate unfairness if the covariance estimates or prior assumptions are misaligned with the true data distribution. A brief discussion on robustness to model mis-specification and fairness considerations would be valuable.

Recommendation:  
Rating 4: Ok but not good enough—rejection.  
Despite the paper’s originality and theoretical contributions, the unrealistic assumptions about known population quantities, the lack of guarantees for the empirical approximation, and significant clarity and reproducibility issues preclude acceptance in its current form.

title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel differentiable rendering framework by reformulating the non-differentiable rasterization and depth‐aggregation steps as linear programs and then applying randomized smoothing via perturbed optimizers. By injecting carefully chosen noise distributions and leveraging Monte Carlo sampling with a control-variates variance-reduction technique, the method yields smooth rendering operators whose gradients are informative and nonzero everywhere. The authors further introduce an adaptive smoothing scheme driven by sensitivity analysis, automatically tuning the noise intensity. The perturbed renderer unifies several existing smooth approximations—most notably SoftRas—under a common theoretical umbrella, and empirical results on single-view 6D pose estimation and self-supervised 3D mesh reconstruction demonstrate improvements over state-of-the-art baselines.

Strengths  
1. Differentiable Reformulation and Theoretical Novelty  
   – The casting of rasterization and z-buffer aggregation as small linear‐programming argmax problems is a fresh perspective that connects differentiable rendering to recent work on perturbed optimizers and randomized smoothing.  
   – This principled approach subsumes SoftRas as a special case (by selecting a particular noise prior) and provides a unified theoretical justification for a family of smooth renderers.  

2. Variance Reduction and Adaptivity  
   – Introducing a control-variates estimator significantly reduces the Monte Carlo sample complexity needed for accurate gradient estimation, as evidenced by the ablation studies (e.g., Figure 5).  
   – The adaptive smoothing mechanism, driven by sensitivity analysis, offers a practical way to adjust noise intensity on the fly, reducing manual hyperparameter tuning.  

3. Empirical Performance  
   – On the 6D pose estimation benchmark, the perturbed renderer outperforms SoftRas and matches or exceeds other baselines (DIB-R, N3MR) in rotation and translation error.  
   – In self-supervised ShapeNet mesh reconstruction, the method shows consistent gains in reconstruction error and solver success rate, validating its robustness in inverse‐graphics tasks.  

4. Broader Impact and Open Science  
   – The authors open-source their implementation, which should facilitate adoption in vision, robotics, and graphics research.  
   – By clarifying the role of noise distributions, this work may inspire further integration of optimization theory into differentiable programming frameworks.  

Limitations and Areas for Improvement  
1. Clarity and Self-Contained Presentation  
   – The current manuscript assumes familiarity with [4] for the perturbed-optimizer framework, making several derivations hard to follow without that background. Key equations (e.g., lines 66, 76, 88 in the draft) lack precise definitions of indices, vectors, and notation (e.g., what exactly is “z,” how is ⟨z,y⟩ computed, and what is “v” in Eq. 3?).  
   – I recommend adding a concise table of symbols, a short pseudocode block summarizing the perturbed rendering pipeline, and more intuitive explanations in sections 3–4 to make the paper fully self-contained.  

2. Analysis of Noise Distributions  
   – While Table 1 suggests Gaussian noise often yields the best trade-off, the paper stops short of analyzing when and why one noise prior is preferable over another.  
   – A brief theoretical or empirical study—perhaps plotting gradient bias and variance across Gaussian, Cauchy, and uniform noise—would strengthen the claim that the framework can generalize to a broad class of perturbations.  

3. Experimental Comparisons and Reproducibility  
   – The paper currently reports scores “extracted from original publications” for competing renderers, which can introduce inconsistencies due to differing lighting setups, camera parameters, or dataset splits.  
   – To ensure a fair comparison, I strongly encourage the authors to re-run SoftRas, DIB-R, and N3MR under the same experimental configuration, or at minimum provide an analysis of how varying rendering settings might affect reported metrics.  

4. Computational Cost and Scalability  
   – Introducing Monte Carlo sampling and evaluating all triangles per pixel raises concerns about runtime, memory footprint, and scalability to high-resolution or dynamic scenes.  
   – A quantitative analysis comparing rendering speed and GPU memory consumption across SoftRas, DIB-R, and the perturbed renderer—especially as scene complexity or sample count grows—would help practitioners assess feasibility.  

5. Evaluation on Real-World Data  
   – All experiments are conducted on synthetic objects (ShapeNet) or controlled pose estimation tasks. It remains unclear how the method performs under real-image conditions with complex lighting, textures, and occlusions.  
   – Including an additional experiment on a real-world dataset (e.g., LINEMOD for pose estimation or real photographs for mesh reconstruction) would greatly bolster the paper’s practical relevance.  

6. Societal Impact and Ethical Considerations  
   – Improved differentiable rendering can advance applications in AR/VR and robotics, but it also raises potential risks (e.g., privacy-invasive reconstruction from surveillance footage).  
   – I recommend adding a brief discussion of ethical deployment guidelines, possible misuse scenarios, and the energy/latency trade-offs inherent in Monte Carlo–based methods.  

Minor Comments  
– Fix minor typos and inconsistent subscript conventions around Equations 7–10.  
– Clarify hyperparameter settings (learning rates, smoothing schedules) in the main text rather than relegating them entirely to the appendix.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work makes a valuable contribution by unifying and extending differentiable rendering through the lens of perturbed optimizers and randomized smoothing. Its novel theoretical foundation, coupled with practical variance reduction and adaptive schemes, yields state-of-the-art performance on core inverse graphics tasks. While the paper would benefit from clearer exposition, more thorough comparisons, and expanded real-world evaluation, these issues are addressable without undermining the core contributions. I therefore recommend acceptance with a marginally above-threshold score (Rating: 6), and look forward to seeing this framework adopted and extended by the community.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
