reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents a novel open-source Python environment for active voltage control in electric power distribution networks, formulating the problem as a decentralized partially observable Markov decision process (Dec-POMDP). The authors benchmark seven continuous-action multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithms—including MADDPG, MATD3, MAPPO, and COMA—across three realistic feeder topologies (6, 22, and 38 inverter agents) and evaluate three voltage-barrier reward functions (L1, L2, and the proposed Bowl shape). Experimental results compare learned policies to traditional droop control and centralized optimal power flow (OPF), offering insights into scalability, reward shaping, and performance trade-offs.

Strengths:  
• Significance and Reproducibility: By releasing a standardized, well-documented environment with multiple network sizes and real load/PV profiles, this work bridges the power-systems and MARL communities and addresses a reproducibility gap. The inclusion of three topologies and multiple penetration levels ensures that researchers can study both mid- and large-scale distribution settings.  
• Comprehensive Benchmarking: The paper rigorously evaluates seven state-of-the-art MARL algorithms and three barrier reward shapes, providing useful performance curves (controllable rate, power loss) and baseline comparisons to droop control and OPF. These benchmarks highlight both the promise and current limitations of MARL for voltage regulation.  
• Insightful Reward-Shaping Study: The novel Bowl-shape barrier function is a noteworthy contribution; its empirical comparison to L1 and L2 barriers reveals how reward geometry affects learning stability and safety performance.  
• Open-Source Contribution: Clear API documentation, code examples, and publicly available data encourage community adoption. A quickstart guide and integration with common MARL frameworks (e.g., RLlib, Stable Baselines3) further lower the barrier for new users.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:  
1. Justification of Core Assumptions:  
   The core assumption—that multiple inverters within a region are centrally controlled with full information—differs from common operational modes, where inverters typically act on local measurements. The manuscript would benefit from a dedicated discussion comparing this centralized-region model to existing decentralized control schemes, and clarifying how realistic distribution utilities might partition control regions in practice.  

2. Practical Usability Details:  
   While the environment is open-source, additional details on user experience would strengthen adoption. For instance, will there be:  
   • A streamlined quickstart tutorial or Docker container?  
   • Standard interfaces to plug into popular MARL toolkits?  
   • Examples of how to customize feeder data, observation scopes, or communication topologies?  

3. Experimental Transparency and Robustness:  
   Alternative reviews noted that hyperparameter configurations, training durations, convergence criteria, and sensitivity analyses (e.g., to PV penetration, observation noise, or model mismatch) are under-specified. Including a summary table of key hyperparameters, training times, and ablation studies on:  
   • Observation window size and action frequency,  
   • Barrier function parameter sensitivity,  
   • Generalization to unseen load profiles or topologies,  
would substantially improve reproducibility and assess policy robustness under real-world uncertainties.

4. Clarity of Notation and Terminology:  
   To make the setting fully accessible to ML practitioners, several domain-specific terms and notations should be better defined or restructured:  
   • Define “power curtailment” (Line 92), “per unit” (Line 140), and clarify “radius” versus “radians” (Line 193).  
   • Explain when and why the distribution feeder is tree-structured—especially since the 33-bus case originally contains loops that are manually removed. A schematic or summary table of the Dec-POMDP tuple (state, action, observation, transition, reward) could aid comprehension.  
   • Clean up the notation of $\mathcal{T}$ versus $\delta$ in the state-transition probability, and clarify “at the last step” (Line 191) and the meaning of $\mathcal{R}$ (Line 184–186).  
   • Streamline Section 4’s dense equations with accompanying intuition and refer to a consolidated notation table.

5. Safety, Interpretability, and Deployment Considerations:  
   While the paper briefly mentions interpretability challenges, it stops short of addressing formal safety guarantees or real-world deployment risks. Drawing on suggestions from alternative reviews, the authors could:  
   • Incorporate safe-RL methods or Lyapunov-based constraints to ensure voltage limits under worst-case disturbances.  
   • Test policies under communication delays, sensor noise, or agent failures to evaluate resilience.  
   • Propose human-in-the-loop validation, formal policy certification, or conservative fallback mechanisms to build operator trust.  
   • Discuss potential negative societal impacts (e.g., equipment damage from policy misbehavior) and mitigation strategies.

Minor Comments:  
• Lines 194–195: “measures” → “measurements”.  
• Line 218: Specify that “loss” refers to the reward-shaping penalty, not ML training loss.  
• Consolidate the many subplot figures into fewer, clearer panels highlighting key trends.

Decision and Rating:  
Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
Overall, this paper makes a valuable contribution by introducing a shared, realistic MARL benchmark for voltage control and offering initial insights into reward shaping and algorithm scaling. However, before broad adoption, the authors should better justify their core assumptions, enhance clarity of notation, and deepen the experimental and deployment analyses. With these improvements, the work will serve as a strong foundation for future research at the intersection of power systems and multi-agent RL.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.
reviews.main_review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

Minor details:

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents an open-source multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) benchmark for active voltage control in power distribution networks. The authors formulate the problem as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP), build a simulator using realistic network topologies and public load/PV data, and evaluate seven state-of-the-art continuous‐action MARL algorithms across three network scales (6, 22, and 38 agents). A novel “bowl-shape” barrier reward is proposed alongside standard L1 and L2 penalties to encode voltage safety constraints. Experiments compare learned policies to traditional droop control and centralized optimal power flow (OPF), and explore the impact of reward shaping on controllable rate and power loss.

Strengths:
1. Benchmark Contribution: Following the main review’s emphasis, the paper fills a genuine reproducibility gap by bridging the power-systems and MARL communities with a shared, realistic environment. Alternative reviews 1 and 4 highlight that this open-source simulator—spanning three feeder topologies and seven algorithms—can catalyze future research in both fields.
2. Reward-Shaping Insight: The introduction of the bowl-shape barrier function is a meaningful advance in encoding safety constraints into reward design. As noted by alternative review 2, one of the key insights is how different barrier shapes influence algorithmic performance, guiding practitioners in shaping rewards for constrained control tasks.
3. Empirical Rigor: The evaluation covers multiple scales of network size, PV penetration levels, and seven MARL methods (e.g., MADDPG, MATD3, MAPPO). The inclusion of traditional baselines (droop control, OPF) contextualizes the results and makes clear that while MARL is competitive, it does not yet surpass OPF under all conditions, as also observed in alternative review 3.

Weaknesses:
1. Missing Code Release: As the main review correctly points out, the absence of the environment’s source code in the supplementary material severely limits reproducibility and community uptake. Alternative reviews 1 and 2 echo this concern, urging that transparency in code publication is essential for assessing practical utility.
2. Insufficient Intuitive Exposition: The dynamics of voltage control—including horizon effects, coupling across regions, and the influence of past actions—remain under-explained. While technical details reside in the Appendix, they are hard to parse for readers outside the power-systems domain. Sample operational trajectories, a schematic of agent observations and action timing, or a consolidated Dec-POMDP tuple table (per alternative review 4) would greatly enhance clarity.
3. Observation and Agent Grouping Ambiguity: It is unclear whether each agent controls one inverter or multiple units, and why multiple agents share identical regional observations. If agents in the same region receive the same inputs, a single shared policy might be more efficient. Alternative reviews 2 and 3 recommend ablations on observation scope, inter-region communication, and the impact of PV penetration heterogeneity to better understand policy scalability and robustness.
4. Limited Partial-Observability Handling: Policies are modeled with MLPs taking only the last two observations, which seems insufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption in this non-stationary setting (variable loads and generation throughout the day). As noted in the main review, more extensive memory (e.g., RNNs) or increased history length should be explored.
5. Lack of Hyperparameter and Training Details: Reproducibility is further hampered by sparse reporting of hyperparameters, training durations, convergence criteria, and computational costs. Alternative reviews 1 and 3 urge the inclusion of sensitivity analyses on learning rates, network architectures, and reward scaling, as well as statistical significance testing over multiple seeds.
6. Safety and Robustness Guarantees: The current simulator omits real-world concerns such as communication delays, sensor noise, hardware constraints, and adversarial attacks. The paper acknowledges interpretability and robustness challenges, but stops short of proposing formal safety mechanisms (e.g., shielded RL, Lyapunov-based certification) or testing out-of-distribution scenarios (sudden load swings, link outages) as suggested by alternative reviews 2 and 4.
7. Societal and Deployment Considerations: A deeper discussion of ethical and regulatory aspects is missing. Potential risks include inadvertent voltage excursions or equipment damage if learned policies fail under unseen conditions. Alternative review 1 recommends human-in-the-loop validation and fallback mechanisms; review 4 highlights equity issues (smaller utilities lacking computational resources) and the carbon footprint of model training versus operational savings.

Minor Issues:
- Notation conflict: h is defined both as the node-to-measurement mapping (line 195) and as history length (line 228).
- Section 5’s dense notation would benefit from illustrative examples or a simplified overview diagram.
- Figures with many subplots could be consolidated to focus on key performance trends.

Recommendation:
Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.      
This work makes a valuable engineering contribution by providing a realistic MARL benchmark for voltage control, but requires improvements in reproducibility (code release, hyperparameter details), clarity (intuitive explanations, observation structure), and robustness (safety guarantees, out-of-distribution tests) before it can fully serve the community.

reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the privacy and utility trade-offs of drawing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior, Dir(x + α), under a Dirichlet prior Dir(α). The authors derive an explicit, closed-form truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP) guarantee in terms of the minimum prior parameter and a tunable truncation parameter γ, and then convert it into an (ε, δ)-DP bound. They further provide analytical utility bounds for two canonical tasks—private Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling (bounding expected KL divergence) and private normalized histogram release (bounding ℓ∞ and ℓ2 errors)—and demonstrate empirically that the Dirichlet mechanism can outperform the Gaussian mechanism under certain regimes, especially for small sample sizes or sparse histograms.

Strengths:
- Clarity and Organization: The manuscript is very clearly written and well structured, guiding the reader from definitions of DP variants to main theorems, utility analyses, and experiments. Background on tCDP and conversion to (ε, δ)-DP is concise yet complete.
- Novel Privacy Analysis: This work appears to be the first to provide closed-form tCDP bounds specialized to Dirichlet posterior sampling, leveraging polygamma function properties. The derivation in Theorem 1, though based on a Taylor expansion, yields interpretable expressions directly in terms of prior parameter α and truncation γ.
- Analytical Utility Guarantees: The paper complements its privacy results with clean utility bounds. In Section 4.1, the bound on expected KL divergence for Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling is new and lends insight into how the prior parameter α controls bias. In Section 4.2, the ℓ∞ and ℓ2 error comparisons to the Gaussian mechanism clearly delineate regimes—small N, high dimension—where the Dirichlet approach yields lower error.
- Empirical Validation: Simulations verify the tightness of the tCDP bound and highlight practical regimes where the Dirichlet sampler outperforms Gaussian noise addition, corroborating the theoretical predictions.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
- Limited Scope of Use Cases: While Sections 4.1 and 4.2 are logically related (both normalize a Dirichlet sample), it remains unclear why they are treated as distinct use cases. A unified framing or discussion of how these applications extend to other Bayesian inference tasks (e.g., bandits, MCMC) would broaden the paper’s appeal.  
- Sample-Independent Bound Looseness: As acknowledged (Alt Rev 1 & 3), the worst-case, sample-independent tCDP bound can be loose when posterior counts are large. Future work could explore data-dependent or instance-specific bounds to tighten the guarantee in high-data regimes.
- Parameter Selection Guidance: The paper optimizes γ to balance the moment parameter ω and privacy cost ρ, but practical guidance on choosing γ and α is limited. Including a small worked example or table illustrating how to select α and γ to achieve target (ε, δ) would greatly aid practitioners.  
- Empirical Evaluation on Real Data: The experiments are confined to synthetic histograms. Evaluating the mechanism on real-world datasets—such as text corpora for language modeling or contingency tables in social science—would strengthen confidence in its practical utility.  
- Prior Assumptions and Fairness: The analysis assumes uniform or fixed priors (αi equal), which may induce bias against small or minority categories. A discussion of how nonuniform or hierarchical priors could mitigate such bias, or post-processing steps to equalize accuracy across subgroups, would enhance the societal impact section.
- Figure and Notation Clarity: 
   • A summary table of notation (especially for parameters α, α′, γ, ω, ρ) would help readers navigate dense expressions in Section 3.  
   • In Figure 2, renaming the y-axis from “ℓ2-accuracy” to “ℓ2-error” or “ℓ2-distance” would avoid confusion about which mechanism is “better” when curves cross.  
   • Some plot legends (e.g., in Figure 1) could be clearer on which curves correspond to theoretical versus empirical divergences.

Minor Comments:
- Introduction (lines 30–32): If by “posterior predictive” you refer to sampling both θ and observations Y, please clarify that Y is drawn per posterior sample, or adjust wording.  
- Theorem 1 Proof: The notation α versus α′ is potentially confusing—please confirm whether α′ is distinct or a typo.  
- Section 3.2: A brief plotted illustration of how varying γ trades between ω and ρ would help intuition.  

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors rightly acknowledge that large truncation parameter γ or large prior counts can lead to vacuous privacy guarantees, and they emphasize the importance of reporting prior choices. However, more concrete advice on auditing privacy cost (ρ thresholds), allocating privacy budgets in composite systems, and monitoring per-group accuracy to detect disparate impacts would make the societal impact section more actionable. Exploring adaptive or hierarchical priors could reduce bias against underrepresented subpopulations.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Overall, this paper makes a solid contribution by delivering explicit, closed-form privacy–utility trade-offs for Dirichlet posterior sampling. Its analytical insights and empirical comparisons to the Gaussian mechanism fill a gap in private Bayesian inference. With clarifications on parameter tuning, notation, and broader empirical validation, the work will be a valuable resource for both theoreticians and practitioners in differential privacy.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.

Main Review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: Summary: This paper gives a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has a single main result, namely a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of the algorithm which takes a sensitive data set as input and a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution on the parameters, and samples a single value from that distribution. 

The main result (Theorem 1) is stated in terms of truncated concentrated DP, though I think it might be easier to state it in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. (I also think it would be worthwhile, in the corollaries, setting $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to get clear statements.) 

I have very few comments on the writing: the paper is clear and easy to understand and, I think, accurately represents and discusses the contributions. The only question here is significance: the paper is providing updated calculations for a well-know idea. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue. I’m less sure that it meets the bar for NeurIPS. If the committee ultimately decides not to accept the paper, I would encourage the authors to submit to an appropriate journal.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Well addressed in a separate section.

Main Review:

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper provides a precise analysis of the differential‐privacy loss incurred by drawing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior over multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set. The core contribution (Theorem 1) is a closed‐form truncated concentrated DP (tCDP) bound expressed in terms of prior parameters (the α vector), sample sensitivities (Δ₂, Δ∞), and a tunable truncation parameter γ. The authors then derive corollaries converting this tCDP guarantee into standard Rényi DP or (ε,δ)-DP statements (Theorem 2), and illustrate how to set Δ∞ = Δ₂ = 1 for concrete bounds. Section 4 analyzes utility in two canonical tasks—(i) one‐time Multinomial‐Dirichlet sampling, bounding expected KL divergence to the true posterior, and (ii) private histogram release, bounding ℓ∞ error and comparing against the Gaussian mechanism. Simulations demonstrate that the theoretical tCDP bound closely tracks actual Rényi divergences and identify regimes (e.g. sparse histograms or small samples) where Dirichlet sampling outperforms Gaussian noise addition.

Strengths:  
1. Novel, explicit formulas. By specializing general exponential‐family sampling results to the Dirichlet case, the paper yields interpretable, sample‐independent tCDP and (ε,δ)-DP expressions that practitioners can apply directly.  
2. Rigorous derivations. The Taylor‐expansion proof for Theorem 1 is detailed (with full derivations in the appendix), and the conversion to approximate DP via an optimized γ parameter is both sound and well motivated.  
3. Two concrete utility analyses. The KL bound for posterior sampling and the ℓ∞ histogram‐error bound provide clear guidance on privacy–utility trade‐offs, with comparisons to the Gaussian mechanism that highlight when the Dirichlet approach has an advantage.  
4. Empirical validation. Simulations verify bound tightness and illustrate practical regimes for superiority over Gaussian noise, helping to build confidence in the theoretical claims.  
5. Clarity of exposition. The overall structure flows logically from definitions through main results to applications. The separate societal impact section addresses potential misuse (e.g. vacuous privacy claims for large ρ) and discusses bias against small subpopulations under uniform priors.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
• Significance and framing. While the specialization to Dirichlet yields useful closed‐form bounds, the paper essentially refines well‐known posterior‐sampling privacy theory. It may strengthen impact to highlight specific downstream applications—such as private bandit algorithms or topic‐model sampling—where these explicit expressions directly inform parameter choices.  
• Alternative DP formulations. The current presentation emphasizes tCDP; many readers would appreciate stating the main result first in terms of Rényi DP (as a function of α and order λ) and then deriving tCDP and (ε,δ)-DP as corollaries. This reordering could streamline understanding and relate more transparently to existing RDP literature.  
• Notation and accessibility. Sections 3.1–3.2 introduce dense notation (polygamma functions, ω, ρ, γ) that may challenge some readers. A summary table of symbols and a small, worked numerical example showing how to choose (α,γ) to meet a target (ε,δ) would greatly aid practitioners. Clearer legends in figures (e.g. Figure 1) would also improve readability.  
• Tightness and data‐dependence. The presented tCDP bound is sample‐independent and can be loose in large‐data regimes. The authors acknowledge this, but providing instance‐specific or adaptive bounds—perhaps leveraging observed posterior counts—would strengthen practical utility. At minimum, a plotted illustration of the γ–privacy–utility trade‐off would help guide parameter selection, or heuristics/cross‐validation strategies for γ could be discussed.  
• Empirical scope. Experiments are restricted to synthetic histograms. Demonstrating performance on real datasets (e.g. text‐modeling counts or network‐traffic histograms) would bolster confidence in the mechanism’s practical advantages and clarify any implementation challenges.  
• Fairness and prior selection. The analysis assumes uniform priors (αi ≥ 1), which may induce bias against small or minority subpopulations. The societal impact section could be expanded by suggesting adaptive or hierarchical prior choices and discussing post‐processing or auditing protocols to detect and mitigate disparate impacts.  
• Privacy composition. In many real‐world systems, one‐time sampling is part of larger private pipelines. A brief discussion or reference on composing these bounds—especially when sampling multiple times or combining with other DP mechanisms—would be valuable for practitioners.

Recommendation (6: Weak Accept / Marginally above threshold):  
The paper delivers a technically solid and well‐motivated specialization of differential‐privacy analysis to Dirichlet posterior sampling, complete with explicit bounds, utility assessments, and simulations. While the ideas build on existing exponential‐family results, the presented closed‐form expressions and application scenarios represent a useful advance for privacy‐aware Bayesian methods. The manuscript would benefit from clearer framing in terms of RDP, more guidance on parameter tuning, broader empirical validation, and deeper discussion of practical safeguards (e.g. fairness, composition). Nonetheless, on balance, I find the work makes a sufficiently novel and practical contribution to merit acceptance in an archival venue.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies releasing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior with several variants of differential privacy in the two specific tasks of Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Both privacy and utility guarantees are analyzed by the authors. The authors compare the results with Gaussian mechanism theoretically and empirically using a simple simulation.

Main Review:
The paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The related works are summarized and allow the reader to compare the drawbacks of existing methods relatively to the proposed methods. All the notations and background definitions are clearly stated. While the paper contains a significant amount of theoretical derivations, many of them are accompanied by intuitions and interpretations (such as discussion of low/high privacy regimes or illustrations of  behavior of the computed quantities as a function of parameters, such as Fig. 1). I was not able to thoroughly verify all the derivations, but the math seems sounds. Overall, i believe the paper makes a good contribution and will be interesting to the researchers in the area.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper contains a section on "Potential negative societal impacts" which reasonably discussed the implications of the usage of the method in an industrial setting.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper provides a thorough study of the inherent differential‐privacy properties of releasing a single sample drawn from a Dirichlet posterior, focusing on two canonical tasks: one‐time Multinomial‐Dirichlet sampling (measured via expected KL divergence) and private normalized histogram release (measured via ℓ∞ error). Building on general exponential‐family posterior‐sampling results, the authors derive the first explicit, closed‐form truncated concentrated DP (tCDP) bound tailored to Dirichlet posteriors (Theorem 1), then convert it into an (ε,δ)‐DP guarantee by optimizing an auxiliary truncation parameter γ (Theorem 2). Theoretical privacy and utility guarantees are complemented by simulations that illustrate tightness of the bounds and regimes—particularly sparse histograms or small sample sizes—where the Dirichlet mechanism can outperform the classical Gaussian mechanism.

Strengths  
1. Novel, Specialized Analysis  
    – Unlike generic RDP or zCDP results for exponential families, the paper’s closed‐form tCDP expressions exploit Dirichlet‐specific properties (via polygamma functions), yielding interpretable trade‐offs in terms of prior parameters (α) and data sensitivity.  
    – The optimized γ‐parameter conversion to (ε,δ)‐DP is an original technical insight that clarifies how to balance the moment bound ω and privacy cost ρ.  

2. Rigorous Utility Characterization  
    – The authors derive clean utility bounds for both tasks: expected KL divergence in Dirichlet sampling (Theorem 3) and ℓ∞ error in histogram release (Theorem 4). They quantify how utility scales in high‐ and low‐privacy regimes, providing sample‐complexity insights.  
    – Empirical simulations validate theoretical predictions, showing that the tCDP bound closely tracks actual Rényi divergences and that the Dirichlet sampler can outperform Gaussian noise in certain parameter regimes.  

3. Clear Organization and Intuition  
    – The manuscript is well structured: background definitions, related work comparisons, main theorems, and applications flow naturally. Notations are stated clearly upfront, and many derivations are accompanied by intuitive discussions (e.g., Fig. 1’s illustration of privacy‐regime transitions).  
    – A dedicated section on “Potential negative societal impacts” demonstrates awareness of real‐world considerations, such as vacuous privacy guarantees when ρ is large.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Scope of Experiments  
    – All empirical evaluations use synthetic Dirichlet‐Multinomial data. To bolster practical impact, experiments on real datasets (e.g., word‐histogram language data or network‐traffic histograms) would help assess whether the worst‐case, sample‐independent privacy bounds remain meaningful in practice.  

2. Practical Guidance for Parameter Tuning  
    – Choosing the truncation parameter γ and prior α impacts both privacy and utility, but guidance is somewhat limited. A brief worked example or a small case study showing how to select (α,γ) to meet specific (ε,δ) targets would aid practitioners. Practical heuristics—such as cross‐validation or grid searches—could be suggested.  
    – The paper acknowledges that the tCDP bound can be loose when posterior counts are large; exploring instance‐specific or data‐dependent bounds (e.g., thresholds on sample counts) could tighten these guarantees.  

3. Notational Density and Accessibility  
    – Sections 3.1–3.2 are heavy in notation, especially around the Taylor‐expansion proof of Theorem 1. A summarized table of key symbols and parameters, or a sidebar “cheat sheet,” would improve readability.  
    – Some figures (e.g., Figure 1) would benefit from clearer legends and annotations explaining the role of γ and ω in the plotted curves.  

4. Assumptions on Priors and Fairness Considerations  
    – The utility bounds often assume uniform priors (αi equal and ≥1). Discussion of more general, nonuniform or hierarchical priors is limited. Since uniform priors can introduce bias against small or minority subpopulations, the paper could briefly note mitigation strategies—such as privacy‐aware prior elicitation or post‐processing corrections—to promote fairness.  

5. Deferred Proofs and Technical Details  
    – Some deferred proofs in the appendix (e.g., remainder‐term bounds in the Taylor expansion) omit technical steps that would reassure careful readers. Expanding these details or adding a short proof sketch of the remainder bound in the main text would strengthen confidence in Theorem 1’s tightness.  

Decision and Rating  
I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper). The work makes a solid, focused contribution by providing explicit tCDP and (ε,δ)‐DP guarantees for Dirichlet posterior sampling, along with meaningful utility analyses. While scope for deeper empirical validation and practical guidance remains, the paper’s theoretical clarity and novel, interpretable bounds will be of significant interest to the differential‐privacy community—particularly those working on private Bayesian methods, synthetic‐data generation, and histogram release.

reviews.summary: This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.
reviews.main_review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the inherent differential‐privacy properties of one‐time sampling from a Dirichlet posterior, focusing on deriving closed‐form truncated concentrated DP (tCDP) bounds for Dirichlet(x+α) sampling, converting them into (ε,δ)‐DP guarantees, and analyzing utility in two canonical tasks: private Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling (via expected KL divergence) and private histogram release (via ℓ∞‐error bounds compared to the Gaussian mechanism). While this specialization to the Dirichlet case yields explicit polygamma‐based expressions—filling a niche unaddressed by generic exponential‐family analyses—the paper suffers from numerous clarity, exposition, and contextualization issues that, in aggregate, hinder its accessibility and perceived significance for the NeurIPS audience.

Strengths (drawn also from alternative reviews):
• Novelty and Technical Soundness: The specialization of posterior‐sampling privacy theory to the Dirichlet case produces the first closed‐form tCDP guarantee tailored to Dirichlet posteriors. The Taylor‐expansion proof of Theorem 1 is correct in outline, and the conversion to (ε,δ)‐DP via optimization of the auxiliary parameter γ follows standard but well‐motivated lemmas. The utility analyses for KL divergence (Theorem 3) and histogram error (Theorem 4) are a valuable complement, outlining regimes in which Dirichlet sampling can outperform Gaussian noise, especially for sparse histograms or small sample sizes.
• Honest Discussion of Limitations: The paper acknowledges that the sample‐independent tCDP bound can be loose in large-N regimes, and that choices of γ affect the privacy–utility tradeoff. Alternative reviews similarly note the need for instance‐specific or data‐dependent bounds to tighten worst‐case guarantees.
• Experimental Validation: Simulations illustrate the tightness of the tCDP bound and highlight privacy–utility regimes. Comparing to the Gaussian mechanism provides intuition about when the Dirichlet approach is advantageous.

Major Concerns:
1. Unclear Problem Formulation and Context  
   – The paper fails to clearly define the privacy problem: What elements of the dataset are sensitive? In what precise sense does “one‐time sampling” differ from prior exponential‐family sampling work? Is “one‐time sampling” meant to denote a single draw, or a batch draw without adaptive decisions? This term is never defined, and readers unfamiliar with bandit or reinforcement‐learning contexts will struggle.  
   – Relatedly, the introduction does not situate this work against closely related DP histogram or synthetic‐data methods (beyond Gaussian addition), nor does it clearly articulate the practical scenarios (e.g., DP bandits, active learning) where these explicit Dirichlet bounds would be deployed.

2. Precision of Language and Notation  
   – Throughout the text, many statements are imprecise or potentially misleading. For example, “DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters” (Line 38)—the term “affected” is ambiguous. A clearer wording would be “analyze how privacy guarantees depend on prior parameters.”  
   – In Line 67, “any posterior sampling with the log‐likelihood bounded by B is 4B‐differentially private” mixes B and ε without clarifying sign conventions or whether the bound is upper or lower.  
   – The phrase “if the condition on the log‐likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability” (Line 70) conflates function‐level notions (Lipschitz) with random‐variable bounds, leaving readers puzzled.  
   – Notation for dataset statistics x, x′, and the empirical distribution p are introduced out of order (Line 74, 142, 150), with no summary table for symbols. A concise “Notation” or “Preliminaries” table would greatly improve readability. Alternative reviews also recommend a worked numerical example to illustrate parameter choices (α, γ) for achieving a target (ε,δ).

3. Missing Definitions and Conceptual Gaps  
   – The key concept of “one‐time sampling” is never formally defined, nor is “one‐time” contrasted with streaming or adaptive sampling.  
   – Theorem 1 employs x and x′ as sufficient statistics from adjacent datasets, but does not specify whether these are drawn with or without replacement, nor how the ℓ₂‐ or ℓ∞‐sensitivities ∆₂, ∆∞ are computed in practice.  
   – The term “one‐time sampling” appears repeatedly but is not found in standard DP literature, hindering reproducibility and follow‐up work.

4. Limited Empirical Scope  
   – Experiments are restricted to synthetic histograms with uniform priors. While synthetic tests confirm theoretical trends, the paper does not evaluate on real datasets (e.g., text, network traffic, or demographic histograms) where nonuniform priors and data skew could expose worst‐case looseness.  
   – Comparisons are limited to a single Gaussian baseline. Incorporating other DP histogram mechanisms (e.g., Laplace, sparse vector, or privacy‐amplified posterior samplers) would contextualize the advantages and limitations of the Dirichlet approach more fully.  
   – Figures such as Figure 2 label the y-axis “accuracy,” confusing readers accustomed to classification accuracy; a more appropriate term like “error” or “norm” should be used, with clear legends.

5. Insufficient Discussion of Societal and Fairness Implications  
   – Although the paper briefly notes that uniform priors may bias small subpopulations and that large ρ can be vacuous, it stops short of proposing mitigation strategies (e.g., hierarchical priors, adaptive priors, or auditing protocols). Alternative reviews call for explicit guidance on privacy‐budget allocation in composite systems and on auditing disparate impacts across groups.

Minor Issues:
• Line 128: “privacy” → “private.”  
• Line 142 vs. Lines 144–145: Inconsistency between p(x|y) interpreted as conditional versus sampling process.  
• Proofs deferred to the appendix often omit key intuition; a brief sketch of remainder‐term bounds in the main text would help.  
• Typographical and article‐usage errors throughout suggest a careful language edit is needed.

Conclusion and Rating:
While the paper provides a novel, technically sound specialization of posterior‐sampling privacy theory to the Dirichlet case, its contribution is undermined by pervasive clarity issues, undefined terminology, and insufficient empirical validation. The problem setting and practical significance remain only loosely motivated, and crucial definitions (e.g., one‐time sampling) are never formalized. Collectively, these shortcomings pose a serious hurdle for non‐expert readers and diminish the work’s impact. I therefore recommend rejection.

Rating: 3 – Clear rejection
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.

Main Review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection

title: Hierarchical Prototype Network for Continual Graph Representation Learning

reviews.summary: This paper introduces continual learning in graph settings, where new graph-based tasks come in over time. To learn effective node classifiers for new tasks without forgetting the old tasks, a three-level prototype network is outlined. Atomic, node, and class prototypes are jointly used on top of Atomic Feature Extractors, where only the relevant features and prototypes are selected and updated in each new task, without disturbing the others. Experiments on eight benchmarks show the effectiveness and lack of forgetting for continual node classification.
reviews.main_review:
[Strengths]

This paper addresses a challenging task in graph learning, namely the scenario where new graph tasks come in over time. The proposed multi-level approach is interesting at a high level as it addresses different levels of granularity.

The paper furthermore excels at outlining current advances in continual learning and specifically in the graph context. This outline helps not only with providing the proper background and starting point of this paper, but also helps to understand what is new here.

The presented results show that the approach not only obtains a high accuracy overall, but at the cost of minimal loss of accuracy with the introduction of new tasks. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach.

[Weaknesses]

There are a number of limitations to the paper that require addressing.

First, the method is not written well and unnecessarily hard to go through. Section 2 contains many unintuitive steps and unclear notations that make it hard to understand what is going on. For example:
- What is the idea behind subsampling the neighbourhood in lines 125-127?
- According to Equation 3, the node v is represented by a set of features, both from node and structure information. Why are these two sets combined in Equation 3 and separated again in Equation 5? And how do you go from AFE_{node}^{select} and AFE_{struct}^{select} to \mathbb{E}_A^{select}(v)?  There is no explanation and completely unclear.
- What is the point of Algorithm 1? It contains so few steps that there is no information anymore. Steps 4 and 5 are the same too.
- Where does \mathbb{A}(v) magically come from in line 178? What does associated with mean? And how big is the set  for each node? Again not clear from the method section.
- The N-prototypes are obtained from \mathbb{A}(v), and C-prototypes are obtained from N-prototypes. What does Equation 11 mean in that case? How can you throw them on one pile?
- At the end, there is a cross-entropy objective in Equation 12, where the likelihoods are obtained from a fully-connected layers that takes inputs from all levels of prototypes. Don’t the prototypes have different dimensionalities however? What is going on on lines 193 and 195?

Next to the mathematical explanation, the writing also requires improvement. A few examples from Section 2.4 and 2.5 (not a full list):
- Line 192: pass = passed
- Line 203: unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware
- Line 204: HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator
- Line 228: is = denotes the / are the

Section 2.5 provides two theorems, but the appendix only provides a proof for Theorem 2. Where is the proof for Theorem 1?

In the experiments, Table 1 shows that the proposed approach outperforms all other approaches. Where do the numbers of the baselines come from? Baseline [39] should btw be updated to:

[a] Zhou, Fan, and Chengtai Cao. "Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting in Graph Neural Networks with Experience Replay." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 5. 2021.

In [a], they report a best PM of 95.66% and 81.83% on Cora and Citeseer, compared to 93.7% and 79.0% for this paper. Notably, [39] obtains only 71.1% and 65.5%. How come there are such big differences between what is reported in the original paper and what is reported in Table 1? Table 1 suggest that the proposed approach is far better than the baselines, while a direct comparison between papers suggests otherwise.

[Overall]

This paper addresses a challenging task with a multi-level approach. The method is intuitive at a high level and the related work is outlined well. The method section itself can however hardly be followed and requires big changes. Experimentally, there are also open questions that require attention regarding reported numbers.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The main limitation that is discussed is that only node classification is investigated. The checklist states that there are no negative societal impacts of the work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the underexplored problem of continual learning on graphs, where node classification tasks arrive sequentially and models must adapt without forgetting prior knowledge. The authors propose Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), which employ Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) to decompose node attributes and multi-hop structure into atomic embeddings. These embeddings are then organized into three levels of prototypes—atomic, node, and class—and selectively activated or refined for each new task. Theoretical results bound the number of prototypes required and provide conditions under which previous-task representations remain unchanged. Experiments on eight benchmarks, including large-scale OGB datasets, demonstrate strong accuracy and low forgetting while using far fewer parameters than competing approaches.

Strengths:
1. Novelty and Significance: Building on recent advances in prototype-based continual learning, the paper uniquely extends these ideas to graph data by introducing multi–granularity prototypes. This tri-level abstraction (atomic, node, class) goes beyond weight regularization or memory replay and directly stores embeddings, offering a fresh perspective on mitigating catastrophic forgetting in GNNs.
2. Theoretical Analysis: The derivation of upper bounds on prototype counts via spherical‐coding arguments and the task‐distance criterion for representation stability lend interpretability and rigor. These results, although relying on idealized assumptions, represent a valuable step toward understanding resource requirements in continual graph learning.
3. Empirical Rigor: The authors evaluate HPNs across eight diverse datasets—spanning citation, co-purchase, web-page, and social networks—and report both average accuracy and forgetting metrics. Comparisons against standard continual‐learning adaptations (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS) and recent graph‐specific methods (ERGNN, TWP) highlight substantial gains, particularly on OGB-Arxiv and OGB-Products. Memory efficiency is also convincingly demonstrated, with HPNs using orders of magnitude fewer parameters than baselines.

Weaknesses:
1. Clarity of Method Description: Section 2 is notation‐heavy and contains unintuitive steps that impede understanding. Key issues include:
   - The rationale for neighborhood subsampling (lines 125–127) is not motivated.
   - Equations 3 and 5 merge and then separate node versus structural features without explanation, and the transition to the select‐operation \mathbb{E}_A^{select}(v) is opaque.
   - Algorithm 1 offers minimal guidance—multiple steps are duplicated (steps 4 and 5) and lack context for prototype activation.
   - The origin and size of the set \mathbb{A}(v) in line 178 (“associated with”) are undefined.
   - It is unclear how atomic prototypes aggregate into node and class prototypes (Equation 11), or how multi‐dimensional prototypes feed into a single cross‐entropy layer (Equations 12, lines 193 and 195).
   A summary table of symbols and a running example illustrating prototype matching across tasks would greatly enhance readability.
2. Missing or Incomplete Proofs: While Appendix B provides the proof for Theorem 2, the proof for Theorem 1 is absent. Given the paper’s reliance on these theoretical claims, complete and accessible proofs are essential.
3. Experimental Reporting and Baselines:
   - Table 1 reports baseline numbers that diverge significantly from original sources (e.g., [39] reports 95.66%/81.83% on Cora/Citeseer, whereas this paper lists 93.7%/79.0%). The authors should clarify whether they re‐ran baselines under identical protocols or adopted published results, and reconcile discrepancies.
   - The paper omits comparisons to dynamic‐graph representation methods (e.g., EvolveGCN, TGAT) that, while focused on temporal dynamics, may serve as proxies for category‐incremental learning in evolving networks.
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Practicality:
   - The threshold parameters t_A, t_N, t_C governing prototype matching are tuned via grid search for each dataset. There is no discussion of adaptive thresholding or prototype pruning, raising concerns about scalability to unseen domains or high‐velocity streams.
   - Runtime complexity of prototype matching—especially on large prototype banks— and sensitivity to task ordering are not analyzed.
5. Writing and Presentation:
   - Minor typos and grammatical errors appear (e.g., “pass = passed,” “unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware”). 
   - Large tables (e.g., Table 1) are hard to parse; splitting into smaller panels or highlighting key comparisons would aid comprehension.
   - Figure captions lack detail on the specific datasets and metrics illustrated.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Revise Section 2 to include a concise glossary of symbols, a step‐by‐step illustrative example of prototype selection and activation, and clear definitions of all sets and operations.
- Provide the missing proof for Theorem 1 in the appendix and, in the main text, offer an intuitive sketch of both theorems for broader accessibility.
- Clarify baseline evaluation protocols: explicitly state whether results are re‐implemented or borrowed, and reconcile any mismatches with original papers.
- Include additional baselines from dynamic‐graph literature to position HPNs relative to methods that handle temporal evolution.
- Discuss adaptive strategies for threshold setting, prototype pruning, and handling rare or rapidly changing categories to improve practicality.
- Analyze the runtime and memory overhead of prototype matching at inference time, and empirically study sensitivity to task ordering.
- Address potential societal impacts: prototype-based methods may inadvertently perpetuate biases present in node attributes or graph structure. A brief discussion on fairness audits or privacy‐preserving extensions would be valuable.

Overall Recommendation:
Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

This work presents a promising and original framework for continual graph learning, supported by solid theoretical foundations and compelling empirical results. However, significant revisions are required to clarify the methodological details, complete theoretical proofs, reconcile experimental discrepancies, and address practical concerns around hyperparameter tuning and computational overhead. With these improvements, the paper would make a strong contribution to the field.

reviews.summary: This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.
