reviews.summary: This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.
reviews.main_review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a finite-time mean-square error analysis for a distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithm over time-varying directed graphs with row-stochastic weight matrices. Building on recent interest in removing the doubly-stochastic requirement, the authors introduce an analysis technique based on absolute probability sequences to track consensus error under Markovian noise and both constant and diminishing step sizes. They further propose a push-sum variant to recover exact averaging of local equilibria and establish its non-asymptotic performance guarantees.

Overall, the relaxation from doubly-stochastic to merely row-stochastic weights and the incorporation of push-sum are meaningful extensions beyond prior work on distributed temporal-difference learning and consensus SA. The use of quadratic comparison functions alongside absolute probability sequences offers a novel analytic path under uni-directional communication. That said, important limitations and presentation issues temper the contribution, leading to a marginally negative recommendation.

Strengths  
1. Relaxation of Weight Assumptions: By allowing only row-stochastic interactions and time-varying directed graphs, the work extends classical consensus-based SA analyses (which typically assume doubly-stochastic or bidirectional connectivity) to more realistic network scenarios.  
2. Technical Novelty: The deployment of absolute probability sequences to obtain a simple recursion for the estimate errors (Equation 4) and the finite-time bounds under both constant and decaying step sizes is an original methodological combination. The push-sum variant further broadens applicability by ensuring convergence to the uniform average.  
3. Rigorous Proofs: Under clear assumptions (mixing times, Hurwitz matrix conditions, uniform connectivity), the derivations are detailed and mathematically sound. The finite-time bounds formally recover existing doubly-stochastic results as special cases.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Limited Scope of Linear SA:  
   – The algorithm in (1) assumes an identical driving matrix A(x) for all agents (while allowing distinct bⁱ(x)). This restriction essentially confines the setting to distributed policy evaluation, rather than the broader class of linear SA problems (e.g., decentralized linear regression). Repositioning the paper as focused on policy evaluation would more accurately reflect its contributions.  
2. Assumption 6 on Absolute Probability Sequences:  
   – This critical assumption demands that the absolute probability sequence converge to a limit vector. The paper’s current justification (Remark 3) covers only trivial cases—doubly-stochastic weights or fixed neighbor sets—and does not convince for genuinely time-varying directed graphs. The authors should provide an analytical example or constructive conditions (beyond mere simulations) under which Assumption 6 holds.  
3. Opaque Finite-Time Constants:  
   – The main bounds (e.g., Theorem 2’s constant C₂) are expressed as depending on parameters L, γ_min, γ_max, A_max, b_max, etc., but without explicit characterization. As noted in [Alternative Reviews 1 & 2], the absence of a detailed dependency or numerical illustration renders the bounds difficult to interpret or use for parameter tuning. A table or running example quantifying these constants would greatly aid clarity and practical relevance.  
4. Comparative Benefit of the Analysis Technique:  
   – It remains unclear how the absolute probability sequence approach outperforms or complements classical analyses (e.g., the methods in [52] or diffusion strategies by Chen & Sayed 2013). A direct comparison—either qualitative or via a small example—would strengthen the case for this technique’s necessity.  
5. Presentation and Readability:  
   – While the exposition is logically structured, the notation is heavy (many constants K₁, C₁–C₁₂, multiple indices). A schematic overview of proof structure, a summary table of constants, or a running toy example would help readers navigate the technical material.  
   – Definitions (such as the absolute probability sequence) should be accompanied by illustrative examples to ground abstract concepts.  
6. Lack of Empirical Validation:  
   – None of the reviews included simulations or case studies. Given the theoretical focus, empirical results on representative network topologies (with uni-directional links, varying connectivity) would demonstrate the tightness and applicability of the finite-time bounds in practice.  

Minor Comments  
– Clarify whether Assumption 6 pertains to out-neighbors or in-neighbors when fixing the number of neighbors.  
– In Theorem 5, provide further explanation on why the current analysis cannot extend to constant step sizes for push-sum and, if possible, suggest avenues to overcome this limitation.  
– Ensure consistent notation for matrix and vector norms, and index the probability sequence πₜ uniformly throughout.

Decision: 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold  
Although the paper addresses a pertinent problem and offers a fresh analytic toolkit for directed consensus SA, significant issues remain. The restricted algorithmic scope (identical A across agents), the unsubstantiated Assumption 6, the opaque bound constants, and the absence of empirical validation outweigh the methodological novelty. I therefore recommend a marginal rejection, with encouragement to revise by:  
• Reframing the contribution explicitly as distributed policy evaluation.  
• Providing analytical constructions or weaker conditions for Assumption 6.  
• Detailing the exact dependencies of the finite-time constants and offering numerical examples.  
• Comparing the absolute probability sequence technique against classical approaches.  
• Including at least a small simulation study to illustrate performance under practical network conditions.

Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper addresses the finite-time analysis of distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms operating under Markovian noise over time‐varying directed graphs whose interaction matrices need only be row‐stochastic. Building on classical consensus‐based SA, the authors derive novel mean‐square error bounds for both constant and diminishing step sizes by leveraging absolute probability sequences and quadratic comparison functions. To recover exact network‐wide averaging, they introduce a push‐sum–based variant and prove its own non‐asymptotic convergence guarantees. These results meaningfully relax prior requirements of doubly stochastic weights or bidirectional communication, broadening the theoretical foundation for distributed multi‐agent learning under asymmetric and mobile network scenarios.

Strengths:  
1. Originality and Technical Depth:  By dispensing with the doubly stochastic assumption and allowing unidirectional links, the paper tackles a significant gap in the literature. The combination of absolute probability sequences with quadratic comparison functions to control consensus error is a technically fresh approach, and the push‐sum extension for straight averaging under row‐stochastic weights is both novel and well motivated.  
2. Rigorous Finite‐Time Bounds:  The authors provide detailed, mathematically sound derivations for mean‐square error bounds under clear assumptions (mixing times, Hurwitz conditions, uniform strong connectivity). They carefully track the dependence on all problem parameters and show that their bounds recover existing doubly stochastic results as special cases.  
3. Well‐Structured Presentation of Main Results:  Definitions, assumptions, and main theorems are stated clearly, and the appendices contain complete proofs. The exposition follows a logical progression from consensus‐only SA to push‐sum SA, which helps guide the reader through the core ideas.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. Lack of Sample Complexity Statements:  The main theorems characterize convergence rates but do not translate these into explicit sample complexity bounds (i.e., total number of samples needed to reach ε‐accuracy). Providing such a statement would enable direct comparison with state‐of‐the‐art distributed SA and reinforcement learning results.  
2. Heavy Notation and Opaque Constants:  The finite‐time bounds involve numerous constants (e.g., C1–C12, mixing parameters, π_min) whose numerical values and interdependencies are not illustrated. This complicates both theoretical interpretation and practical parameter tuning. A consolidated table or schematic summarizing these constants—and a brief discussion of their typical magnitudes—would greatly enhance accessibility.  
3. Empirical Validation Missing:  Although theoretical, the paper would benefit from at least a toy simulation or small‐scale experiment. A simple numerical example could demonstrate the tightness of the bounds, illustrate how the constants influence convergence, and validate performance under realistic communication delays or link failures.  
4. Discussion of Key Assumptions:  
   • Assumption 6 on the convergence of the absolute probability sequence is critical yet its practical verification in time‐varying directed networks can be nontrivial. The authors should provide intuition or a worked example showing how this assumption holds in common settings, or discuss potential relaxations.  
   • The dependence on certain condition numbers (e.g., spectral gaps, Hurwitz constants) is noted but not explored. A dedicated discussion of how these quantities affect convergence in practice would make the results more convincing.  
5. Clarity and Readability:  The manuscript suffers from dense notation (inconsistent norms, bracket styles, indexing of πₜ). Introducing a running example or schematic proof outline in the main text would help readers track the flow of arguments. A summary box of notation at the start could also reduce cognitive load.  
6. Related Work and Context:  While the literature review is extensive, it overlooks some recent developments in nonlinear gossip and asynchronous SA (e.g., Mathkar & Borkar, 2016). Placing the current contributions in dialogue with these works would more clearly delineate the novelty of the proposed analysis.  
7. Societal Impact and Robustness:  As distributed RL and SA find their way into safety‐critical applications (autonomous vehicles, sensor networks, smart grids), a brief discussion of potential privacy, security, or adversarial considerations would be valuable. In particular, how might the algorithm behave under malicious node behavior, and can robustness guarantees be integrated?

Decision: Weak Reject (Rating: 5 – Marginally below the acceptance threshold)  
The paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by extending finite‐time SA analysis to row‐stochastic, directed networks and by proposing a push‐sum variant. However, the lack of sample complexity statements, empirical validation, and practical guidance on constants and assumptions, combined with unanswered concerns from the rebuttal regarding key assumptions, limit its immediate impact. Addressing the points above—especially sample complexity, illustrative experiments, and clearer treatment of constants and assumptions—would substantially strengthen the work for future resubmission.

title: Local $K$-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.
reviews.main_review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents Local K-Means (LKM), a variant of k-means clustering that leverages a precomputed k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph to restrict distance computations to local neighborhoods. The authors further show that by adjusting an exponent parameter p, this formulation unifies LKM, KSUMS, and spectral clustering within a single objective, and they derive an efficient coordinate-descent solver with O(n·k) time per iteration. Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic and sixteen real-world datasets demonstrate that LKM matches or exceeds the clustering accuracy (ACC, NMI, ARI) of standard k-means, KSUMS, spectral clustering and other graph-based methods, while offering substantial speedups.

Strengths:
• Unified framework:  Framing k-means, KSUMS, and ratio-cut spectral clustering as instances of the same trace-minimization objective is conceptually neat and contributes clarity to graph-based clustering methods.  
• Computational efficiency:  Exploiting sparsity in the k-NN graph to achieve O(n·k) updates is a practical advance for large-scale, high-cluster-count problems, as evidenced by consistent per-iteration speedups in the experiments.  
• Robustness to outliers:  By relying on local neighborhoods rather than global distances, LKM empirically shows improved resilience to noise and outliers on synthetic benchmarks.  
• Broad empirical evaluation:  Testing on both toy problems (e.g., Figure 3) and diverse real datasets provides convincing evidence of the method’s effectiveness across settings.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Missing runtime reporting on real data (Table 3).  
   One of LKM’s principal claimed advantages is computational efficiency. Yet wall-clock times for the real-world benchmarks in Table 3 are omitted. The authors should report and compare end-to-end runtimes (including graph construction) against all baselines, and discuss preprocessing and per-iteration costs in detail.

2. Lack of statistical analysis and initialization details.  
   LKM depends on the initialization of cluster labels y; however, the paper does not specify whether results in Table 1 and Table 3 are averaged over multiple random initializations, nor does it report standard deviations or statistical significance. I recommend running multiple seeds, reporting means ± std, and conducting significance tests to support claims of superiority or parity.

3. Sensitivity to hyperparameters and graph quality.  
   The choice of k in the k-NN graph, the exponent p, and the dissimilarity cap λ can substantially affect performance. Alternative reviews note that no sensitivity studies are provided. The authors should include ablation plots showing clustering accuracy and runtime as functions of k, p, and λ, and analyze how approximate graph construction (e.g., via locality-sensitive hashing or approximate nearest neighbors) influences results.

4. Convergence properties and theoretical guarantees.  
   While the coordinate-descent updates are intuitive, there is no discussion of convergence behavior—neither empirical iteration counts nor theoretical guarantees. Including a brief convergence analysis (e.g., monotonic objective decrease, bound on number of iterations) or at least reporting typical iteration counts across datasets would strengthen the methodological contribution.

5. Clarity and presentation issues.  
   • Section 3’s definition of neighborhood is confusing: the directed nature of k-NN graphs makes the neighbor relation asymmetric, which should be explicitly addressed.  
   • Equations (19–22) and Eqs. 19–20 in other versions are densely packed; interleaving more explanatory text or a small running example would aid comprehension.  
   • Key pseudocode currently resides in the supplementary material; moving an abridged version or flow diagram into the main text would improve accessibility.  
   • Figures lack some axis labels (e.g., in Figure 3), and Table 3’s formatting hampers quick interpretation. Minor typesetting errors (parentheses, unicode artifacts) should be corrected.

6. Related work and broader context.  
   The analogy between k-NN graphs and social networks is not particularly insightful and can be replaced with a discussion of exemplar-based or manifold-regularized clustering approaches. For instance, recent work on regularized NMF that leverages k-NN graphs (Mani et al., SDM 2021) and fast k-means variants such as coreset or mini-batch methods warrant comparison or contrast, both in terms of methodology and runtime.

7. Reproducibility and code release.  
   To facilitate adoption, the authors should provide code for graph construction, hyperparameter settings, and the LKM solver. Detailed procedures for building the k-NN graph (exact vs. approximate), along with parameter ranges, would greatly aid reproducibility.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The reliance on a precomputed k-NN graph imposes upfront computational and memory costs, especially in high-dimensional or non-metric spaces, and approximate neighborhood errors can degrade clustering quality. A more thorough discussion of approximate neighbor search techniques, their cost/benefit trade-offs, and mitigation strategies (e.g., dynamic k selection, confidence weighting) is advised. As with any unsupervised clustering, responsible use is important: when applied to sensitive demographic or medical data, practitioners should audit clusters for fairness and privacy implications. The manuscript currently underplays these aspects and would benefit from explicit guidance on ethical deployment.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. 

Overall, LKM offers a promising and practical acceleration of graph-based k-means with a neat unified perspective, but the paper requires additional empirical rigor (runtime reporting, statistical analyses), deeper hyperparameter and graph sensitivity studies, clarity improvements, and discussion of practical considerations before it can be confidently recommended to the community.

reviews.summary: This submission introduces a computationally efficient clustering algorithm called LKM, which focuses on the scenarios where data is presented in the form of graph. In brief, only the k-nearest neighbour distances of each point are considered in the objective and a simple truncation of large distances is introduced to mitigate the effect of outliers.  The method is shown to produce promising practical performance in comparison with existing techniques both computationally and in terms of clustering performance.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

LKM is developed based on the k-means problem which is a fundamental problem in machine learning and the authors might bring some new insights and develop a new algorithm. The main strength of LKM lies in the practical aspects of the proposed algorithm, in that it is efficient and the performance is compelling. 
Specifically, the computational overhead is O(nk), where n and k denote the number of samples and nearest neighbors, respectively.

In addition, the unified framework of KSUMSC, and LCM also seems interesting. It revolves that SC, a popular clustering algorithm derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as that of LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9: "Specifically....are denote the...". The sentence is grammatically incorrect, "are" should be removed.

2. Some works relating to the connection between k-means and clustering with graph cuts are not discussed in Section 2 (Related work).

3. The performance of SC optimized by the proposed algorithm is not shown.

4. What does \bar{y} stand for in Eq. (15) and (16)?

5. e_i should be a column vector.

6. Some steps in the derivations are not explained. What is the connection between (11) and (17)? 

7. The evaluation needs to be improved. The proposed clustering method uses ball-tree to construct a k-nearest neighbors graph as an intermediate step. Correspondingly, it is suggested to include the clustering performance of ball-tree as a baseline to compare.

8. Have the paper proof-read to remove grammatical errors

9. In page 7, line 178, "#Iter" should be "#Iter.".

10. In page 7, table 2, "FLK" should be "LKM".

11. References should be shown in the same format.

**comment after rebuttal**
I acknowledge that I read and other reviewers' comments and authors' responses. I would like to keep my score as most concerns of mine have been well addressed.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents Local K-Means (LKM), a computationally efficient clustering algorithm that operates on a precomputed k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph. By truncating large distances to mitigate outliers and restricting the k-means update to local neighborhoods, LKM achieves per-iteration complexity O(nk) where n is the number of samples and k the neighborhood size. Moreover, the authors introduce a unified framework parameterized by p that subsumes LKM, KSUMS, and spectral clustering (SC) under a common trace-minimization objective, and they show that all three can be optimized in linear time via a sparse coordinate-descent scheme. Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic and sixteen real-world datasets demonstrate that LKM matches or outperforms standard k-means, spectral clustering, and other graph-based methods in clustering quality (ACC, NMI, ARI) while delivering significant speedups.

Strengths:
1. Novel synthesis and efficiency.  Building on the fundamental k-means problem, the authors offer a fresh perspective by incorporating graph-based locality and deriving an O(nk) solver. This combination of sparsity-exploiting updates and local distance truncation is both conceptually clean and practically effective.
2. Unified clustering framework.  The parameterized objective unites k-means, KSUMS, and ratio-cut spectral clustering, highlighting underlying connections and enabling the same linear-time updates. This unification is a valuable contribution to clustering theory and practice.
3. Empirical performance.  LKM demonstrates competitive or superior clustering accuracy and robustness to outliers across a broad suite of benchmarks, and the runtime comparisons show clear advantages especially as the number of clusters grows.
4. Practical significance.  The low per-iteration complexity makes LKM attractive for large-scale or high-cluster-count applications, and its robustness to noisy or irregular graphs enhances its usability in real-world scenarios.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Related Work Coverage.  Section 2 omits discussion of several relevant studies on the connection between k-means and graph cuts, as well as recent graph-accelerated k-means and coreset methods. Including these would position LKM more clearly in the existing literature.
2. Graph Construction Discussion.  Since LKM depends on a k-NN graph, the cost and quality of graph construction in high dimensions deserves deeper treatment. A brief survey of approximate neighbor search techniques and their impact on clustering quality (e.g., missing or spurious edges) would bolster the practical guidance.
3. Missing SC Performance Results.  Although the unified framework claims linear-time spectral clustering updates, the empirical section does not report SC’s performance under the proposed solver. Adding these results would complete the experimental validation.
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity.  The choice of k, the exponent p, and the dissimilarity truncation parameter λ are central yet lack systematic sensitivity analyses. Small studies on how varying these settings affects clustering metrics would aid reproducibility and offer tuning guidance to practitioners.
5. Theoretical Guarantees.  While the derivations are sound, the paper does not provide convergence proofs or bounds on optimality. Even a brief remark on convergence behavior—empirical iteration counts or conditions under which the coordinate descent converges—would strengthen the theoretical contribution.
6. Clarity of Derivations.  Some derivation steps, notably the transition between Eqs. (11) and (17), are terse. Adding intermediary explanations or a short illustrative example would help readers follow the logic.
7. Notation and Definitions.  (a) In Eqs. (15)–(16), the symbol “ȳ” is used without definition—please clarify its meaning. (b) Ensure eᵢ is treated consistently as a column vector. 
8. Algorithm Presentation.  The pseudocode for the update scheme currently resides only in the supplementary material. Since it’s central to the method, consider including a concise algorithm box in the main text.
9. Empirical Baselines.  As the method uses a ball-tree to build the k-NN graph, including ball-tree clustering as a baseline would contextualize the benefit of the subsequent LKM updates.
10. Figure/Table Formatting.  Some figures lack axis labels, and Table 3’s formatting hampers quick interpretation. Uniformly label all plots and ensure tables are easily readable.
11. Minor Editorial Corrections:  
    – Page 1, line 9: remove the extraneous “are” in “...where n and k are denote...”.  
    – Page 7, line 178: change “#Iter” to “#Iter.”  
    – Table 2: correct “FLK” to “LKM”.  
    – Ensure all references follow a consistent citation format and proofread for grammar.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The requirement of a precomputed k-NN graph may limit LKM in high-dimensional or streaming contexts where neighbor search is costly or approximate. Reproducibility would benefit from released code and detailed graph-construction procedures. Societal risks are low given the unsupervised focus, but the authors should note potential biases when clustering sensitive demographic or medical data and encourage responsible use, including fairness auditing and privacy safeguards.

Decision:
7: Good paper, accept.  
LKM provides a compelling, efficient, and well-validated approach to graph-based clustering. Addressing the above points—particularly on related work, empirical completeness, and clarity—will further solidify its impact and usability.

reviews.summary: The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.
reviews.main_review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!

Summary:
The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Local K-Means (LKM), a variant of k-means clustering that operates on a precomputed k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph. By reformulating the classical k-means objective over a sparse distance graph and introducing a parameter p, the authors unify LKM with KSUMS and spectral clustering under a single trace-minimization framework. They derive an O(nk)-time coordinate-descent algorithm that updates cluster assignments by only considering local neighbors. Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic and sixteen real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that LKM matches or outperforms standard k-means, KSUMS, and spectral clustering in clustering accuracy metrics (ACC, NMI, ARI), while offering significant per-iteration speedups.

Overall Recommendation:
4: Ok but not good enough – rejection.

Strengths:
1. Unified Formulation: As noted by multiple reviewers, recasting k-means, KSUMS, and ratio-cut spectral clustering as instances of a single objective with an exponent parameter p is conceptually appealing and helps elucidate the connections among these methods.
2. Sparsity-Aware Solver: The O(nk)-time update scheme—leveraging local neighbor counts instead of full-distance recomputations—represents a useful synthesis of graph-based clustering and acceleration ideas.
3. Empirical Breadth: The authors evaluate on a large suite of synthetic and real-world benchmarks, showing robustness to outliers and consistent speedups over vanilla Lloyd’s algorithm and several graph-based baselines.

Weaknesses and Areas for Revision:
1. Incomplete Runtime Analysis:
   - The claimed O(nc) or O(n(k + c)) runtime bound neglects the number of iterations to convergence. Without a bound—or empirical characterization—of iteration counts, the complexity claim is misleading. Lloyd’s algorithm similarly has an unbounded iteration count, and any fair comparison must account for this factor. The paper should either include theoretical or empirical bounds on iteration numbers or clearly retract the per-iteration complexity claim as the overall runtime bound.
2. Missing Real-World Runtimes and Dataset Details:
   - Runtime evaluations are limited to toy settings and per-iteration costs. There is no end-to-end timing on real-world datasets, where high dimensionality and ambiguous cluster boundaries could substantially influence both iteration counts and graph construction overhead.
   - The main text omits dataset characteristics (number of points, dimensionality) for the sixteen benchmarks. While supplied in the supplement, many readers will not consult it. Reporting these details upfront would aid reproducibility and interpretation.
3. Dependence on k-NN Graph Construction:
   - LKM requires a precomputed k-NN graph, which can be expensive or inaccurate in high dimensions. The paper briefly mentions this but does not analyze how graph quality or construction cost affects clustering performance. Incorporating recent approximate neighbor search techniques (e.g., hashing, tree-based dual-tree methods) and reporting their impact on runtime/accuracy would strengthen the practical contribution.
4. Lack of Theoretical Convergence Guarantees:
   - No convergence proof or iteration-complexity bound is provided for the coordinate-descent solver. As several reviewers have pointed out, formal guarantees (even weak ones) or at least empirical convergence plots across datasets would bolster confidence in the method.
5. Limited Comparisons to Accelerated k-Means Variants:
   - The empirical section compares LKM primarily to vanilla Lloyd’s algorithm and graph-based methods like KSUMS. However, practitioners often use accelerated k-means variants (e.g., Elkan’s, Hamerly’s, tree-based, or coreset approaches) that achieve orders-of-magnitude speedups. Including these baselines—or at least discussing their relative merits—would provide a more realistic evaluation.
6. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Tuning:
   - Key parameters (neighbor count k, exponent p, dissimilarity cap λ) are introduced without guidance on selection or sensitivity analysis. A section devoted to how performance varies with these settings (perhaps via grid search results or ablation plots) is necessary for practitioners.
7. Clarity and Reproducibility:
   - Algorithm 1 omits details on the random initialization of cluster assignments y; is it uniform per point, or based on a more sophisticated scheme? Given the known importance of initialization for k-means, this must be specified.
   - Pseudocode is only in the supplement. Moving core algorithms into the main text, accompanied by a running example or illustrative diagram, would improve readability.
   - There are numerous minor proofreading issues (tense inconsistencies, equation formatting, figure axis labels) that should be corrected.
8. Societal Impact and Responsible Use:
   - While clustering is generally low-risk, applying unsupervised methods to sensitive demographic or medical data can inadvertently encode biases. A brief discussion on responsible use, fairness considerations when constructing similarity measures, and potential privacy safeguards would be appropriate.

Conclusion:
LKM presents a thoughtful unification of graph-based clustering objectives and a sparsity-exploiting solver that achieves per-iteration O(nk) costs. However, the current manuscript overstates its runtime claims by ignoring iteration counts, lacks end-to-end runtime analyses on realistic datasets, and omits critical discussions on graph construction, convergence guarantees, hyperparameter tuning, and comparisons to widely used accelerated k-means variants. Addressing these points would substantially strengthen the paper. As it stands, I recommend revision before acceptance.

reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.summary: This paper considers a variant of k-means which takes the k-nearest neighbor graph as input. The proposed algorithm, called local k-means (LKM in short), then works by locally optimizing the assignment vector of a point using an objective function that is roughly the incremental cost for the k-means objective. The paper provided a lot of empirical data to show the advantages of LKM.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, this paper proposes a novel k-means algorithm, but I think there has not been enough discussion on its guarantees (from both a theoretical and empircal point of view) to make it convincing. The paper writing can also be greatly improved.

Originality: The mthods used in this paper are new. The paper converts the k-means objective into a different format which is compatible with nearest-neighbor graph inputs, where the main variable is the indicator matrix, and optimizes the columns in this matrix one by one (each column corresponding to the assignment vector of one point). The idea of optimizing the assignment of each point to clusters might not be new to the community, but the proposed local optization function seems to be novel.

Quality: The paper does not provide any theoretical guarantees for the proposed algorithm and mainly used empirical evaluation approaches to show its superiority. The experiments are carefully designed, but I think the authors are trying to make some claims that are too broad and thus the provided empirical results do not seem to be convincing enough to show that this approach really beats state-of-the-art. LKM is compared with a lot of baselines and that may still not cover the entire state-of-the-art clustering approaches. Some comments:
- The self-synthesized data are well designed, and serves to show that LKM performs well on some datasets whose natural clustering cannot be captured by the optimal k-means objective function. However, it seems to me that they might be suitable for other types of clustering such as density-based clustering (DBSCAN might be good for "Mickey"), or common k-means implementation packages might also be good for them (k-means++ might be good for "Outlier" depending on the sampled seeds?).  I wonder if the authors have considered these?
- After reading the experiment section I get a bit more confused about the claims that this paper is trying to make: LKM seems to be beating most baselines on the datasets in clustering quality and be significantly faster than k-means? The improvement of LKM , especially when compared with KSUMS on most of the datasets, do not seem to be very significant. Actually, the results gave me the impression that there are many datasets and each of them might be suitable for different types of clustering methods, with LKM performing relative well for all datasets.

Clarity: The writing clarity is fine, although occasionally there are some flaws such as definition/notation that has not been used again later, typos and so on, but it does not affect reading too much. It has a clear problem definition and algorithm description. That being said, I'm not very impressed with the organization of the paper. 
- The paper spent enough space on problem definition but the paragraphs did not serve to fully motivate this design. For example, previous methods like KSUMS and LKM, whose math formulation, as the authors pointed out, have very similar structure with the one used in this paper, but the authors did not provide intuition about why the differences lead to better performance.
- The description of the algorithm design is only about one page and I think there should be more intuitive explanation of why this particular design of local objective function (18) might lead to a better solution and make it robust against outliers. 

Significance: This paper could potentially propose a solution that advances state-of-the-art, but the comparsion between the new algorithm and previous approaches does not seem to convince people of its superiority. Also, I'm not sure if the improvement is significant enough for this venue.

Minor comments:
- In abstract, line 5, the authors mentioned "KSUMS and SC" without citation and before defining which algorithms these are referring to.
- line 26, "For matrix A, we call it indicator matrix", I don't really see the point of introducing the indicator matrix here, and the notation is not really used again in this paper.
- line 86, "where diag(A) = [a11,..., ann]^T",  A is not used in (7), and I'm not sure about what a11, ..., ann are referring to.
- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Local K-Means (LKM), a variant of k-means that operates on a precomputed k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph. The authors further frame LKM, KSUMS, and spectral clustering within a unified objective parameterized by an exponent p, and derive an efficient coordinate-descent solver that exploits graph sparsity to achieve O(nk) time per iteration. Empirical results on eleven synthetic and sixteen benchmark datasets suggest that LKM attains competitive clustering accuracy (ACC, NMI, ARI), robust performance in the presence of outliers, and significant runtime speedups compared to classical k-means and several graph-based alternatives.

Originality:
The unified formulation that interpolates between k-means, KSUMS, and spectral clustering via a single parameter p represents a useful synthesis, and the specialization to local distances on a sparse graph is a novel twist on accelerating k-means. While optimizing each point’s assignment via a local objective parallels existing coordinate-descent ideas, casting the indicator-matrix view in combination with graph-based costs appears to be a fresh contribution. Related work on fast k-means variants, coreset methods, and approximate k-NN graph construction is cited but could be discussed in more depth to clarify distinctions.

Quality:
The mathematical derivations of the unified model and the O(nk) update scheme are sound and logically presented. However, the paper does not provide convergence guarantees or approximation bounds for the proposed algorithm. Empirical evaluations are extensive and well designed on both synthetic “Mickey”- and “Outlier”-style datasets and standard benchmarks. Yet, key claims—such as LKM’s superiority over state-of-the-art clustering—are not fully substantiated. In particular:
- Baseline coverage could be broader: comparisons to density-based methods (e.g., DBSCAN), popular implementations of k-means++ or mini-batch k-means, and coreset-based accelerations are missing.
- Sensitivity analyses for hyperparameters (k, p, and the dissimilarity cap λ) and graph construction quality (exact vs. approximate k-NN) are absent, leaving practitioners with little guidance on tuning.
- Statistical significance testing for performance differences is not reported.
Addressing these points would bolster the empirical case and clarify the algorithm’s robustness across settings.

Clarity:
Overall the exposition is coherent, with a clear problem statement and algorithm outline. Nonetheless, several presentation issues hinder readability:
- Notation inconsistencies (e.g., unused “indicator matrix” A, undefined diag(A) elements) and occasional typos distract the reader.
- Equations (notably Eqs. 18–22) are densely packed; accompanying illustrative examples or a running toy example would improve intuition.
- Pseudocode currently relegated to the supplementary material should be moved into the main text, ideally accompanied by an algorithm flow diagram.
- Figures and tables require complete axis labels and consistent formatting for quick interpretation.

Significance:
LKM’s O(nk) per-iteration complexity and its robustness to outliers make it an attractive option for large-scale clustering when a k-NN graph is available or can be efficiently approximated. The unified framework may inspire further work on graph-based clustering algorithms. However, the dependence on a precomputed neighbor graph—especially in high-dimensional spaces where graph construction is costly or inaccurate—limits immediate applicability. Without theoretical guarantees or systematic guidance on hyperparameter selection, the impact on practitioners remains constrained.

Minor Comments:
- Abstract (line 5) references “KSUMS and SC” without prior definition or citation.
- Equation (9): the rationale for setting d_ij^(k) = γ in the dissimilarity conversion should be justified.
- Line 26: the introduction of the indicator matrix A seems superfluous if never reused.
- Provide citations for recent fast graph-construction methods and approximate neighbor search.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors acknowledge the cost of k-NN graph construction and note minimal direct societal risks for unsupervised clustering. To strengthen this section, the manuscript should discuss how approximate graph errors affect clustering quality and outline best practices for responsible use—particularly when clustering sensitive demographic or behavioral data to mitigate privacy or fairness concerns.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper considers a variant of k-means which takes the k-nearest neighbor graph as input. The proposed algorithm, called local k-means (LKM in short), then works by locally optimizing the assignment vector of a point using an objective function that is roughly the incremental cost for the k-means objective. The paper provided a lot of empirical data to show the advantages of LKM.

Main Review:
Overall, this paper proposes a novel k-means algorithm, but I think there has not been enough discussion on its guarantees (from both a theoretical and empircal point of view) to make it convincing. The paper writing can also be greatly improved.

Originality: The mthods used in this paper are new. The paper converts the k-means objective into a different format which is compatible with nearest-neighbor graph inputs, where the main variable is the indicator matrix, and optimizes the columns in this matrix one by one (each column corresponding to the assignment vector of one point). The idea of optimizing the assignment of each point to clusters might not be new to the community, but the proposed local optization function seems to be novel.

Quality: The paper does not provide any theoretical guarantees for the proposed algorithm and mainly used empirical evaluation approaches to show its superiority. The experiments are carefully designed, but I think the authors are trying to make some claims that are too broad and thus the provided empirical results do not seem to be convincing enough to show that this approach really beats state-of-the-art. LKM is compared with a lot of baselines and that may still not cover the entire state-of-the-art clustering approaches. Some comments:
- The self-synthesized data are well designed, and serves to show that LKM performs well on some datasets whose natural clustering cannot be captured by the optimal k-means objective function. However, it seems to me that they might be suitable for other types of clustering such as density-based clustering (DBSCAN might be good for "Mickey"), or common k-means implementation packages might also be good for them (k-means++ might be good for "Outlier" depending on the sampled seeds?).  I wonder if the authors have considered these?
- After reading the experiment section I get a bit more confused about the claims that this paper is trying to make: LKM seems to be beating most baselines on the datasets in clustering quality and be significantly faster than k-means? The improvement of LKM , especially when compared with KSUMS on most of the datasets, do not seem to be very significant. Actually, the results gave me the impression that there are many datasets and each of them might be suitable for different types of clustering methods, with LKM performing relative well for all datasets.

Clarity: The writing clarity is fine, although occasionally there are some flaws such as definition/notation that has not been used again later, typos and so on, but it does not affect reading too much. It has a clear problem definition and algorithm description. That being said, I'm not very impressed with the organization of the paper. 
- The paper spent enough space on problem definition but the paragraphs did not serve to fully motivate this design. For example, previous methods like KSUMS and LKM, whose math formulation, as the authors pointed out, have very similar structure with the one used in this paper, but the authors did not provide intuition about why the differences lead to better performance.
- The description of the algorithm design is only about one page and I think there should be more intuitive explanation of why this particular design of local objective function (18) might lead to a better solution and make it robust against outliers. 

Significance: This paper could potentially propose a solution that advances state-of-the-art, but the comparsion between the new algorithm and previous approaches does not seem to convince people of its superiority. Also, I'm not sure if the improvement is significant enough for this venue.

Minor comments:
- In abstract, line 5, the authors mentioned "KSUMS and SC" without citation and before defining which algorithms these are referring to.
- line 26, "For matrix A, we call it indicator matrix", I don't really see the point of introducing the indicator matrix here, and the notation is not really used again in this paper.
- line 86, "where diag(A) = [a11,..., ann]^T",  A is not used in (7), and I'm not sure about what a11, ..., ann are referring to.
- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Proximal and Federated Random Reshuffling

reviews.summary: This paper studies a proximal extension of random reshuffling algorithm, namely ProxRR for finite-sum composite optimization. ProxRR requires only one proximal steps per round, which reduces the complexities incurred by costly proximal operators. This paper also studies a federated version of ProxRR, namely FedRR, and proved theoretical advantages over Local SGD.
reviews.main_review:
I would like to express my appreciation for your submission to NeurIPS 2021. The paper is overall well-written and I enjoyed reading it. My concerns are as follows.

1. The ProxRR presented in this paper applies only one proximal step per epoch. This reduces the number of calls to proximal operators, but at the same time incurs additional complexities (say, in eq (6)). It is hard to calibrate the significance of ProxRR bound (6) with [1] or Proximal SGD (7) since there are two factors mixed in --- reshuffling and reduced proximal calls. Have you considered doing ablation study to separate these two factors? For example, how does “ProxRR with proximal at every step” work? Will it eliminate the additional term in (6)?

2. This work compares  FedRR with other smooth federated optimization algorithm such as FedAvg and SCAFFOLD. How does FedRR compare with other federated composite algorithms such as [2] (theoretically or empirically)? For example, for experiments presented in Fig. 2, how does FedRR compare with FedDualAvg [2] if $\lambda_1 \neq 0$?

[1] Random Reshufﬂing: Simple Analysis with Vast Improvements, NeurIPS 2020.
[2] Federated Composite Optimization, ICML 2021.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: See main review for limitations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies a proximal extension of random reshuffling algorithm, namely ProxRR for finite-sum composite optimization. ProxRR requires only one proximal steps per round, which reduces the complexities incurred by costly proximal operators. This paper also studies a federated version of ProxRR, namely FedRR, and proved theoretical advantages over Local SGD.

Main Review:
I would like to express my appreciation for your submission to NeurIPS 2021. The paper is overall well-written and I enjoyed reading it. My concerns are as follows.

1. The ProxRR presented in this paper applies only one proximal step per epoch. This reduces the number of calls to proximal operators, but at the same time incurs additional complexities (say, in eq (6)). It is hard to calibrate the significance of ProxRR bound (6) with [1] or Proximal SGD (7) since there are two factors mixed in --- reshuffling and reduced proximal calls. Have you considered doing ablation study to separate these two factors? For example, how does “ProxRR with proximal at every step” work? Will it eliminate the additional term in (6)?

2. This work compares  FedRR with other smooth federated optimization algorithm such as FedAvg and SCAFFOLD. How does FedRR compare with other federated composite algorithms such as [2] (theoretically or empirically)? For example, for experiments presented in Fig. 2, how does FedRR compare with FedDualAvg [2] if $\lambda_1 \neq 0$?

[1] Random Reshufﬂing: Simple Analysis with Vast Improvements, NeurIPS 2020.
[2] Federated Composite Optimization, ICML 2021.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
See main review for limitations.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper studies Proximal Random Reshuffling (ProxRR), which applies exactly one proximal update per epoch to finite-sum composite objectives, and its federated variant FedRR, obtained via a consensus-constraint reformulation. ProxRR reduces expensive proximal calls by up to a factor of n compared to classical proximal SGD, while FedRR provably lowers communication rounds relative to Local SGD and distributed GD. The authors establish convergence guarantees under convex, strongly convex, and nonconvex settings, cover both constant and decreasing step sizes, introduce an importance-sampling extension, and validate their findings empirically on elastic-net logistic regression and federated benchmarks.

Strengths:  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Rigor:  
   - ProxRR’s design—deferring the proximal step to once per epoch—is a creative extension of random reshuffling theory to composite optimization.  
   - FedRR’s formulation via product-space consensus constraints elegantly adapts ProxRR to federated learning, yielding clear communication benefits.  
   - The convergence analysis is thorough and unified: linear rates in strongly convex regimes, sublinear rates in nonconvex settings, and explicit bounds on the “shuffling radius” that subsume prior variance measures. Importance-sampling via summand replication is also an innovative touch.  

2. Clarity and Organization:  
   - The manuscript is generally well written, with clear problem statements, algorithm descriptions, and theorem statements.  
   - Notation is consistent, and proofs are carefully deferred to the appendix.  

3. Significance:  
   - By drastically reducing proximal-operator complexity, ProxRR addresses practical bottlenecks in regularized learning tasks.  
   - FedRR advances federated optimization by improving communication efficiency in finite-sum settings, bridging theory and practice.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Disentangling Reshuffling vs Reduced Proximal Calls:  
   - The bound in equation (6) mixes two effects—reshuffling and fewer proximal steps—making it difficult to assess each contribution. As noted in prior work on proximal RR, an ablation study where ProxRR performs a proximal update at every iteration (rather than once per epoch) would clarify whether the additional term in (6) vanishes when reshuffling alone is isolated.  

2. Comparison to Related Proximal and Federated Methods:  
   - While comparisons to FedAvg and SCAFFOLD are provided, the paper omits federated composite baselines such as FedDualAvg (ICML 2021) and variance-reduced proximal methods (e.g., ProxSARAH). Including theoretical complexity comparisons or empirical results for FedDualAvg when λ₁≠0—and for accelerated proximal schemes—would strengthen contextualization.  

3. Empirical Evaluation Scope:  
   - Experiments are limited to convex logistic regression tasks on moderate-scale datasets. Evaluating on nonconvex models (e.g., neural networks) or problems with more complex regularizers would demonstrate robustness and practical scalability.  
   - Reporting wall-clock runtimes and breaking down the cost per proximal vs gradient evaluation would offer deeper insight into real-world benefits.  

4. Practical and System Considerations:  
   - The importance-sampling extension doubles the effective sum size, which may be impractical for large n. Empirical ablation on its overhead and benefits would be valuable.  
   - In federated settings, assumptions of full client participation, synchronized updates, and honest participants may not hold. Discussing straggler effects, partial participation, and possible integration with secure aggregation or differential privacy would address practical deployment concerns.  
   - A schematic diagram of FedRR’s communication pattern and a table summarizing recommended parameter choices (step sizes, epoch lengths) would improve readability and adoption.  

5. Minor Comments:  
   - Consider adding a notation table for quick reference.  
   - Clarify how the shuffling radius constant (σ_rad) behaves in typical non-IID data splits.  
   - Briefly discuss potential privacy and fairness implications arising from repeated reshuffling across clients.  

Decision: 6 — Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
Overall, this submission offers a compelling theoretical and algorithmic contribution by extending random reshuffling to composite and federated learning. Addressing the above points—particularly disentangling reshuffling vs. proximal savings, broader comparisons, and expanded experiments—would solidify its impact.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.
reviews.main_review: As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.
